questions.questionText:

1) there is more than one load carrying component.
2) one load carrying component is sufficient for a given load, provided it is strong enough.

4) the design is based on the principle of redundancy of components.
The combination that regroups all of the correct statements is:

options: slight reduction in functional capabilities or safety margins.
significant reduction in functional capabilities or safety margins.
large reduction in functional capabilities or safety margins.

questions.questionText: According EASA CS the worst effect of a HAZARDOUS FAILURE on the aeroplane could be:
options: slight reduction in functional capabilities or safety margins.
large reduction in functional capabilities or safety margins.

questions.questionText:

1) In structural design, FAIL SAFE implies parallel structural parts.

replacement of parts after a given number of cycles or hours of use.

options: serious or fatal injury to a small number of passengers or cabin crew.

questions.questionText: According EASA CS the worst effect of a MINOR FAILURE on the occupants of an aeroplane excluding flight crew could be:
options: serious or fatal injury to a small number of passengers or cabin crew.

options: physical distress or excessive workload, impairs ability to perform tasks.

the material suffers progressively more permanent damage each time that it is loaded and unloaded.

replacement of parts after a given number of cycles or hours of use.
monitoring of critical parameters and the replacement of parts if a limit value is exceeded.

questions.questionText: What is 'hard time' maintenance of an aircraft component?
options: A component that is monitored for critical parameters and replaced when a limit value is exceeded.
A component that is overhauled or replaced after a set number of hours, cycles or operations.
A component that is replaced when it approaches a certain percentage of predicted life.
A component that is overhauled or replaced at some point when convenient to the operator.

questions.questionText:

1) Composite structures will give you the same tensile strength in all directions.

options: aluminium alloy with a covering layer of pure aluminium.
two thin metal sheets and a light core material.

1) In a structural component with given dimensions constructed of composite materials, the strength is the same in all directions.

1) For a structural component with given dimensions composite materials enable a structural component's strength to be tailored to the direction of the load.

options: compression in both the upper and the lower surfaces.
compression in the upper surface, tension in the lower surface.
tension in both the upper and the lower surfaces.
tension in the upper surface, compression in the lower surface.

questions.questionText: One function of a rib is:
options: to stabilise the fuselage skin against buckling.
to carry tensile load of the fuselage pressure hull.
to maintain the aerodynamic shape of the wing.

questions.questionText: When a wing bends downwards, aileron flutter might occur if the aileron deflects:
options: downwards, because the location of the aileron centre of gravity lies behind the hinge line.
upwards, because the location of the aileron centre of gravity lies in front of the hinge line.
downwards, because the location of the aileron centre of gravity lies in front of the hinge line.
upwards, because the location of the aileron centre of gravity lies behind the hinge line.

questions.questionText: When a wing bends upwards, aileron flutter might occur if the aileron deflects:
options: downwards, because the location of the aileron centre of gravity lies in front of the hinge line.
upwards, because the location of the aileron centre of gravity lies in front of the hinge line.
upwards, because the location of the aileron centre of gravity lies behind the hinge line.
downwards, because the location of the aileron centre of gravity lies behind the hinge line.

questions.questionText: The function of ribs in a wing structure is to:

questions.questionText: One design method to avoid control surface flutter is:
options: through the correct use of balance tabs.
ensuring correct mass distribution within the control surface.

through the correct use of trim tabs.

questions.questionText: A cantilever wing is:
options: a wing with a rectangular planform.

questions.questionText: The type of hydraulic fluid most frequently used in the hydraulic systems of modern transport aeroplanes is:

Low flash point, high volatility, high compressibility, low viscosity and low flammability.
High flash point, high volatility, low compressibility, high viscosity and low flammability.

a red light will illuminate in the cockpit to warn the pilot of the failure.

the highest to minimize power consumption and resistance to flow.
the lowest to minimize power consumption and resistance to flow.
the highest to provide excellent lubrication properties.

questions.questionText: One of the functions of an accumulator in a hydraulic system is:
options: to act as a pressure relief valve.
to damp pressure surges in the system.

questions.questionText: The color of a fresh synthetic hydraulic fluid is:

1) thermal stability.

4) high compressibility.
5) high volatility.

options: to function as a buffer to assist the hydraulic system during heavy braking.
to damp pressure fluctuations of the autobrake system.
to supply a limited amount of brake energy in the event of loss of all hydraulic systems supplying the brakes.
to store the hydraulic energy recovered by the anti-skid system to prevent wheel locking.

questions.questionText: According EASA CS the worst effect of a MAJOR FAILURE on the aeroplane could be:
options: large reduction in functional capabilities or safety margins.
slight reduction in functional capabilities or safety margins.
no effect on operational capabilities or safety.
significant reduction in functional capabilities or safety margins.

questions.questionText: According EASA CS the worst effect of a MAJOR FAILURE on the flight crew could be:
options: physical discomfort or a significant increase in workload.
physical distress or excessive workload, impairs ability to perform tasks.

questions.questionText: With regard to an aircraft fuselage, a FAIL SAFE structure is one:
options: in which the adjacent structural members can carry the load for an unlimited time.
in which the adjacent structural members can carry the load for a limited time.

questions.questionText: The principle of DAMAGE TOLERANT structural design of an aircraft is based on the:
options: capability to withstand a certain amount of weakening of the structure without catastrophic failure.
monitoring of critical parameters and the replacement of parts if a limit value is exceeded.
fact that there is no need to inspect the structure.
replacement of parts after a given number of cycles or hours of use.

questions.questionText: According to EASA CS the allowable quantitative average failure probability per flight hour for a CATASTROPHIC FAILURE should be on the order of:
The principle of the SAFE LIFE design of an aircraft is based on the:
options: capability to withstand a certain amount of weakening of the structure without catastrophic failure.
monitoring of critical parameters and the replacement of parts if a limit value is exceeded.
redundancy of the structure or equipment.
replacement of parts after a given number of cycles or hours of use.

questions.questionText:
Which of these statements about structural design principles are correct or incorrect?
1) In structural design, FAIL SAFE implies the structure will never fail.
2) In structural design, SAFE LIFE implies the structure will never fail during a declared time period or number of cycles.

Which of these statements about structural design principles are correct or incorrect?
1) In structural design, FAIL SAFE implies the structure will never fail.
2) A SAFE LIFE structure is based on a declared time period or number of cycles.
According EASA CS the worst effect of a HAZARDOUS FAILURE on the occupants of an aeroplane excluding flight crew could be:
options: serious or fatal injury to a small number of passengers or cabin crew.

questions.questionText:
For FAIL SAFE designed structural components:
1) there is more than one load carrying component.
2) one load carrying component is sufficient, provided it is strong enough.
3) the component is removed at the end of the calculated life time or number of cycles.
4) the design is based on the principle of redundancy of components.
The combination that regroups all of the correct statements is:
A SAFE LIFE aircraft structural component:
options: may be used during a declared number of cycles or flight hours.
should have enough strength during the whole lifetime of an aircraft.
is so strong that it never will fail during a declared time period.

questions.questionText:
Which of these statements about structural design principles are correct or incorrect?
1) The DAMAGE TOLERANCE principle assumes cracks in the structure will never occur.
2) The SAFE LIFE principle is based on the replacement of parts after a given number of cycles or flight hours.
An under-inflated tire will:
options: experience reduced wear at the shoulders.
be more subject to viscous aquaplaning.
experience increased wear at the shoulders.

questions.questionText: The reason for fitting thermal plugs to aeroplane wheels is that they:
options: prevent heat transfer from the brake disks to the tires.
prevent tire burst after excessive brake application.
release air from the tire in the event of overpressure due to over-inflation.

questions.questionText: The function of a fusible plug is to:
options: protect the brake against brake disk fusion due to excessive temperature.
function as a special circuit breaker in the electric system.
protect against excessive pressure in the pneumatic system.
protect the tire against explosion due to excessive temperature.

questions.questionText: Tire "creep" may be described as the:
options: decrease in inflation pressure due to increase in ambient temperature.
increase in inflation pressure due to decrease in ambient temperature.
gradual circumferential increase of tire wear.
circumferential movement of the tire in relation to the wheel flange.

questions.questionText:
Compared to a tire fitted with an inner tube, a tubeless tire presents the following characteristics:
1) high heating.
2) valve fragility.
3) lower risk of bursting.
4) better adjustment to wheels.
The combination containing all the correct statements is:
Thermal plugs are installed in:
To avoid the risk of tire burst from overheating there is:
options: a pressure relief valve situated in the filler valve.
the "Emergency Burst" function of the anti-skid system that adapts braking to the tire temperature.
a thermal plug that deflates the tire at a specific temperature.
water injection triggered at a fixed temperature in order to lower tire temperature.

questions.questionText: Thermal plugs are installed in:
A flight control surface actuator is said to be "reversible" when:
options: there is feedback to the pilot's controls of the aerodynamic forces acting on the control surface.
the pilot does not feel any force when moving that flight control surface in flight.
there is a need to have an artificial feel system.
the flight control system has an alternate means of control in case of a control jam.

questions.questionText:
Which of these statements about a gust lock system are correct or incorrect?
1) Irreversible flight controls should have a gust lock.
2) There is no need for a gust lock on manual flight controls.
Most large conventional aeroplanes are not provided with aileron and rudder trim tabs. Is it still possible to trim these control surfaces?
options: Yes, but trimming is only possible when before the flight, the respective auxiliary surfaces are correctly adjusted for cruising conditions by the maintenance department.
Yes, trimming is possible by adjusting the neutral point of the artificial feel mechanism by means of a trim switch.
No, because without trim tabs trimming is not possible.
Yes, but trimming is only possible when the autopilot is engaged.

questions.questionText: Which of the following statements is UNTRUE? The disadvantages of manual flight control systems include the following statements:
options: Manual flying control systems rarely have redundancy built into them.
Aerodynamic forces mean that the control loads increase with airspeed.
Jammed controls may cause an accident.
Manual flying control systems are complex.

questions.questionText: An aeroplane equipped with irreversible flight controls:
options: must be equipped with control locks.
may be equipped with simple spring type feel units on all flight controls.
does not require an artificial feel system.

questions.questionText: Which of these statements regarding most gust lock systems is correct?
options: A gust lock can be used in flight to reduce the effect of turbulence.
On reversible flight controls, there is no need for a gust lock.
A gust lock is only fitted on the elevator and the rudder.
When the gust lock is ON there is protection to prevent take-off.

questions.questionText: What is the function of hydraulic valves in fully powered control systems?
options: They only provide the pilot with an "artificial feel" feedback.
They directly cause a movement of the control surface.
They act on the actuator of the control surface.
They decrease the control force generated by the cables or pushrods.

questions.questionText: An aeroplane equipped with fully powered flight controls (irreversible type):
options: does not require the use of an artificial feel system.
must be equipped with control locks.
remains normally controllable in case of total loss of power to the flight control system.
requires the use of an artificial feel system.

questions.questionText:
Which of these statements about rudder limiting are correct or incorrect?
1) A rudder ratio changer system reduces the rudder deflection for a given rudder pedal deflection as the IAS increases.
2) A variable stop system limits both rudder and rudder pedal deflection as the IAS increases.
PRIMARY flight controls are:
options: control wheel or stick, rudder pedals, flap lever and throttle.
control wheel or stick, rudder pedals and speed brake.

questions.questionText: Trimming of aileron and rudder in an irreversible flight control system:
options: is achieved by adjusting the neutral point of the flight control actuator.
is achieved by adjusting the "zero force point" of the feel system.

questions.questionText:
Which of these statements about a gust lock system are correct or incorrect?
1) There is no need for a gust lock on irreversible flight controls.
2) Manual flight controls should have a gust lock.

The expression "primary flight control" applies to the:
1) elevator.
2) speed brake.
3) lift-augmentation devices.
4) roll spoilers.
The combination that regroups all of the correct statements is:

Which of these statements about rudder limiting are correct or incorrect?
1) A rudder ratio changer system limits both rudder and rudder pedal deflection as the IAS increases.
2) A variable stop system reduces the rudder deflection for a given rudder pedal deflection as the IAS increases.

Which of these statements about trimming in a irreversible flight control system of a conventional aeroplane are correct or incorrect?
1) The zero force position of the control column changes when using the pitch trim.
2) The zero force position of the control wheel changes when using the aileron trim.

Which of these statements about rudder limiting are correct or incorrect?
1) A rudder ratio changer system reduces the rudder deflection for a given rudder pedal deflection as the IAS decreases.
2) A variable stop system limits both rudder and rudder pedal deflection as the IAS increases.
A flight control surface actuator is said to be "irreversible" when:
options: there is a need to lock the flight controls on the ground.
there is no feedback to the pilot's controls of the aerodynamic forces acting on the control surface.
the flight control system has an alternate means of control in case of a control jam.
the pilot does not feel any force when moving that control surface in flight.

questions.questionText: An artificial feel unit is necessary in the pitch channel of a flight control system when:
options: the elevators are actuated by irreversible servo-control units.
the elevators are actuated by reversible servo-control units.
the elevators are fitted with servo-tabs or trim tabs.

questions.questionText:
Which of these statements about a gust lock system are correct or incorrect?
1) A gust lock can be used in flight to reduce the effects of turbulence.
2) There is no need for a gust lock on reversible flight controls.
Given a conventional transport aeroplane with irreversible flight controls on the ground with engines running. Which of these statements about rudder trim actuation is correct?
options: The "zero force point" of the artificial feel system changes, the rudder does not move.
The rudder moves, the rudder pedals move in the corresponding direction.
The rudder trim tab moves and the rudder pedals do not move.
The rudder moves, the rudder pedals do not move.

questions.questionText: The function of the rudder limiter system is:
options: to reduce pilot's workload during engine failure.
to restrict the rudder deflection during flight at high altitudes.
to restrict rudder deflection during flight at high IAS.
to limit pedal movement in heavy turbulence.

questions.questionText: Rudder trim adjustment in an aeroplane with irreversible flight controls is:
options: an adjustment of the zero force rudder position.
unnecessary because this aeroplane does not need rudder trim.
an adjustment of the rudder trim tab.
an adjustment of the rudder ratio changer.

questions.questionText: Most transport aeroplanes are provided with protection against control jamming. This means that:
options: seized brakes can be released from the cockpit.
the flight control system has provisions to disconnect the part of the control system that becomes blocked.
the aeroplane is protected against the adverse effects of strong electromagnetic radiation.
in case of seizure, engine control is taken over automatically by an alternate electric circuit.

questions.questionText: An aeroplane equipped with reversible flight controls:
options: is equipped with simple spring type feel units.
need not be equipped with a separate gust lock system.
does not require an artificial feel system.

questions.questionText:
Which of these statements about a gust lock system are correct or incorrect?
1) There should be suitable design precautions to prevent flight with the gust lock engaged.
2) Reversible flight controls should have a gust lock.

Which of these statements about trimming in a irreversible flight control system of a conventional aeroplane are correct or incorrect?
1) The zero force position of the control column does not change when using the pitch trim.
2) The zero force position of the control wheel does not change when using the aileron trim.
Given an aeroplane with irreversible primary flight controls, how is control maintained if one hydraulic system is lost due to a hydraulic leak?
options: Sufficient reserve hydraulic fluid is available to compensate the effects of the leak.
The remaining systems will take over control.
By switching to manual back-up mode.
By switching the flight control system to the reversible mode.

questions.questionText:
The expression "primary flight control" applies to the:
1) stabilizer.
2) rudder.
3) speed brake.
4) aileron.
The combination that regroups all of the correct statements is:
An aeroplane equipped with irreversible flight controls:
options: does not require an artificial feel system.
need not be equipped with a separate gust lock system.
must have a back-up control system.
may be equipped with simple spring type feel units on all flight controls.

questions.questionText: An artificial feel system:
options: functions in series with an irreversible hydraulic actuator.
is mounted in parallel with a spring tab.
functions in parallel with an irreversible hydraulic actuator.
is necessary in a reversible flight control actuator unit.

questions.questionText:
The expression "secondary flight control" applies to the:
1) elevator.
2) speed brake.
3) lift-augmentation devices.
4) roll spoilers.
The combination that regroups all of the correct statements is:
On a large transport aeroplane, the auto-slat system:
options: ensures that the (part of) slats are always extended when the ground/flight system is in the "ground" position.
extends (part of) the slats automatically when a certain value of angle of attack is exceeded.
provides for automatic slat retraction after take-off.

questions.questionText:
The expression "secondary flight control" applies to the:
1) trimmable stabilizer.
2) rudder.
3) speed brake.
4) aileron.
The combination that regroups all of the correct statements is:
A Krueger flap is normally located at the:
options: trailing edge close to the wing root.
trailing edge close to the wing tip.

questions.questionText: The reason for a double switch on the elevator trim is:
options: because there are two trim motors.
to reduce the probability of a trim runaway.
to be able to use two different trim speeds, slow trim rate at high speed and high trim rate at low speed.
to prevent both pilots from performing opposite trim inputs.

questions.questionText: The automatic ground spoiler extension system is normally activated during landing by:
Deploying a Fowler flap, the flap will:

The advantages of fly-by-wire control are:
1) reduction of the electric and hydraulic power required to operate the control surfaces.
2) lesser sensitivity to lightning strike.
3) direct and indirect weight saving through simplification of systems.
4) immunity to different interfering signals.
5) improvement of piloting quality throughout the flight envelope.
The combination regrouping all the correct statements is:
Which modes of operation can be found in fly-by-wire aircraft?
options: Take-off/Go-around, Flight, Landing, and sometimes Taxi

questions.questionText: Modern fly-by-wire systems feature different modes of operation. Which one is the most basic?
Modern fly-by-wire systems usually feature different modes of operation. Which are the most common ones?
options: Normal mode, Alternative (secondary) mode, Direct mode and Mechanical Backup
Normal mode, Alternative (secondary) mode and Direct mode
Alternative (secondary) mode, Direct mode and Mechanical Backup
Normal mode, Direct mode and Mechanical Backup

questions.questionText: Consider a piston engined aircraft. Which source is pneumatic air taken from?
Bleed air extracted from turbine engine can be used for:
options: supplying the engine with high pressure airflow.

questions.questionText: Engine bleed air used for air conditioning and pressurization in turbo-jet aeroplanes is usually taken from the:
In flight, the cabin air for large jet transport aeroplanes is usually supplied by:
Cabin air for modern transport airplanes is usually taken from:
options: HP compressor and from the LP compressor if necessary.

questions.questionText: The purpose of the "pressure relief valve" in a high pressure oxygen system is to:
options: relieve overpressure if the pressure reducing valve malfunctions.
reduce pressure in the oxygen reservoir to a suitable manifold pressure for the regulator.
maximize the charging pressure of the system.
act as a manual shut-off valve.

questions.questionText: During level flight at a constant cabin pressure altitude (which could be decreased, even at this flight level), the cabin outflow valves are:
options: fully closed until the cabin descends to a selected altitude.
fully closed until the cabin climbs to a selected altitude.
at the pre-set position for take-off.

questions.questionText: The term "bootstrap", when used to identify a cabin air conditioning and pressurization system, refers to the:
options: cold air unit (air cycle machine) arrangement.
cooling air across the inter-cooler heat exchanger.
means by which pressurization is controlled.

questions.questionText: Refer to figure 021-28. In a pressurized aircraft whose cabin altitude is 6000 ft, a crack in a cabin window makes it necessary to reduce the differential pressure to 7 psi. The flight level to be maintained in order to keep the same cabin altitude is:
Cabin heating in a large turbojet transport aeroplane is obtained from:
options: hot air extracted from the engine turbines.
hot air extracted from the engine compressors.

questions.questionText: On most large transport aeroplanes, the cabin pressure is controlled by regulating the:
Assuming cabin differential pressure has attained the required value in normal flight conditions, if flight altitude is maintained:
options: there will be a constant air mass flow through the cabin.
the pressurisation system must be controlled manually.
the pressurization system ceases to function until leakage reduces the pressure.
the outflow valves will move to the fully open position.

questions.questionText: In a typical air conditioning system of a short-haul turbojet aircraft the cabin air is not controlled in respect to the:
"Conditioned" air is air that has:
options: been controlled in respect of temperature and pressure.
had any moisture removed from it.

questions.questionText: The maximum cabin differential pressure of an aeroplane with a maximum certified altitude of 41 000 ft is approximately.
Whilst in level cruising flight, an aeroplane with a pressurized cabin experiences a malfunction of the pressure controller. If the cabin vertical speed indicator reads 200 ft/min rate of descent:
options: the crew has to intermittently cut off the incoming air flow in order to maintain a zero cabin altitude.
a descent must be initiated to prevent the oxygen masks dropping when the cabin altitude reaches 14000 ft.
the aircraft has to climb to a higher flight level in order to reduce the cabin altitude to its initial value.
the differential pressure will rise to its maximum value, thus causing the safety relief valves to open.

questions.questionText: In a turbo compressor air conditioning system (bootstrap system), the purpose of the heat exchangers is to:
options: cool the bleed air in front and behind the compressor of the air cycle machine.
allow a steady compressor outlet temperature.
cool bleed air before entering the complete pneumatic system.
allow a homogeneous temperature by mixing air flows from various air conditioning groups in operation.

questions.questionText: Refer to figure 021-20 Automatic temperature control of the system as shown, would be accomplished by.
options: the cabin sensors only, modulating the mix valve.
the temperature selector only, modulating the mix valve.
the temperature selector in conjunction with cabin sensors and the temperature regulator, modulating the mix valve.
automatic control of the ram air.

questions.questionText: The term "cabin altitude" refers to the:
options: flight level at which the aeroplane is flying.
difference in height between the cabin floor and ceiling.

questions.questionText: Assuming cabin pressure decreases, the cabin rate of climb indicator should indicate:
options: a rate of descent dependent upon the cabin differential pressure.
a rate of descent of approximately 300 feet per minute.

questions.questionText: In a typical "bootstrap" cooling system the supply air is:
options: passed across an expansion turbine, then compressed and passed through a heat exchanger.
passed across an expansion turbine, then through a secondary heat exchanger and then across a compressor.
compressed, passed through a secondary heat exchanger, and then across an expansion turbine.
passed across an expansion turbine, then directly to the heat exchanger.

questions.questionText: A turbo compressor air conditioning system (bootstrap system) includes two heat exchangers - the primary exchanger (P) and the secondary exchanger (S). The functions of these heat exchangers are as follows:
options: P. precools the engine bleed air; S: cools air behind the pack's compressor.
P. pre-cools the engine bleed air; S: increases the temperature of the air used for air-conditioning of cargo compartment (animals).
P. warms up engine bleed air; S: recirculates the cabin air, reducing its temperature.
P. warms up engine bleed air; S: increases the temperature of air originating from the compressor of the pack.

questions.questionText: Under normal conditions (EASA CS-25) the cabin pressure altitude of a transport aeroplane is not allowed to exceed:
Temperature regulation of an air conditioning system:
options: is manually controlled by the pilot reading the cabin temperature gauge and increasing or decreasing hot air as required.
is automatically controlled by sensing the output temperature from the control valve in relation to the temperature set on the pilot's control panel.
is automatically controlled by sensing cabin temperature in relation to engine compressor outlet temperatures.

questions.questionText: The pack cooling fan provides:
options: cooling air to the primary and secondary heat exchanger during slow flight and ground operation.
cooling air to the primary and secondary heat exchanger during cruise.
air to the eyeball outlets at the Passenger Service Unit (PSU).

questions.questionText: Assuming that during cruise flight with air-conditioning packs ON, all the outflow valves fully close:
options: the air supply would automatically be stopped.
the cabin pressure would become equal to the ambient outside air pressure.
the skin of the cabin would be overstressed.
the pressure differential would go to the maximum value.

questions.questionText: In a cabin air conditioning system with an air cycle machine (bootstrap system), the mass air flow is routed via the
options: secondary heat exchanger outlet to the turbine inlet.
secondary heat exchanger outlet to the compressor inlet.
compressor outlet to the primary heat exchanger inlet.
turbine outlet to the primary heat exchanger inlet.

questions.questionText: During a normal pressurized climb after take-off:
options: the cabin differential pressure is maintained constant.
cabin pressure decreases more slowly than atmospheric pressure.
the pressurization system is inoperative until an altitude of 10 000 ft is reached.
absolute cabin pressure increases to compensate for the fall in pressure outside the aeroplane.

questions.questionText: A cabin pressure controller maintains a pre-set cabin altitude by regulating the:
options: mass air flow into the cabin.
position of the inward relief valves.
position of the duct relief valves.

questions.questionText: A turbo compressor air conditioning system (bootstrap system) will:
options: increase charge air pressure whilst causing the temperature to drop in the heat exchanger.
cause a pressure drop as well as an associated temperature drop in the charge air.
not affect the charge air pressure.
decrease charge air pressure whilst causing the temperature to rise in the heat exchanger.

questions.questionText: The purpose of the cabin pressure controller operating in automatic mode is to: 1) control the cabin altitude. 2) control the cabin altitude rate of change. 3) limit the differential pressure. 4) balance the aircraft altitude with cabin altitude. 5) ventilate the cabin. 6) keep a constant differential pressure throughout all the phases of flight. The combination regrouping all the correct statements is:
Cabin air for a large aeroplane during flight:
options: has a constant oxygen ratio regulated to a preset value.

questions.questionText: The cabin differential pressure is:
options: the pressure differential between the air entering and leaving the cabin.

questions.questionText: The term "pressure cabin" applies when an aeroplane:
options: only pressurises the flight deck area.
has the ability to maintain a sea level cabin altitude at all altitudes.
has the ability to maintain a zero cabin differential pressure at all altitudes.
has the means to maintain cabin pressure higher than ambient pressure.

questions.questionText: If the automatic control of an air conditioning system fails:
options: a red failure light will illuminate.
the pilot can revert to manual control and set the control valve to the required setting.
the system must be shut down.
manual control can be selected but this is limited to "hot air" or "cold air" setting only.

questions.questionText: In a "bootstrap" cooling system, the bleed air is compressed in the cold air unit to:
options: ensure an adequate air flow across the secondary heat exchanger.
maintain a constant cabin mass air flow.
ensure a sufficient temperature drop in the secondary heat exchanger.
increase the cabin air supply pressure when the inlet pressure is too low.

questions.questionText: If the pressure in the cabin tends to become lower than the outside ambient air pressure the:
options: negative pressure relief valves will close.
negative pressure relief valves will open.

questions.questionText: Cabin differential pressure means the pressure difference between:
options: cabin pressure and ambient air pressure.
cabin pressure and ambient air pressure at MSL.
actual cabin pressure and selected pressure.

questions.questionText: In an air cycle machine (bootstrap system), bleed air downstream of the first heat exchanger is:
options: passed across an expansion turbine, then compressed and passed through a secondary heat exchanger.
compressed, passed through a secondary heat exchanger, and then passed across an expansion turbine.
passed across an expansion turbine, then directly passed to the second heat exchanger.
compressed, then passed across an expansion turbine and finally passed across the second heat exchanger.

questions.questionText: Under normal flight conditions, cabin pressure is controlled by.
options: regulating the discharge of air through the outflow valves.

questions.questionText: In a turbo compressor air conditioning system (bootstrap system), the main water separation unit is located:
Main cabin temperature is:
options: not controllable at the maximum cabin differential pressure.
only controllable at maximum cabin differential pressure.
controlled automatically, or by flight crew selection.

questions.questionText: In an air cycle machine (bootstrap system) the:
options: turbine drives the compressor, which increases the temperature of the air, thus making the second heat exchanger more efficient.
turbine increases the pressure of the air supply to the cabin.
temperature drop across the turbine is the main contributor to the cooling effect of the air cycle machine.
turbine drives the compressor, which provides pressurisation.

questions.questionText: At FL310, the cabin outflow valve moves to a completely open position, causing depressurisation. The crew don their oxygen masks and start an emergency descent. The following indications can be seen on the cabin VSI:
options: The cabin altitude increases until the pressure differential is zero, then the cabin VSI shows a descent equal to the aircraft VSI.
The cabin altitude decreases more slowly than the aircraft altitude indication until the pressure differential is zero, then it descends at the same speed.
The cabin VSI shows a climb until it indicates the altitude of the valve failure, then it reads zero, while the aircraft VSI indicates cabin and aircraft altitude.
The cabin VSI shows a climb until cabin pressure equals ambient pressure, then a descent at half the rate of the aircraft VSI.

questions.questionText: In a typical air conditioning system of modern large transport aeroplanes the reduction of air temperature and pressure is achieved by:
Assuming cabin differential pressure has reached the required value in normal flight conditions, if flight altitude and air conditioning system setting are maintained:
options: the outflow valves will move to the fully open position.
the pressurization system ceases to function until leakage reduces the pressure.
the mass air flow through the cabin is constant.
the outflow valves will move to the fully closed position.

questions.questionText: The purpose of an air conditioning pack inlet flow valve (pack valve) is to:
options: discharge cabin air to atmosphere if cabin pressure rises above the selected altitude.
regulate cabin pressure at the maximum cabin pressure differential.
regulate cabin pressure to the selected altitude.
maintain a constant and sufficient air mass flow to ventilate the cabin.

questions.questionText: A warning device alerts the crew in case of an excessive cabin altitude. This warning must be triggered on reaching the following altitude:
If the maximum certified altitude of an aeroplane is limited by the pressurised cabin, this limitation is due to the maximum:
options: positive cabin differential pressure at maximum cabin altitude.
negative cabin differential pressure at maximum cabin altitude.

questions.questionText: When the air is compressed for pressurization purposes, the percentage oxygen content is:
options: dependent on the degree of pressurization.

questions.questionText: The primary function of an air cycle machine is to:
options: pump the conditioned air into the cabin.
decrease the pressure of the bleed air.
remove water from the bleed air.

questions.questionText: If the maximum operating altitude of an airplane is limited by the pressurized cabin, this limitation is due to the maximum:
options: negative cabin differential pressure at maximum operating ceiling.
positive cabin differential pressure at maximum cabin altitude
negative differential pressure at maximum cabin altitude.
positive cabin differential pressure at maximum operating ceiling.

questions.questionText: Cabin pressure is controlled by.
options: the cabin air mass flow control inlet valve(s).
delivering a substantially constant flow of air into the cabin and controlling the outflow.
controlling the flow of air into the cabin with constant outflow.

questions.questionText: The purpose of the pack cooling fans in the air conditioning system is to:
options: supply the heat exchangers with cooling air during cruise flight.
supply the Passenger Service Unit (PSU) with fresh air.
supply the heat exchangers with cooling air during slow flights and ground operation.

questions.questionText: A cabin rate of descent:
options: results in a cabin pressure decrease.
results in a cabin pressure increase.
is always the same as the aeroplane's rate of descent.
is not possible at constant aeroplane altitudes.

questions.questionText: An air cycle machine (air conditioning pack):
options: increases outlet pressure whilst causing the temperature to drop in the heat exchanger.
does not affect the bleed air.
decreases bleed air pressure whilst causing the temperature to rise in the heat exchanger.
causes a pressure and temperature drop in the bleed air.

questions.questionText: An aeroplane with a pressurised cabin flies at FL310 and, following a malfunction of the pressure controller, the outflow valve runs to the open position. Given: CAB V/S = Cabin rate of climb indication CAB ALT = Cabin pressure altitude DELTA P = Differential pressure This will result in a:
options: CAB V/S increase, CAB ALT increase, DELTA P decrease
CAB V/S decrease, CAB ALT decrease, DELTA P increase
CAB V/S increase, CAB ALT increase, DELTA P increase
CAB V/S decrease, CAB ALT increase, DELTA P decrease

questions.questionText: In a large transport aeroplane the main temperature reduction of the conditioned air is achieved in:
If the cabin altitude rises (aircraft in level flight), the differential pressure:
options: may exceed the maximum permitted differential unless immediate preventative action is taken.

questions.questionText: Which of the following components of a common turboprop aircraft (e.g. Dash 8) are protected by an anti-icing system?
options: Wings, pitot tubes and static ports.
Engine inlets, pitot tubes and static ports.
APU inlet, pitot tubes, static ports and wings.
Wings, pitot tubes, static ports and waste water exhaust.

questions.questionText: Windscreen heating systems usually.
options: are powered from the emergency DC bus.
depend upon the pilot monitoring the windscreen temperature probe for control of the heating system.
cycle ON / OFF to maintain a windscreen temperature between approximately 18° and 35 °C.
consist of warm air from the cabin conditioning system blown across the inner surface of the windscreen.

questions.questionText: The ice protection for propellers of most turboprop aeroplanes works:
The de-icing of a propeller by fluid is achieved through:
With regard to pneumatic mechanical devices that afford ice protection the only correct statement is:
options: they are used extensively on modern aircraft as they are inexpensive and easy to maintain.
they can only be used as anti-icing devices.
they can be used as both de-icing and anti-icing devices.
they can only be used as de-icing devices.

questions.questionText: The heating facility for the windshield of an aircraft is:
options: used on a continual basis as it reduces the thermal gradients which adversely affect the useful life of the components.
only used when hot air demisting is insufficient.
used only at low altitudes where there is a risk of ice formation.

does not affect aerodynamic performance of the wing and causes no reduction in maximum thrust.

electromechanical unit and can be disconnected in flight.

questions.questionText: How is the voltage controlled in a modern alternator?
options: By increasing the number of windings on the armature.
By changing the RPM of the high pressure turbine.
By several diodes connected in series.
It is directly controlled by the voltage regulator.

questions.questionText: On an aeroplane using AC as primary power supply, the batteries are charged in flight from:
options: the AC bus via current limiters.

questions.questionText: If the electrical load of an AC generator providing a constant output voltage increases, the voltage regulator will:
options: change the direction of the excitation current.

questions.questionText: A 3 phase AC generator has 3 separate stator windings spaced at:
When two DC generators are operating in parallel, control of load sharing is achieved by:
options: an equalising circuit which, in turn, controls the speed of the generators.
an equalising circuit which, in conjunction with the voltage regulators, varies the field excitation current of the generators.

questions.questionText: When AC generators are operated in parallel, they must be of the same:
On detection of a persistent overvoltage fault on an AC generator connected to the aircraft AC busbars (paralleled bus system), the on-board protection device opens:
options: the generator breaker and tie breaker.
the exciter breaker, the generator breaker and tie breaker.
the exciter breaker and the generator breaker.

questions.questionText: Refer to figure 021-25. The diagram shown in annex represents a twin engine aeroplane electrical system. Batteries are located positions:
An aircraft electrical circuit which uses the aircraft structure as a return path to earth, may be defined as a:
Refer to figure 021-30. GEN 2 can be used to feed the AC BUS 1 when: (Note: all contactors not mentioned are assumed to remain open)
options: C1-4, C2-2 and C2-1 are closed.
C1-4, C5-3, C7-4, C7-1 and C5-1 are closed.

questions.questionText: Refer to figure 021-30. GEN 2 can be used to feed the DC ESS BUS: (Note: all contactors not mentioned are assumed to remain open)
options: via AC BUS 2, when C1-4, C3-2 and C5-2 are closed.
via AC BUS 2 and AC ESS BUS, when C1-4, C3-2 and C5-2 are closed.
via AC BUS 2 and AC ESS BUS, when C1-1, C1-4, C3-1, C3-2 and C5-2 are closed.
via AC BUS 2, when C1-1, C3-1 and C5-2 are closed.

questions.questionText: In an aircraft electrical system where AC generators are not paralleled, the bus tie breaker (relay) may allow:
options: connection of the failed AC generator to the opposite generator's busbar.
connection of the ground power truck to its distribution busbar.
connection of the AC generator to its distribution busbar.
power to be supplied to the failed AC generator's busbar.

questions.questionText: To ensure correct load sharing between AC generators operating in parallel:
options: both real and reactive loads must be matched.
the matching of loads is unimportant.
only reactive loads need to be matched.
only real loads need to be matched.

questions.questionText: The so called HOT BUSSES or DIRECT BUSSES are:
options: automatically connected to the battery if generators have failed.
kept in operating conditions by an electrical resistance in the case of energy failure.

questions.questionText: In a two generator system, a differential relay will ensure that:
options: only one generator can supply the busbar at the same time.
one generator comes "on-line" before the other.
generator voltages are not equal, dependent on load.
generator voltages are almost equal before the generators are paralleled.

questions.questionText: Load shedding means:
options: reduction of airloads on the flaps by means of the flap load relief valve.
to leave behind extra cargo if the centre of gravity moves outside limits.
temporary or permanent switching off of certain electric users to avoid overload of electric generators.
a procedure used in control systems to reduce the stick forces.

questions.questionText:
The advantages of grounding the negative pole of the aircraft structure are:
1) weight saving
2) easy fault detection.
3) increase of short-circuit risk.
4) reduction of short-circuit risk.
5) circuits are not single-wired lines.
The combination regrouping all the correct statements is:
The function of the generator breaker is to close when the voltage of the:
options: generator is greater than battery voltage and to open when the opposite is true.
battery is greater than the alternator voltage and to open when the opposite is true.
alternator is greater than the battery voltage and to open when the opposite is true.
battery is greater than the generator voltage and to open when the opposite is true.

questions.questionText: The phase relationship between two unparalleled AC generators:

The advantages of alternating current on board an aircraft are:
1) simple connection.
2) high starting torque.
3) flexibility in use.
4) lighter weight of equipment.
5) easy to convert into direct current.
6) easy maintenance of machines.
The combination of correct statements is:
Because of the connection in parallel of electrical equipment on an AC busbar, isolation of individual equipments:
In order for DC generators to achieve equal load sharing when operating in parallel, it is necessary to ensure that:
options: equal loads are connected to each generator busbar before paralleling.
the synchronising busbar is disconnected from the busbar system.

questions.questionText:
On-board electrical systems are protected against faults of the following type:
1) AC generator over-voltage.
2) AC generator under-voltage.
3) over-current.
4) over-speed.
5) under-frequency.
6) undue vibration of AC generators.
The combination of correct statements is:
If two constant frequency AC generators are operating independently, then the phase relation between both generators:
When the AC generators are connected in parallel, the reactive loads are balanced by means of the:
options: torque of the constant speed drive (CSD).

questions.questionText: On detection of a persistent phase imbalance between an AC generator connected to the main busbars and other AC generators, the protection device that opens is/are the:
options: exciter control relay and the generator breaker.
generator breaker and the tie breaker(s).

questions.questionText: A "bus bar" is:
options: a device which may only be used in DC circuits.
a distribution means for electrical power.
the stator of a moving coil instrument.
a device permitting operation of two or more switches together.

questions.questionText: When AC generators are operated in parallel, they must be of the same:
When carrying out battery condition check using the aeroplane's voltmeter:
options: no load should be applied to the battery because it would depress the voltage.
a load should be applied to the battery in order to give a better indication of condition.

questions.questionText: Immediately after starting engine(s) with no other electrical services switched on, an ammeter is showing a high charge rate to the battery.
options: indicates a generator failure, thus requiring the engine to be shut down immediately.
indicates a battery failure since there should be no immediate charging.
would be normal and is only cause for concern if the high charge rate persists.
indicates a faulty reverse current relay.

questions.questionText: Generators, when connected to the same busbar, are usually connected:
options: dependent on the type of engine.
dependent on the type of generator.

questions.questionText: Direct current generators are connected:
options: in parallel to provide maximum voltage.
in series to provide maximum voltage.
in parallel to provide maximum power.
in series to provide maximum power.

questions.questionText: In the event of an AC generator fault, on a twin engine aircraft, the protection device will open:
options: the generator breaker and the bus tie breaker.
the exciter control relay, the generator breaker and the bus tie breaker
the exciter control relay and the generator breaker.

questions.questionText: The measured output power components of a constant frequency AC system are:
Refer to figure 021-34. What is located in the box between DC ESS BUS and AC ESS BUS (marked with red number "1")?
Pulling the fire shut-off handle causes a number of devices to disconnect. In respect of the AC generator it can be said that the:
options: exciter control relay and the generator breaker open.
exciter control relay, the generator breaker and the tie breaker open.

questions.questionText: The type of windings commonly used in DC starter motors are:
To reverse the direction of rotation of a shunt-type (parallel field) DC electric motor, it is necessary to:
options: reverse the polarity of the motor connections.
connect a phase-shift capacitor to the field circuit.
reverse the polarity of either the stator or the rotor.
change the connections from shunt to series.

questions.questionText: The speed of an asynchronous 4-pole motor supplied at a frequency of 400 Hertz is:
The crank assembly of a piston engine comprises the:
options: Crankcase, crankshaft, pistons and connecting rods.

questions.questionText: The power of a piston engine, which is measured by a friction brake, is:
Diesel engines, compared to petrol engines have:

Both gas turbine and piston engines use a cycle made up of induction, compression, combustion and exhaust phases. Which of these statements about a piston engine are correct or incorrect?
1) The process in a piston engine is intermittent.
2) Theoretically the combustion occurs at constant volume.
The part of a piston engine that transforms reciprocating movement into rotary motion is called the:
The power output of a diesel engine without a turbo charger is regulated by:
The power output of a piston engine is directly proportional to:
Apart from flight into known icing conditions, the intake system of a diesel engine:
options: is never heated because a diesel engine is an injection engine.
needs to be heated to prevent ice on the air filter.
needs to be heated at low power settings, due to vaporisation heat.
is never heated because diesel engines operate at very high temperatures.

questions.questionText: The working cycle of a four-stroke engine is:
Assuming the same swept volume and no turbo charger, diesel engines compared to petrol engines:
options: produce a higher maximum power output.
show a higher fuel flow at the same power output.
operate at higher exhaust gas temperatures.

questions.questionText: The correct formula to calculate the total displacement (engine capacity) of a multi-cylinder piston engine is the:
options: cylinder volume x number of cylinders
piston area x piston stroke x number of cylinders

questions.questionText: On four-stroke piston engines, the theoretical valve and ignition settings are readjusted in order to increase the:
The ignition occurs in each cylinder of a four-stroke engine (TDC = Top Dead Centre):
options: before TDC at each crankshaft revolution.
behind TDC at each second crankshaft revolution.
behind TDC at each crankshaft revolution.
before TDC at each second crankshaft revolution.

questions.questionText: Specific fuel consumption is defined as the:
options: mass of fuel required to produce unit power for unit time.
quantity of fuel required to run the engine for one minute at maximum operating conditions.
designed fuel consumption for a given RPM.
maximum fuel consumption of the aircraft.

questions.questionText: The power output of a piston engine can be calculated by:

Both gas turbine and piston engines use a cycle made up of induction, compression, combustion and exhaust phases. Which of these statements about a piston engine are correct or incorrect? 1) The process in a piston engine is continuous. 2) Theoretically the combustion occurs at constant volume.
The useful work area in an ideal Otto engine indicator diagram is enclosed by the following gas state change lines:

Both gas turbine and piston engines use a cycle made up of induction, compression, combustion and exhaust phases. Which of these statements about a piston engine are correct or incorrect? 1) The process in a piston engine is continuous. 2) Theoretically the combustion occurs at constant pressure.
Diesel engines always produce a certain amount of soot, because:
options: the fuel droplets coming from the atomiser do not burn completely.
the fuel droplets are too small.
the intake air is too cold.
the mixture is always too rich.

questions.questionText: A piston engine compression ratio is the ratio of the:
options: swept volume to the clearance volume.
total volume to the swept volume.
clearance volume to the swept volume.
total volume to the clearance volume.

questions.questionText: The thermal efficiency of a piston engine is approximately:

Given the following statements about diesel engines: 1) power is regulated by the throttle valve. 2) there is no throttle valve. 3) power is set by the fuel flow. 4) thermal efficiency is lower than that for a petrol engine. 5) diesel fuel is less inflammable than petrol. The combination that regroups all of the correct statements is:
In a reciprocating four-stroke engine, the only "driving" stroke is the:

Given the following statements about diesel engines: 1) power is set by the mixture control. 2) there is no mixture control. 3) the amount of power is determined by the fuel flow only. 4) thermal efficiency is higher than that for a petrol engine. 5) diesel fuel is more inflammable than petrol. The combination that regroups all of the correct statements is:
The torque of an aeroplane engine can be measured at the:
options: gear box which is located between the engine and the propeller.

questions.questionText: The compression ratio of a piston engine is the ratio of the:
options: area of the piston to the cylinder volume.
diameter of the bore to the piston stroke.
weight of the air induced to its weight after compression.
volume of the cylinder with the piston at bottom dead centre to that with the piston at top dead centre.

questions.questionText: Both gas turbine and piston engines use a cycle made up of induction, compression, combustion and exhaust phases. Which of these statements about a piston engine are correct or incorrect? 1) The process in a piston engine is intermittent. 2) Theoretically the combustion occurs at constant pressure.
The thermal efficiency of a diesel engine is higher than that of a petrol engine because:
options: the compression ratio is much higher.
the air mass flow through the engine is higher.
the calorific value of the fuel is higher.

questions.questionText: If an engine detonates during climb-out, the normal corrective action would be to:
The relationship between compression ratio of a piston engine and the required fuel octane rating is that the:
options: higher the compression ratio, the lower the octane rating required.
higher the compression ratio, the higher the octane rating required.
lower the compression ratio, the higher the octane rating required.
required octane rating is independent of the compression ratio.

questions.questionText: The use of too low an octane fuel may cause:
The cylinder head and oil temperatures may exceed their normal operating ranges if:
options: the engine is operated at a higher than normal oil pressure.
the engine is operated at a too rich mixture.
a higher octane rating than specified for the engine is used.
a lower octane rating than specified for the engine is used.

questions.questionText: Aircraft fuel tanks should be checked for water at least:
options: before the first flight of the day.

questions.questionText: When detonation is recognised in a piston engine, the correct procedure is to:
options: reduce manifold pressure and lean the mixture.
increase manifold pressure and lean the mixture.
increase manifold pressure and fully enrich the mixture.
reduce manifold pressure and fully enrich the mixture.

questions.questionText: Pre-ignition refers to the condition that may arise when:
options: the mixture is ignited by abnormal conditions within the cylinder before the spark occurs at the plug.
the sparking plug ignites the mixture too early.
a rich mixture is ignited by the sparking plugs.
the mixture is ignited before the piston has reached top dead centre.

questions.questionText: The conditions which can cause detonation are:
options: low manifold pressure and high revolutions per minute.
high manifold pressure and high revolutions per minute.
low manifold pressure and high fuel flow.
high manifold pressure and low revolutions per minute.

questions.questionText: On design purpose, the relationship between the fuel octane rating and the maximum compression ratio of a piston engine is:
options: the maximum compression ratio is independent of the octane rating.
the higher the octane rating is, the higher the maximum compression ratio is.
the lower the octane rating is, the higher the maximum compression ratio is.
the higher the octane rating is, the lower the maximum compression ratio is.

questions.questionText: Vapour lock is:
options: vaporizing of fuel in the nozzles.
the formation of water vapour in a fuel system.
the inability of a fuel to vaporize in the nozzles.
vaporizing of fuel prior to reaching the engine.

questions.questionText: The octane rating of a fuel characterises the:
options: quantity of heat generated by its combustion.

questions.questionText: Which one of the following factors would be most likely to increase the possibility of detonation occurring within a piston engine?
options: Using an engine with a low compression ratio.
The use of a fuel with a high octane rating as compared to the use of one with a low octane rating.

questions.questionText: Vapour lock is:
options: a blockage in a fuel feed line caused by a fuel vapour bubble.
the abnormal mixture enrichment caused by a greater gasoline vaporisation in the carburettor.
the exhaust gas obstruction caused by an engine overheating.
the effect of water vapour bubbles in the induction manifold caused by condensation.

questions.questionText: Vapour lock is the phenomenon by which:
options: burnt gas plugs forming and remaining in the exhaust manifold following an overheat and thereby disturbing the exhaust.
abrupt and abnormal enrichment of the fuel/air mixture following an inappropriate use of carburettor heat.
heat produces vapour bubbles that can block the fuel line.
water vapour plugs are formed in the intake fuel line following the condensation of water in fuel tanks which have not been drained properly.

questions.questionText: A piston engine may use a fuel of a different grade than the recommended:
options: provided that the grade is higher.
provided that the grade is lower.
provided that it is an aeronautical petrol.

questions.questionText: The auxiliary fuel pumps installed on single-engine low-wing aeroplanes are typically used: 1) during take-off. 2) during cruise. 3) during landing. 4) in emergency situations. 5) in case of a suspected vapour lock. 6) in case of failure of the engine fuel pump.
In an engine equipped with a float-type carburettor, the low temperature that causes carburettor ice is normally the result of:
options: freezing temperature of the air entering the carburettor.
compression of air at the carburettor venturi.
vaporization of fuel and expansion of the air in the carburettor.

questions.questionText: Excessive priming of a piston engine should be avoided because: 1) it drains the carburettor float chamber. 2) the risk of engine fire. 3) the risk of flooding the engine. 4) it fouls the spark plugs. The combination that regroups all of the correct statements is:
The main purpose of the mixture control is to:
options: decrease the oxygen supplied to the engine.
increase the oxygen supplied to the engine.
decrease the air supplied to the engine.
adjust the fuel flow to obtain the correct fuel/air ratio.

questions.questionText: In which sections of the carburettor would icing most likely occur?
options: Accelerator pump and main metering jet.
Main air bleed and main discharge nozzle.
Float chamber and fuel inlet filter.

questions.questionText: The mixture controller of a carburettor:
options: alters the pressure drop at the main discharge nozzle.
moves the butterfly valve through a separate linkage to the main throttle control.
varies the air supply at the main discharge nozzle.
varies the fuel supply to the main discharge nozzle.

questions.questionText: To maintain a constant mixture at low and high power settings in a piston engine, a carburettor is fitted with:
The function of the primer pump in a reciprocating engine is to:
options: provide additional fuel for engine start.
serve as main supply pump in a fuel injection system.
inject additional fuel during engine acceleration.
serve as an alternate pump in case of engine driven pump failure.

questions.questionText: Variations in the mixture ratios for carburettors are achieved by the adjustment of:
In a piston engine, the purpose of an altitude mixture control is to:
options: enrich the mixture strength due to decreased air density at altitude.
prevent a weak cut when the throttle is opened rapidly at altitude.
correct for variations in the fuel/air ratio due to decreased air density at altitude.
weaken the mixture strength because of reduced exhaust back pressure at altitude.

questions.questionText: The operating principle of float-type carburettors is based on the:
options: increase in air velocity in the throat of a venturi causing an increase in air pressure.
measurement of the fuel flow into the induction system.
difference in air pressure at the venturi throat and the air inlet.
automatic metering of air at the venturi as the aircraft gains altitude.

questions.questionText: With respect to a piston engined aircraft, ice in the carburettor:
options: will only form at OATs below +10 °C.
may form at OATs higher than +10 °C.
will only form at OATs below the freezing point of fuel.
will only form at outside air temperatures (OAT's) below the freezing point of water.

questions.questionText: In addition to the fire hazard introduced, excessive priming should be avoided because:
options: it washes off the lubricant from the cylinder walls.
the gasoline dilutes the oil and necessitates changing oil.
it drains the carburettor float chamber.

questions.questionText: Which statement is correct concerning the effect of the application of carburettor heat?
options: The volume of air entering the carburettor is reduced, thus leaning the mixture.
The density of the air entering the carburettor is reduced, thus enriching the mixture.
The density of the air entering the carburettor is reduced, thus leaning the mixture.
The volume of air entering the carburettor is reduced, thus enriching the mixture.

questions.questionText: A fuel strainer, when fitted to a carburettor will be positioned:
options: between the needle valve and the metering jet.
between the metering jet and the discharge nozzle.

questions.questionText: The purpose of the barometric correction in a carburettor is to:
options: maintain the correct weight fuel-to-air ratio when the altitude increases
maintain a constant fuel flow irrespective of the altitude
reduce the fuel-to-air mass ratio when altitude increases
increase the fuel-to-air mass ratio when altitude increases

questions.questionText: When applying carburettor heating:
options: no change occurs in the mixture ratio.
a decrease in RPM results from the lean mixture.

questions.questionText: The first indication of carburettor icing during cruise, in aeroplanes equipped with constant speed propellers, would most likely be a:
options: rough running engine followed by loss in RPM.
rough running engine followed by an increase in manifold pressure.

questions.questionText: The purpose of the venturi in a carburettor is to:
options: prevent enrichment of the mixture due to high air velocity through the carburettor.
ensure complete atomisation of the fuel before entering the injection system.
create a rise in pressure at the throat before the mixture enters the induction system.
create the suction necessary to cause fuel to flow through the carburettor main jets.

questions.questionText: The main reason for opening the cowl flaps is to control the:
On an air-cooled reciprocating engine the cooling airflow is provided by:
The purpose of the vents in oil tanks is primarily to:
options: prevent excessive pressure from building up in tank.
allow for expansion of hot oil.

questions.questionText: The dry sump oil system for a piston engine incorporates an oil tank with normally two stack pipes installed. Where can you find those stack pipes?
options: Both in the sides of the tank.
One in the top and one in the bottom of the tank.
Both in the bottom of the tank.
Both in the top of the tank.

questions.questionText: The lubricating system of an air cooled piston engine is used to:
options: to operate the fuel control unit.
reduce internal friction and provide cooling.

questions.questionText: Viscosity is:
options: the temperature dependence of an oil.
the flow velocity inside the oil lines.
the tendency of a liquid or gas to resist flow.
the pressure resistance of an oil.

questions.questionText: The reading on the oil pressure gauge is the:
options: difference between the pressure pump pressure and the scavenge pump pressure.
pressure of the oil on the inlet side of the pressure pump.
pressure in the oil tank reservoir.
pressure of the oil on the outlet side of the pressure pump.

questions.questionText: The dry sump oil system for a piston engine incorporates an oil cooler that is fitted:
options: between the oil tank and the pressure pump.
after the pressure pump but before the oil passes through the engine.
after the oil has passed through the engine and before it enters the sump.
in the return line to the oil tank after the oil has passed through the scavenge pump.

questions.questionText: For internal cooling, reciprocating engines are especially dependent on:
For a given type of oil, the oil viscosity depends on the:
If the ground wire between the magneto and the ignition switch becomes disconnected, the most noticeable result will be that the engine:
options: will not operate on the right magneto.
will not operate on the left magneto.
cannot be started with the switch in the ON position.
cannot be shut down by turning the switch to the OFF position.

questions.questionText:
An aircraft magneto is switched off by:
1) grounding the primary circuit.
2) opening the primary circuit.
3) opening the secondary circuit.
4) grounding the secondary circuit.
The combination that regroups all of the correct statements is:
If the magneto selector switch is set to the OFF position a piston engine continues to run normally. The most probable cause is that:
options: there is a carbon deposit on the spark plugs electrodes.
a wire from the magneto is in contact with a metallic part of the engine.
the grounding wire of one of the magnetos is broken.
there are local hot points in the engine (probably due to overheating of the cylinder heads).

questions.questionText: A capacitor in parallel with breaker points:
options: permits arcing across the breaker points
induces a very high current across the primary windings.
induces a very high current across the secondary windings.
prevents arcing across the breaker points.

questions.questionText: Ignition systems of a running piston engine receive electrical energy from:
The very rapid magnetic field changes (flux changes) in the primary coil of a magneto are accomplished by the:
options: distributor arm aligning with one of the high tension segments.
rotor turning past the position of maximum flux in the armature.

questions.questionText: A capacitor in parallel with breaker points:
options: induces a very high current across the primary windings.
induces a very high current across the secondary windings.
induces a very high voltage across the secondary windings.

questions.questionText: An impulse coupling is installed on a magneto of a piston engine to:
options: provide a stronger spark on top dead centre for engine starting.

questions.questionText: With an aircraft fitted with a fixed pitch propeller, during flight at normal cruising speed, one magneto fails completely. This will first cause:
options: an additional load on the other magneto.

questions.questionText: If the ground wire between the magnetos and the ignition switch becomes disconnected the most noticeable result will be that:
options: a still operating engine will shut down.
the engine cannot be shut down by turning the ignition switch to the "OFF" position.
the engine cannot be started with the ignition switch in the "ON" position.
the power developed by the engine will be strongly reduced.

questions.questionText: An impulse magneto coupling:
options: gives a retarded spark at starting.
advances ignition timing and gives a hotter spark at starting.
gives an automatic spark increase during high speed operation.
reduces magneto speed during engine warm-up.

questions.questionText: The purpose of a contact breaker (ignition switch) is to:
options: connect the battery to the magneto.
connect the contact breaker and condenser in series with the primary coil.
connect the secondary coil to the distributor.
control the primary circuit of the magneto.

questions.questionText: The purpose of a distributor in the ignition system of a piston engine is to distribute:
options: primary current to the sparking plugs.
secondary current to the sparking plugs.

questions.questionText: In a piston engine, magnetos are used to produce the spark which ignites the fuel/air mixture. The operating principle of magnetos consists of:
options: obtaining a high amperage, low voltage current in order to generate the spark.
breaking the primary circuit in order to induce a low amperage, high voltage current, which is distributed to the spark plugs.
accumulating in a capacitor a low voltage current from the battery and inducing it as a high voltage current at the moment the spark is generated.
creating a brief high intensity magnetic field that will be sent through the distributor at the appropriate time.

questions.questionText: Under normal running conditions a magneto draws primary current:
options: from the aircraft batteries via an inverter.
from a self-contained electromagnetic induction system.

questions.questionText: Dual ignition provides a factor of reliability and:
options: saves wear caused by using one magneto constantly.

questions.questionText: An impulse coupling is inhibited at higher than normal rotational starting speeds by:
options: electro-magnetic action of an operating magneto.

questions.questionText: Once the engine has started, ignition systems of piston engines are:
options: independent of the electrical system of the aircraft.

questions.questionText: As altitude increases, if the mixture is not leaned:
options: the density of air entering the carburettor decreases and the fuel flow increases.
the density of air entering the carburettor decreases and the amount of fuel remains constant.
the volume of air entering the carburettor decreases and the amount of fuel decreases.
the volume of air entering the carburettor remains constant and the fuel flow decreases.

questions.questionText: From the cruise, with all the parameters correctly set, if the altitude is reduced, to maintain the same mixture the fuel flow should:
options: increase or decrease, depending on the engine type.

questions.questionText: When excessively leaning the mixture for a better fuel economy, but still on the rich side of the peak EGT, the following engine parameter(s) may exceed their normal operating ranges:
options: low cylinder head and exhaust gas temperature.
high cylinder head and exhaust gas temperature.

questions.questionText: At constant RPM with a normally aspirated engine and a fixed pitch propeller, as altitude increases, if the mixture is not leaned:
options: both the density of air entering the carburettor and the fuel flow decrease.
the volume of air entering the carburettor remains constant and the fuel flow increases.
the mixture remains constant and the fuel flow decreases.
the density of air entering the carburettor decreases and the fuel flow increases.

questions.questionText: In a piston engine if the ratio of air to fuel is approximately 9:1, the mixture is:
A mixture setting richer than best power has to be used during climb segments. This results in a:
Maximum exhaust gas temperature (EGT) of a piston engine is theoretically associated with a:
options: mixture ratio very close to idle cut-out.

questions.questionText:
Refer to figure 021-12
On the diagram showing the power output of a piston engine as a function of mixture ratio, theoretical best economy on the rich side of the peak EGT is shown at:
An EGT (Exhaust Gas Temperature) indicator for a piston engine is used to:
options: assist the pilot to set the correct mixture.
control the carburettor inlet air flow.

questions.questionText: As altitude increases, the mixture ratio of a piston engine should be adjusted to:
options: increase the fuel flow in order to compensate for the decreasing air pressure and density.
reduce the fuel flow in order to compensate for the decreasing air density.
reduce the fuel flow in order to compensate for the increasing air density.

questions.questionText: When altitude increases, in a normally aspirated piston engine, mixture must be adjusted because there is:
options: an increase of air density resulting in too lean a mixture.
a decrease in air density resulting in too rich a mixture.
a decrease of air density resulting in too lean a mixture.
an increase of air density resulting in too rich a mixture.

questions.questionText: For a piston engine, the chemically correct fuel/air ratio is:
When starting an engine or when the engine runs at an idle RPM on ground, the mixture is:
options: rich, because the choke valve is closed.
rich, to make starting possible and to cool the engine sufficiently when idling.
lean, to prevent that the engine consumes too much fuel.
rich, because carburettor heat is switched on.

questions.questionText: When altitude increases without adjustment of the mixture ratio, the piston engine performance is affected because of:
options: a decrease of air density with a fuel flow which becomes too low.
a decrease of air density with a fuel flow which becomes too high.
an increase of air density with a fuel flow which becomes too low.
an increase of air density with a fuel flow which becomes too high.

questions.questionText: Overheating of a piston engine is likely to result from an excessively:
What may happen during a continuous climb with a mixture setting fully rich?
options: The engine will operate smoother even though fuel consumption is increased.

questions.questionText: For piston engines fuel/air ratio or mixture is the ratio between the:
options: mass of fuel and volume of air entering the carburettor.
volume of fuel and volume of air entering the carburettor.
volume of fuel and volume of air entering the cylinder.
mass of fuel and mass of air entering the cylinder.

questions.questionText: When leaning the mixture for the most economic cruise fuel flow, excessive leaning will cause:
When the pilot moves the mixture lever of a piston engine towards a lean position the:
options: amount of fuel entering the cylinders is reduced.
volume of air entering the carburettor is increased.
amount of fuel entering the cylinders is increased.
volume of air entering the carburettor is reduced.

questions.questionText:
Refer to figure 021-12
When does the mixture get richer?
An excessively rich mixture can be detected by.
options: a long purple flame from exhaust.

questions.questionText: In the event of an engine failure during flight, the blades of a constant speed propeller in a single engine aeroplane not fitted with a feathering system tend to move:
options: to a blade angle dependent on windmilling RPM.
towards the smallest blade angle by centrifugal twisting moment and/or spring force.
towards the highest blade angle by oil pressure created by the windmilling propeller.
towards the highest blade angle by the aerodynamic twisting moment.

questions.questionText: The main advantage of a constant speed propeller as compared to a fixed pitch propeller is that it:
options: maintains its maximum propeller efficiency over a wider range of airspeeds.
has a higher maximum propeller efficiency over a wider range of airspeeds.
does not require propeller blade twist.
has a lower propeller blade stress.

questions.questionText: When increasing true airspeed with a constant engine RPM, the angle of attack of a fixed pitch propeller.
options: stays constant because it only varies with engine RPM.

questions.questionText: During take-off, the angle of attack of the blades of a fixed pitch propeller, optimised for cruise condition is:
If the manifold pressure is increased, the blade angle of a constant speed propeller will:
options: reduce so that the engine RPM can increase
increase and after a short time will reduce.

questions.questionText: The blade angle of a propeller is the angle between the:
options: plane of rotation and the relative airflow.
reference chord line and the relative airflow.
reference chord line and the propeller axis of rotation.
reference chord line and the propeller plane of rotation.

questions.questionText: To unfeather a propeller during flight, the pilot typically has to:
options: increase speed so as to use the engine unfeathering pump.
manually release the blade latch mechanism.

questions.questionText: What is the principle advantage of using propeller reduction gears?
options: To enable the engine RPM to be increased with an accompanying increase in power and allow the propeller to remain at a lower, more efficient RPM.
The diameter and blade area of the propeller can be increased.
To enable the engine RPM to be increased with an accompanying increase in propeller RPM.
