options: a warning light for each engine and a single aural alert common to all engines.
one warning light and one aural alert common to all engines.
a warning light common to all engines but a separate aural alert for each engine.
both a warning light and an aural alert unique to each engine.

questions.questionText: A fault protection circuit in a fire detection system will:
options: automatically initiate APU shutdown and fire extinguisher striker activation in the event of fire.
inhibit the fire detector when the detection line is connected to ground.
activate the fire detection system when the detection line is connected to ground.
activate an alarm in the cockpit and in the landing gear bay for ground crew.

questions.questionText: Explain the working principle of a gaseous fire detection system:
options: A closed tube, when heated, causes the gas inside to expand and trigger an alarm via a pressure switch.
A closed tube consists of a gas which changes its translucence when heated. Light sensors will sense such change in translucence and trigger an alarm.
A closed tube, when heated, causes the gas inside to become a conductor and thereby closes an alarm circuit.
A closed tube, when heated, causes the gas inside to contract which causes a negative pressure and thereby trigger an alarm via a pressure switch.

questions.questionText: An engine fire in a large transport aircraft is indicated by a:
options: visual warning and an aural alert.

questions.questionText: When a part of a continuous loop fire detection system is heated: 1) its resistance decreases. 2) its resistance increases. 3) the leakage current increases. 4) the leakage current decreases. The combination regrouping all the correct statements is:
The most common extinguishing agent used in gas turbine engine fire protection system is:
Halon is used as a fire extinguishing agent because it:
options: acts as a very effective flame inhibitor.
uses the cooling effect created by the venturi during discharge.

questions.questionText: In a fire detection system with single-loop continuous components (with no fault protection), if the inner core contacts the outer casing:
options: the engine fire extinguisher striker is automatically activated.
the power supply is cut off automatically.
there will be no effect on the system.

questions.questionText: In a fire detection system with single-loop continuous components, with no fault protection, the sensing element is crushed, but the inner core does not touch the outer casing.
options: The fire extinguisher is automatically activated.
The power supply is automatically cut off.
The fire alarm will be triggered.

questions.questionText: In the event of an engine fire:
options: the fuel supply is disconnected by a quick release coupling.
an automatic shut-off valve is moved to the closed position controlled by the fire sensing system.
the fuel supply is isolated from the engine by pilot action (a pilot controlled fuel shut-off valve).
the fuel installation is protected by an automatic fire extinguishing system.

questions.questionText: When a continuous loop wire type fire detection system is tested:
options: the wiring and the warning are tested.
a part of the wire is heated.

questions.questionText: The bimetal strip detectors of an engine fire detection system: 1) are arranged in series 2) are arranged in parallel 3) open during a fire 4) close during a fire The combination containing all of the correct statements is:
When a bimetallic strip is used as a switch in a fire detection loop, a delay is incorporated after a fire alarm is triggered in order to:
options: wait for the triggering of the second fire detection loop in order to confirm the fire.
delay the triggering of the fire extinguishers and increase their efficiency.
avoid false alarms in case of vibrations.

questions.questionText: Generally, when the fire handle of the engine fire-extinguishing system on an aeroplane is pulled, the effects are: 1) opening of the bleed air valves and HP valves on the engine concerned. 2) arming of the extinguishing system. 3) immediate discharge of extinguishing agent. 4) deactivation of the electric generators. The combination that regroups all of the correct statements is:
A continuous loop detector system is a:
What is true when a fire loop detector detects low resistance and low capacity?
options: A test of the system is made.
At least one engine is on fire.

questions.questionText: A gaseous fire loop system is tested by:
options: checking the sensor with external pressurised gas.
checking the continuity of the system.
checking the wiring harness for faults but not the sensor.

questions.questionText: Continuous loop fire detector systems operate on the principle that an increase in temperature produces:
options: a decrease in the reference current.

questions.questionText: Power plant fire extinguishers are operated by:
options: a mechanical link withdrawing a safety pin and allowing a spring operated plunger to rupture the bottle seal.
an electrically fired cartridge rupturing a seal in the head of the bottle.
an electrically heated capsule rupturing a seal in the pipeline at the engine.
manual remote control opening a discharge valve.

questions.questionText: The correct statement about rain protection for cockpit windshields is that:
options: the electric de-icing system for cockpit windows is also suitable for rain protection.
rain repellent should never be sprayed onto the windshield unless the rainfall is very heavy.
wipers are sufficient under heavy rain conditions to provide adequate view through the cockpit windows.
the alcohol de-icing system for cockpit windows is also suitable for rain protection.

questions.questionText: When a cabin oxygen mask is pulled downwards, the passenger breathes:
options: pure oxygen at the ambient pressure.
a mixture of oxygen and cabin air.

questions.questionText: A diluter-demand oxygen regulator selected to 100%:
options: is only recommended for use with smoke in the cockpit.
delivers oxygen flow only above FL100.
mixes air and oxygen in a passenger oxygen mask.

questions.questionText: The opening of the doors giving access to the oxygen masks for the passengers is: 1) pneumatic for the gaseous oxygen system. 2) electrical for the chemical oxygen system. 3) pneumatic for the chemical oxygen system. 4) electrical for the gaseous oxygen system. The combination regrouping all the correct statements is:

options: The oxygen masks are automatically presented to cabin crew members and passengers.
The oxygen masks are automatically presented to flight crew members.
Manual override of the automatic presentation of passenger oxygen masks is, generally speaking, not possible.
If the automatic mask presentation has been activated, the oxygen will flow within the first 3 minutes.

The remaining fuel should be at least the same amount as the required fuel to reach an aerodrome where a safe landing can be made, with final reserve fuel remaining.
A diversion is an unplanned event so the commander is not responsible concerning this amount of fuel.

questions.questionText: Consider an IFR flight with a departure aerodrome called 'DEP' and a destination aerodrome called 'DEST' and a decision point called 'DP', since the decision point procedure has to be used. There is also a suitable aerodrome for refuelling, called aerodrome 'REF'. The pilot has calculated a trip fuel from DEP to DEST so the contingency will be at least:

The following table: Cruise: FL350, Air distance to be covered: 1 830 NM, Maximum Landing Mass: 180 000 kg, Maximum Take-off Mass: 205 000 kg, Maximum tank capacity: 40 000 kg, Planned Take-off Mass: 190 000 kg (with a minimum required fuel quantity of 30 000 kg that includes a Trip Fuel of 22 000 kg). The additional fuel quantity will be:
The purpose of the decision point procedure is?
options: To reduce the landing weight and thus reduce the structural stress on the aircraft.
To reduce the minimum required fuel and therefore be able to increase the Traffic Load.
To increase the safety of the flight.
To increase the amount of extra fuel.

questions.questionText: Which of the following statements is (are) correct with regard to the advantages of computer flight plans? 1) The computer can file the ATC flight plan. 2) Wind data used by the computer is always more up-to-date than that available to the pilot.
In the ATS flight plan, for a non-scheduled flight which of the following letters should be entered in item 8 (type of flight):
In the ATS flight plan item 15 (cruising speed), when not expressed as a Mach number, cruising speed is expressed as:

In the appropriate box of a flight plan form, concerning equipment, the letter to be used to indicate that the aircraft is equipped with a mode A 4096 codes transponder with altitude reporting capability is:
In an ATS flight plan item 15, in order to define a position as a bearing and distance from a VOR, the group of figures should consist of:
options: VOR ident, true bearing and distance in kilometres.
full name of VOR, true bearing and distance in kilometres.
VOR ident, magnetic bearing and distance in kilometres.
VOR ident, magnetic bearing and distance in nautical miles.

questions.questionText: In the ATS flight plan item 15, for a flight along a designated route, where the departure aerodrome is not on or connected, to that route:
options: it is necessary only to give the first reporting point on that route.
it is not necessary to indicate the point of joining that route as it will be obvious to the ATS unit.
the words "AS CLEARED" should be entered.
the letters "DCT" should be entered, followed by the point of joining the ATS route.

questions.questionText: In the ATS flight plan Item 15, a cruising speed of 470 kts will be entered as:
When completing an ATS flight plan, an elapsed time (item 16) of 1 hrs 55 min should be entered as:
Repetitive flight plans (RPLs) shall be used for flights operated regularly on the same day(s) of consecutive weeks and:
options: on at least ten (10) occasions or every day over a period of at least 10 consecutive days.
on at least ten (10) occasions or every day over a period of at least 20 consecutive days.

questions.questionText: A repetitive flight plan (RPL) is filed for a scheduled flight: Paris Orly to Angouleme, Paris Orly as alternate. Following heavy snow falls, Angouleme airport will be closed at the expected time of arrival. The airline decides before departure to plan a re-routing of that flight to Limoges.
options: The RPL must be cancelled for that day and a specific flight plan has to be filed.
It is not possible to plan another destination and the flight has to be simply cancelled that day (scheduled flight and not chartered).
The airline's Operations Department has to transmit a change in the RPL at the ATC office, at least half an hour before the planned time of departure.
The pilot-in-command must advise ATC of his intention to divert to Limoges at least 15 minutes before the planned time of arrival.

questions.questionText: For a repetitive flight plan (RPL) to be used, flights must take place on a regular basis on at least:
For flights in accordance with IFR within advisory airspace, an ATS Flight plan:
options: does not have to be submitted.
shall be submitted. There is no obligation to report changes.
shall be submitted. Changes to the flight plan have to be reported.
may be submitted at the pilot's discretion. In case a flight plan is submitted, all changes shall be reported as soon as practicable to the appropriate ATS unit.

questions.questionText: Pilots of controlled flights are requested to inform the appropriate ATS whenever the average TAS at cruising level between reporting points varies or is expected to vary from the TAS given in the flight plan by.
How many hours in advance of departure time should a flight plan be filed in the case of flights into areas subject to air traffic flow management (ATFM)?
A current flight plan is a:
options: flight plan in the course of which radio communication should be practised between aeroplane and ATC.
flight plan with the correct time of departure.
filed flight plan with amendments and clearance included.

questions.questionText: In the event of a delay for an uncontrolled flight for which an ATS flight plan has been submitted, the flight plan should be amended or a new ATS flight plan submitted and the old one canceled, when the delay exceeds the:

If a pilot lands at an aerodrome other than the destination aerodrome specified in the flight plan, he must ensure that the ATS unit at the destination aerodrome is informed within a certain number of minutes of his planned ETA at destination. This number of minutes is:
In the event that a controlled flight inadvertently deviates from its track given in the current ATS Flight Plan, action should be taken in order to:
options: Notify the appropriate ATS unit immediately notifying the new track and to comply with instructions from ATS.
Adjust the heading of aircraft to regain track as soon as practicable.
Climb or descent by 500 ft, inform the appropriate ATS unit.
Maintain VMC, wait for instructions from the appropriate ATS unit.

questions.questionText: During a flight, a change from instrument flight rules (IFR) to visual flight rules (VFR) is only acceptable, when VFR is permitted in that airspace and when:
options: the change is initiated by the PIC with a message containing the specific expression "cancelling my IFR flight".
the position of the change has been noted on the ATC flight plan. The cancellation of the IFR flight will then be made automatically by ATC.
the PIC has requested and obtained an ATC CLR for the change and has filed a special VFR flight plan.
ATC invites the PIC to change from IFR to VFR.

questions.questionText: It is possible, in flight, to: 1) file an IFR flight plan. 2) modify an active IFR or VFR flight plan. 3) cancel an active VFR flight plan. 4) close an active VFR flight plan. Which of the following combinations contains all of the correct statements?
In the ATS flight plan item 15, it is necessary to enter any point at which a change of cruising speed takes place. For this purpose a CHANGE OF SPEED is defined as:
In the event of a delay of a controlled flight, the submitted ATS flight plan should be amended or canceled and a new ATS flight plan submitted when the delay is in excess of:
options: 60 minutes of the estimated off-blocks time.
30 minutes of the estimated off-blocks time.
60 minutes of the estimated time of departure.
30 minutes of the estimated time of departure.

questions.questionText: During an IFR flight TAS and time estimate for the next reporting point appear to deviate from the data in the flight plan. In order to conform to PANS-RAC the deviations should be reported to ATC as soon as they are in excess of:
Changing of flight rules from IFR to VFR is possible:
options: if instructed by ATC as long as VMC is forecast during the next 60 minutes.
if instructed by ATC as long as VMC is forecast during the next 30 minutes.

questions.questionText: An aeroplane is on an IFR flight. The flight is to be changed from IFR to VFR. Is it possible?
options: No, you have to remain IFR in accordance to the filed flight plan.
No, only ATC can order you to do this.
Yes, the pilot in command must inform ATC using the phrase "cancelling my IFR flight".
Yes, but only with permission from ATC.

questions.questionText: You have a flight plan IFR from Amsterdam to London. In the flight plan it is noted that you will deviate from the ATS route passing the FIR boundary Amsterdam/London. The airway clearance reads "Cleared to London via flight planned route". Which of the following statements is correct?
options: The route according to the flight plan is accepted.
You will get a separate clearance for the deviation.
It is not allowed to file such a flight plan.
The filed deviation is not accepted.

questions.questionText: When an ATS flight plan has been submitted for a controlled flight, the flight plan should be amended or cancelled in the event of the off-block time being delayed by.
Which of the following statements regarding filing a flight plan is correct?
options: A flight plan should be filed when a national FIR boundary will be crossed.
A flying college can file repetitive flight plan for VFR flights.
Any flight plan should be filed at least 10 minutes before departure.
In case of ATFM (Air Traffic Flow Management) the flight plan should be filed at least three hours in advance of the EOBT.

questions.questionText: The planned departure time from the parking area is 1815 UTC. The estimated take-off time is 1825 UTC. The IFR flight plan must be filed with ATC at the latest at:
Change from IFR to VFR will always take place only:
options: as instructed by an air traffic control unit.
on the initiative of the aircraft commander.
when the aircraft is leaving controlled airspace during VMC.
at the clearance limit, irrespective of the weather conditions.

questions.questionText: The appropriate ATS unit has to be informed in case of inadvertent changes from a Flight plan:
options: if the time estimate for the next reporting point is found to be in error in excess of more than ten minutes from that notified to ATS.
if the average TAS at cruising level varies or is expected to vary by ±5% from that given in the flight plan.

questions.questionText: From the options given below select those flights which require ATC flight plan notification: 1) any Public Transport flight. 2) any IFR flight. 3) any flight which is to be carried out in regions which are designated to ease the provision of the Alerting Service or the operations of Search and Rescue. 4) most cross-border flights. 5) any flight which involves overflying water.

What will be the estimated landing mass at the alternate airport?

Block Fuel: 645 kg. The remaining flight time to destination is 1 h 50 min. The minimum fuel aboard to cover the rest of the flight is:
You are flying a constant compass heading of 252°. Variation is 22°E, deviation is 3°W and your INS is showing a drift of 9° to the right. True track is?

An aircraft flying at 7 500 ft is cleared to descend to be level at 1 000 ft, 6 NM before reaching a beacon. If ground speed is 156 kts and Rate of Descent is 800 ft/min, how many miles before the beacon should descent begin?

After a balked landing at the destination airport, you have to divert to the alternate airport with the gear extended. The re-calculated flight time to the alternate due to the reduced speed is 1 hr 50 min and the fuel flow will be 840 kg/h. Final Reserve Fuel remains unchanged. What will be the estimated landing mass at the alternate airport?

What will be the estimated landing mass at the alternate airport?
The still air distance in the climb is 189 Nautical Air Miles (NAM) and time 30 minutes. What ground distance would be covered in a 30 kts headwind?

Minimum planned take-off fuel is 160 kg. This amount includes a fuel reserve which equals to 30% of the trip fuel. Assume the groundspeed on this trip is constant. When the aeroplane has done half the distance the remaining fuel is 70 kg. Is diversion to a nearby alternate necessary?
options: Diversion to a nearby alternate is necessary, unless the captain decides to continue on his own responsibility.
Diversion to a nearby alternate is not necessary, because the reserve fuel has not been used completely.
Diversion to a nearby alternate is not necessary, because it is allowed to calculate without reserve fuel.
Diversion to a nearby alternate is necessary, because the remaining fuel is not sufficient.

questions.questionText: Refer to figure 033-64. Planned and actual data as shown in the Flight Log excerpt. Provided that flight conditions on the leg GAMMA to DELTA remain unchanged and fuel consumption remains unchanged, what fuel remaining should be expected at waypoint DELTA?

The flight time for direct flight GAMMA to MIKE will be 1 hr 08 min. Provided that flight conditions on the direct leg GAMMA to MIKE remain unchanged and fuel consumption remains unchanged, what fuel remaining should be expected at waypoint MIKE?

A multi engine piston aeroplane is on an IFR flight. The fuel plan gives a Trip Fuel of 65 US gallons. The alternate fuel, final reserve included, is 17 US gallons. Contingency fuel is 5% of the Trip Fuel. The usable fuel at departure is 93 US gallons. At a certain moment the fuel consumed according to the fuel gauges is 40 US gallons and the distance flown is half of the total distance. Assume that fuel consumption does not change. Which statement is right?
options: At destination the required reserves remain intact.
The remaining fuel is not sufficient to reach the destination with reserves intact.
At departure the reserve fuel was 28 US gallons.
At the destination there will still be 30 US gallons in the tanks.

questions.questionText: During a flight the fuel indicators show that the remaining amount of fuel is 100 lbs after 38 minutes. The total take-off fuel at departure was 160 lbs. For the alternate fuel, 30 lbs is necessary. The planned fuel for taxi was 13 lbs. Final reserve fuel is estimated at 50 lbs. If the fuel flow remains the same, how many minutes can be flown to the destination with the remaining fuel?

Refer to figure 033-71. Planned and actual data as shown in the Flight Log excerpt. Provided that flight conditions on the leg GAMMA to DELTA remain unchanged and fuel consumption remains unchanged, what fuel remaining should be expected at waypoint DELTA?
