Nose wheel weight: 1750 N
Left main wheel weight: 4050 N
Right main wheel weight: 4080 N
The basic empty mass of an aeroplane is recorded in:
options: the weighing schedule and is amended to take account of changes due to modifications of the aeroplane
the weighing schedule. If changes occur, due to modifications, the aeroplane must be re-weighed always.
the loading manifest. It differs from the zero fuel mass by the value of the "Traffic Load".
the loading manifest. It differs from dry operating mass by the value of the "Traffic Load".

questions.questionText: In relation to an aeroplane, the term "Basic Empty Mass" includes the mass of the aeroplane structure complete with its powerplants, systems, furnishings and other items of equipment considered to be an integral part of the particular aeroplane configuration. Its value is:
options: found in the latest version of the weighing schedule as corrected to allow for modifications.
found in the flight manual and is inclusive of unusable fuel plus fluids contained in closed systems.
printed in the Loading Manual and includes unusable fuel.
inclusive of an allowance for crew, crew baggage and other operating items. It is entered in the loading manifest.

questions.questionText: The principle of the index method is:
options: to round all values for easier and much faster calculation.
to calculate the centre of gravity position (CG) and stabiliser trim setting without calculating the load sheet.
to refer all the items of an aircraft to a position which is called BA (Balance Arm).
to divide high magnitude moments by a constant and thereby simplify calculations by decreased values.

questions.questionText: The method of using indexes in mass and balance calculations is used to:
options: decrease the magnitude of the moments.
increase the magnitude of the moments.
increase the magnitude of the useful load.
decrease the magnitude of the useful load.

questions.questionText: State the advantage(s) of the index method to determine moments:
options: it reduces the dimensions of the moments thus making it easier to calculate
it directly produces the centre of gravity position (CG) and the stabiliser trim setting.
it enables the pilots to calculate the centre of gravity position (CG) by mental arithmetic and thereby expedites mass and balance calculations.
it is a digital system and can be used to automatically upload data to the aircraft flight management computer (FMC) for determining the stabiliser trim setting

questions.questionText: Usually in mass and balance calculations an index is used. The loaded index is:
options: a value of moment which is simplified and scaled down.
used in fuel density calculations and is called fuel-loading index.
called floor-loading index and it is used to simplify the loading of an aircraft.
used for ground handling purposes and is called wing-loading index.

questions.questionText: In mass and balance calculations the "index" is:
options: a location in the aeroplane identified by a number.
is a figure without unit of measurement which represents a moment.
the range of moments the centre of gravity (CG) can have without making the aeroplane unsafe to fly.
an imaginary vertical plane or line from which all measurements are taken.

questions.questionText:
Given the following information at take-off:
The following table:
ITEM MASS (kg) ARM (cm) MOMENT (kgcm)
Basic Empty Condition 12045 +30 +361 350
Crew 145 -160 -23 200
Freight 1 570 +200 +114000
Freight 2 410 -40 -16400
Fuel 6045 -8 -48360
Flight time: 2 hrs
Estimated fuel flow: 1050 l/hr
Fuel density: 0.79 kg/l
The "Freight 2" will be dropped during flight within the scope of a rescue action. Calculate the CG position at landing.
What is the DOI?
options: The index for the position of the centre of pressure at any mass.
The index for the position of the centre of gravity at operating mass.
The index for the position of the centre of gravity at dry operating mass.
The index for the position of the centre of gravity when dropping cargo during flight.

questions.questionText:
Given the following information, calculate the loaded centre of gravity (CG).
The following table:
STATION MASS (kg) ARM (cm) MOMENT (kgcm)
Basic Empty Mass 12045 +30 +361 350
Crew 145 -160 -23 200
Freight 1 5455 +200 +1091 000
Freight 2 410 -40 -16400
Fuel 6045 -8 -48 360
Additional equipment for the mission 124 +40 +4960
How can you calculate the DOI?
The fuel index:
options: is only used for aeroplanes with wing tip tanks.
is the difference between the zero fuel mass index and the DOI.
is a standard value given by EASA and can be used for different types of aircraft.
is used to calculate the correct position of the CG due to different locations of the fuel tanks.

questions.questionText:

Fuel Index

They are removed from the load and trim sheet (standard mass of 84 kg per passenger and 14 kg per bag). The baggage was in Hold 4 and the passengers were to be seated in Compartment Oc. Which statement is correct after these changes have been accounted for?

Cargo Zone A: 350 lbs
Baggage Zone B: 35 lbs
Pilot and front seat passenger. 300 lbs (total)

For the purpose of calculating Traffic Loads, an operators Loading Manual gives the following standard mass values for passengers (these values include an allowance for hand baggage):
Male: 88 kg
Female: 70 kg
Child: 35 kg
Infant; 6 kg
The standard mass value for hold baggage is 14 kg/pcs.

Passengers loaded: 40 males, 65 females, 8 children, 5 infants
Baggage in hold number 4: 120 pieces
Using the standard mass values given and the data in the appendix, select from the following the correct value for the mass of freight (all loaded in hold No. 1) which constitutes the remainder of the Traffic Load:
options: no cargo can be loaded in hold number 1.

questions.questionText:
Refer to figure 031-10
With respect to multi-engine piston powered aeroplane, determine the Ramp Mass in the following conditions:
Basic Empty Mass: 3210 lbs
Basic arm: 88.5 inches
One pilot: 160 lbs
Front seat passenger. 200 lbs
Centre seat passengers: 290 lbs
One passenger rear seat: 110 lbs
Baggage in zone 1: 100 lbs
Baggage in zone 4: 50 lbs
Block Fuel: 100 US Gal
Trip Fuel: 55 US Gal
Fuel for start up and taxi (included in Block Fuel): 3 US Gal
Fuel density. 6 lbs/US Gal

Refer to figure 031-32
An aeroplane is carrying a Traffic Load of 10320 kg. Complete the necessary sections of the attached appendix and determine which of the answers given below represents the maximum increase in the Traffic Load:
Once the mass and balance documentation has been signed prior to flight:
options: The documentation is not signed prior to flight.
Acceptable last minute changes to the load must be documented.
Documented last minute changes to the load may be incorporated.

questions.questionText:
Refer to figure 031-34
The aft centre of gravity limit is located at 2.97 m. The datum is located in front of the aircraft. Is it allowed to fly under otherwise identical conditions if a pilot is weighing 10 kg less as given?
options: Yes, the new centre of gravity is located 1 cm forward of the aft limit, thus still within the allowable range.
Yes, the reduced mass can only have a positive effect
No, the new centre of gravity is located 1 cm aft of the aft limit, thus outside the allowable range.
Yes, the new centre of gravity is now located more forward within the allowable range.

questions.questionText:

Passengers embarked at row 7: 3 females
Passengers embarked at row 7: 2 males
Baggage loaded in compartment 5: 5 pieces

Given:
The following table:
Item Index Mass

Find the approximate CG position as % MAC for the take-off:

Basic Empty Mass: 3210 lbs
Basic arm: 88.5 in
One pilot: 160 lbs
Front seat passenger 200 lbs

Baggage in zone 1: 100 lbs
Baggage in zone 4: 50 lbs
Zero Fuel Mass: 4120 lbs
Moment at Zero Fuel Mass: 377751 lbs.in

Trip Fuel: 55 US Gal

Just prior to departure, you accept 10 passengers additional on board who will be seated in compartment OC and you have 750 kg unloaded from cargo compartment 5. The take-off centre of gravity in MAC % (Mean Aerodynamic Chord) will be located at:

With respect to multi-engine piston powered aeroplane, determine the Block Fuel moment (Ibs.in) in the following conditions:
Basic Empty Mass: 3210 lbs, Basic arm: 88.5 in
One pilot: 160 lbs, Front seat passenger: 200 lbs
Centre seat passengers: 290 lbs (total)
One passenger rear seat: 110 lbs
Baggage in zone 1: 100 lbs, Baggage in zone 4: 50 lbs
Block Fuel: 100 US Gal, Trip Fuel: 55 US Gal
Fuel for start up and taxi (included in Block Fuel): 3 US Gal
Fuel density: 6 lbs/US Gal

Using the data given in the load and trim sheet, determine from the following the correct values for the Take-off Mass and the position of the centre of gravity at that mass if the fuel index correction to be applied is given as -0.9.

The empty mass of your aircraft is 1 100 kg with a CG-position at 3.05 m.
The load is as follows:
Total Mass of pilot and co-pilot: 150 kg
Total Mass of passengers at rear: 200 kg
In order not to exceed the limitations the minimum remaining fuel on board should be:

95.3 in aft of datum and the moment is 380373 in.lb
113.6 in aft of datum and the moment is 430 998 in.lb
115.7 in aft of datum and the moment is 365074 in.lb

questions.questionText: Calculate the basic empty mass (BEM) by using the attached loading manifest:

Without the crew, the weight and the CG position of the aircraft are 7000 kg and 4.70 m.
The mass of the pilot: 90 kg
The mass of the copilot: 75 kg
The mass of the flight engineer: 90 kg
With this crew on board, the CG position of the aircraft will be:
From the data contained in the attached appendix, the maximum Allowable Take-off Mass and Traffic Load are respectively:
Using the data given at the appendix, determine which of the following correctly gives the values of the Zero Fuel Mass (ZFM) of the aeroplane and the load index at ZFM:

With respect to a multi-engine piston powered aeroplane, determine the total moment (lbs/in) at landing in the following conditions:
Basic Empty Mass: 3210 lbs
One pilot: 160 lbs
Front seat passenger: 200 lbs
Centre seat passengers: 290 lbs (total)
One passenger rear seat: 110 lbs
Baggage in zone 1: 100 lbs
Baggage in zone 4: 50 lbs
Block Fuel: 100 US Gal
Trip Fuel: 55 US Gal
Fuel for start up and taxi (included in Block Fuel): 3 US Gal
Fuel density: 6 lbs/US Gal
Total moment at take-off: 432226 lbs.in

Without the crew, the mass and longitudinal CG position of the aircraft are 6000 kg and 4.70 m.
The mass of the pilot: 90 kg
The mass of the copilot: 100 kg
The mass of the flight engineer: 80 kg
With the crew, the mass and longitudinal CG position of the aircraft are:

From the data given at the appendix and assuming a fuel index shift of -5.7 from the ZFM loaded index, determine which of the following, is the correct value (percentage MAC) for the position of the centre of gravity at Take-off Mass:
Using the load and trim sheet, which of the following is the correct value for the index at a Dry Operating Mass (DOM) of 35000 kg with a CG at 14% MAC?

Contrary to the load sheet forecasts you have:
empty Cargo compartment 1: 1000 kg
Cargo compartment 2: 3000 kg
Cargo 3 compartment: 2000 kg
Cargo compartment 4: 1000 kg
Cargo compartment 5: 20
Passengers in compartment OA: 20
Passengers in compartment OB: 30
Passengers in compartment OC:
The take-off centre of gravity in MAC % (Mean Aerodynamic Chord), will be located at:

Length of the Mean Aerodynamic Chord: 1 m
Moment Arm of the forward cargo: -0.50 m
Moment Arm of the aft cargo: +2.50 m
Aircraft mass: 2200 kg
Centre of gravity: 25% MAC
To move the centre of gravity to 40%, which mass has to be transferred from the forward to the aft cargo hold?

The planned Take-off Mass of a turbojet aeroplane is 180000 kg, with its centre of gravity located at 26% MAC (Mean Aerodynamic Chord).
Shortly prior to engine start, the local staff informs the flight crew that 4 000 kg must be unloaded from cargo 4 (23.69 m aft of reference point).
Given:
Distance from reference point to leading edge: 14 m
Length of MAC: 4.6 m
After the handling operation, the new centre of gravity location in % MAC will be?

An aircraft has a mass of 5000 lbs and the CG is located at 80 in aft of the datum. The aft CG limit is at 80.5 in aft of the datum. What is the maximum mass that can be loaded into a hold situated 150 in aft of the datum without exceeding the limit?

An aeroplane, whose specific data is shown in the annex, has a planned Take-off Mass of 200 000 kg, with its centre of gravity (CG) is located at 15.38 m rearward of the reference point, representing a CG location at 30% MAC (Mean Aerodynamic Chord). For performance purposes, the captain decides to reset the value of the centre of gravity location to 35% MAC. The front and rear cargo compartments are located at a distance of 15 m and 25 m from the reference point respectively, the cargo load mass which needs to be transferred from the front to the rear cargo compartment is:
options: It is not possible to establish the required centre of gravity location.

questions.questionText: The mass of an aeroplane is 1 950 kg. If 450 kg is added to a cargo hold 1.75 metres from the loaded centre of gravity (CG). The loaded CG will move:

The Total Mass of an aeroplane is 9000 kg. The centre of gravity (CG) position is at 2.0 m from the datum line. The aft limit for CG is at 2.1 m from the datum line. What mass of cargo must be shifted from the front cargo hold (at 0.8 m from the datum) to the aft hold (at 3.8 m), to move the CG to the aft limit?

Given:
Total Mass: 7500 kg
Centre of gravity (CG) location station: 80.5
Aft CG limit station: 79.5
How much cargo must be shifted from the aft cargo compartment at station 150 to the forward cargo compartment at station 30 in order to move the CG location to the aft limit?

Given that the Total Mass of an aeroplane is 112000 kg with a centre of gravity position at 22.62 m aft of the datum. The centre of gravity limits are between 18 m and 22 m. How much mass must be removed from the rear hold (30 m aft of the datum) to move the centre of gravity to the middle of the limits:
The loaded mass of an aircraft is 2000 kg. What is the movement of the CG if additional 400 kg of cargo is loaded to a hold 2 m aft of the present CG position?

Given:
Aeroplane mass = 36 000 kg
Centre of gravity (CG) is located at station 17 m
What is the effect on CG location if you move 20 passengers (Total Mass = 1 600 kg) from station 16 to station 23?

Given:
Total Mass: 2900 kg
Centre of gravity (CG) location: 115.0
Aft CG limit station: 116.0
The maximum mass that can be added at station 130.0 is:

The Total Mass of an aeroplane is 145000 kg and the centre of gravity limits are between 4.7 m and 6.9 m aft of the datum. The loaded centre of gravity position is 4.4 m aft. How much mass must be transferred from the front to the rear hold in order to bring the out of limit centre of gravity position to the foremost limit?

A jet aeroplane, with the geometrical characteristics shown in the appendix, has a take-off weight (W) of 460.000 N and a centre of gravity (point G on annex) located at 15.40 m from the zero reference point. At the last moment the station manager has 12000 N of freight added in the forward compartment at 10 m from the zero reference point. The final location of the centre of gravity, calculated in percentage of Mean Aerodynamic Chord AB (from point A), is equal to:

An aeroplane, whose specific data is shown in the annex, has a planned Take-off Mass of 200 000 kg, with its centre of gravity (CG) located at 15.38 m rearward of the reference point, representing a CG location at 30% MAC (Mean Aerodynamic Chord). The current cargo load distribution is:
Front cargo: 6500 kg
Rear cargo: 4000 kg
For performance purposes, the captain decides to reset the value of the centre of gravity location to 33% MAC. The front and rear cargo compartments are located at a distance of 15 m and 25 m from the reference point respectively. After the transfer operation, the new cargo load distribution is:
options: front cargo: 3740 kg; rear cargo: 6760 kg.
front cargo: 9 260 kg; rear cargo: 1 240 kg.
front cargo: 4550 kg; rear cargo: 5 950 kg.
front cargo: 6760 kg; rear cargo: 3740 kg.

questions.questionText:
Assume.
Aeroplane gross mass: 4750 kg
Centre of gravity at station: 115.8
If 100 kg is moved from station 30 to station 120 the new position of the centre of gravity will be at station:

Given:
Actual mass: 116500 lbs
Original CG station: 435.0
Compartment A station: 285.5
Compartment B station: 792.5
If 390 lbs of cargo is moved from compartment B (aft) to compartment A (forward), what is the station number of the new CG?
Which of the listed types of cargo can most likely be found in a bulk cargo compartment?
Passenger baggage of an aircraft is usually stored:
options: on the aircraft floor and secured with nets.

questions.questionText: Imagine some large transport aircraft (e.g. Airbus A340, A380 or Boeing 747) which is flying from London to New York. The passengers personal belongings should be placed:
options: on cargo pallets, safely tied by nets.
as baggage in special cargo containers.
underneath as bulk cargo, in order to minimize the space required.
overhead the occupant's seats compartments, so they are easily accessible during flight.

questions.questionText: Which of the following statements about loading is correct?
options: Live animals are never loaded as bulk cargo.
Bulk cargo loading requires much less ground personal than the loading of containers.
Containers can be connected directly to the aircraft and each container has its own manifest.
Pallets are mainly used on small aircraft and are strapped down.

questions.questionText: Which statement about palletised cargo is true?
options: It consists of individual or multiple items stored on pallets and secured with nets and/or straps.
It can be loaded without any specific loading equipment.
It comprises passenger baggage on pallets which is stored in cargo holds.
It is not used anymore because of the lack of protection.

questions.questionText: The basic advantage of standard size containers:
options: Standard size containers are designed to fit and lock into the cargo compartment for maximum space efficiency.
With the cargo loaded in containers one does not have to observe the maximum floor loading limit.
Maximum mass of cargo loaded in the containers is always limited by the MTOM.
Maximum mass of cargo loaded in the containers is often limited by the G-forces in flight.

questions.questionText: Rush bags, last minute baggage and cabin crew baggage is held in/on:
The floor limit of an aircraft cargo hold is 5000 N/m². It is planned to load-up a cubic container measuring 0.4 m of side. It's maximum gross mass must not exceed (assume g=10 m/s²)

Given:
Pallet: 1.2 x 1.2 m
Max. running load: 1 915 lb/m
Find the maximum allowed mass which can be loaded on the pallet without exceeding the maximum running load.
The structural limitation of a baggage compartment is 50 lb/ft² A load has a mass of 320 kg and has to be distributed over an area of at least:

The maximum floor loading for a cargo compartment in an aeroplane is given as 750 kg per square metre. A package with a mass of 600 kg is to be loaded. Assuming the pallet base is entirely in contact with the floor, which of the following is the minimum size pallet that can be used?

The maximum load per running metre of an aeroplane is 350 kg/m. The width of the floor area is 2 metres. The floor strength limitation is 300 kg per square metre. Which one of the following crates can be loaded directly on the floor?
options: A load of 700 kg in a crate with dimensions 1.8 m x 1.4 m x 0.8 m.
A load of 500 kg in a crate with dimensions 1.5 m x 1 m x 1 m.
A load of 400 kg in a crate with dimensions 1.4 m x 0.8 m x 0.8 m.
A load of 400 kg in a crate with dimensions 1.2 m x 1.2 m x 1.2 m

questions.questionText: A pallet having a freight platform which measures 200 cm x 250 cm has a Total Mass of 300 kg. The pallet is carried on two ground supports each measuring 20 cm x 200 cm. Using the Loading Manual for the transport aeroplane, calculate how much mass may be added to, or must be off loaded from, the pallet in order for the load intensity to match the maximum permitted distribution load intensity for lower deck forward cargo compartment.

The maximum intensity floor loading for an aeroplane is given in the Flight Manual as 650 kg per square metre. What is the maximum mass of a package which can be safely supported on a pallet with dimensions of 80 cm by 80 cm?
Loads must be adequately secured in order to:
options: avoid any centre of gravity (CG) movement during flight.
prevent excessive g-loading during the landing flare.
avoid unplanned centre of gravity (CG) movement and aircraft damage.

questions.questionText: An aeroplane operating under the 180 minutes ETOPS rule may be up to:
options: 180 minutes flying time to a suitable airport under the prevailing weather condition with one engine inoperative.
180 minutes flying time from suitable airport in still air with both engines operating at a normal cruising speed.
180 minutes flying time to a suitable airport in still air with one engine inoperative
60 minutes flying time in still air with both engines operating at the normal cruising speed.

questions.questionText: Which of the jet engine ratings below is not a certified rating?
Which force compensates the weight in unaccelerated straight and level flight?
options: The resultant from lift and drag.

questions.questionText: Which statement is correct for a descent without engine thrust at maximum lift to drag ratio speed?
options: A headwind component increases the ground distance.
A tailwind component decreases the ground distance.
A tailwind component increases fuel and time to descent.
A tailwind component increases the ground distance.

questions.questionText: What happens to thrust required and drag if you fly below VMD?
options: Thrust required increases and drag increases.
Thrust required increases and drag decreases.
Thrust required decreases and drag increases.
Thrust required decreases and drag decreases.

questions.questionText: A decrease in atmospheric pressure has, among other things, the following consequences on take-off performance:
options: a reduced take-off distance and degraded initial climb performance.
a reduced take-off distance and improved initial climb performance.
an increased take-off distance and degraded initial climb performance.
an increased take-off distance and improved initial climb performance.

questions.questionText: The pilot of a single engine aircraft has established the climb performance. The carriage of additional pieces of baggage will cause the climb performance:
options: unchanged, if a short field take-off is adopted.

questions.questionText: Which of the equations below expresses approximately the unaccelerated percentage climb gradient for small climb angles?
The effect that an increased outside air temperature has on the climb performance of an aeroplane is that it:
options: reduces both the climb gradient and the rate of climb.
does not affect the climb performance.
increases the climb gradient and decreases the rate of climb.
reduces the climb gradient and increases the rate of climb.

questions.questionText: In straight horizontal steady flight, at speeds below that for minimum drag:
options: the aeroplane cannot be controlled manually.
a higher speed, but still below that for minimum drag, requires a higher thrust.
a lower speed requires a higher thrust.
the aeroplane can be controlled only in level flight.

questions.questionText: The point where Drag coefficient / Lift coefficient is a minimum is:
options: on the "back side" of the drag curve.
the point where a tangent from the origin touches the drag curve.
the lowest point of the drag curve.

questions.questionText: What is the minimum width of a clearway?

Consider an airplane with four engines. One engine failed. Calculate the climb gradient with the given values: Mass: 358 000 kg, Drag: 455 000 N, Thrust: 245 000 N (each engine) (Consider acceleration of gravity = 10 m/s^2)
The maximum operating altitude for a certain aeroplane with a pressurised cabin:
options: is only certified for four-engine aeroplanes.
is the highest pressure altitude certified for normal operation.

questions.questionText: For a turbojet aircraft, what is the speed associated with best endurance?
During a descent a headwind will:
options: increase the angle of the descent flight path.
increase the descent distance over ground.

questions.questionText: Which of the following statements is correct?
options: An underrun is an area beyond the runway end which can be used for an aborted take-off.
A clearway is an area beyond the runway which can be used for an aborted take-off.
If a clearway or a stopway is used, the liftoff point must be attainable at least at the end of the permanent runway surface.
A stopway means an area beyond the take-off runway, able to support the aeroplane during an aborted take-off.

questions.questionText: The absolute ceiling:
options: can be reached only with minimum steady flight speed.
is the altitude at which the best climb gradient attainable is 5%.
is the altitude at which the maximum rate of climb is zero.
is the altitude at which the aeroplane reaches a maximum rate of climb of 100 ft/min.

questions.questionText: The load factor in a turn in level flight with constant TAS depends on:
options: the radius of the turn and the bank angle.
the radius of the turn and the weight of the aeroplane.
the true airspeed and the bank angle.

questions.questionText: The tangent from the origin to the thrust required/drag against true airspeed curve, for a jet aeroplane, shows:
options: the speed for minimum thrust required

questions.questionText: At a higher gross mass on a piston-engined aeroplane, in order to maintain a given angle of attack, configuration and altitude:
options: the airspeed must be increased and the drag will remain unchanged.
the airspeed must be decreased and the drag will increase.
the lift/drag ratio must be increased.
the airspeed must be increased and the drag will also increase.

questions.questionText: With regard to an unaccelerated horizontal flight, which of the following statements concerning Minimum Drag is correct?
options: it is independant of the aircraft mass.
it is a function of the pressure altitude.
it is proportional to the aircraft mass.
it is a function of the density altitude.

questions.questionText: What is true about a clearway?
options: A clearway has a minimum width of 90 m.
A clearway must have an imaginary gradient of 1.5%.
A clearway can be water or land and must be under the control of the aerodrome authority.
A clearway can be used to bring the aircraft to a full stop after an engine failure.

questions.questionText: Explain the difference, if any, between the effect of a tailwind on the flight path angle and the effect of a tailwind on the descent angle.
options: A tailwind will decrease the flight path angle and decrease the descent angle.
A tailwind will decrease the flight path angle and the descent angle will remain constant.
A tailwind will increase the flight path angle and decrease the descent angle.
In a tailwind, the flight path angle will remain constant and the descent angle will remain constant.

questions.questionText: If the aerodrome pressure altitude increases it will:
What happens to the performance of an aircraft if the QNH is low?
options: The engine thrust decreases due to a lower air density.
The take-off performance increases due to higher engine thrust.
The landing performance decreases and the go-around performance increases.

questions.questionText: An increase in atmospheric pressure has, among other things, the following consequences on take-off performance:
options: an increased take-off distance and degraded initial climb performance.
a reduced take-off distance and degraded initial climb performance.
an increased take-off distance and improved initial climb performance.
a reduced take-off distance and improved initial climb performance.

questions.questionText: The length of a clearway may be included in:
Refer to the attached figure 032-41 and choose a correct statement below:
options: Point P. maximum rate of descent
Point C: maximum angle of climb
Point D. the aircraft would decelerate if it would fly straight and level
The speed at point C would result in a greater angle of descent than at point B.

questions.questionText: A higher outside air temperature:
options: reduces the angle and the rate of climb.
reduces the angle of climb but increases the rate of climb.
does not have any noticeable effect on climb performance.
increases the angle of climb but decreases the rate of climb.

questions.questionText: What is correct regarding a clearway?
options: An area centrally located about the extended centreline of the runway, but need not be under the control of the airport authorities.
Threshold lights may protrude above the clearway plane if their height above the end of the runway is 0.66 m (26 in) or less, independent of their location.
An area beyond the runway not less than 152 m wide under the control of airport authorities.
An area beyond the runway with an upward slope not exceeding 1.5%.

questions.questionText: For a piston engine aeroplane, the speed for maximum range is:
options: 1.4 times the stall speed in clean configuration.
that which gives the maximum lift to drag ratio.
that which gives the maximum value of lift
that which gives the minimum value of induced drag.

questions.questionText: Explain the difference, if any, between climb/descent angle and flight-path angle:
options: Climb/descent angle is ground related and flight-path angle is air mass related.
Climb/descent angle is ground related and flight-path angle is ground related.
Climb/descent angle is air mass related and flight-path angle is ground related.
Climb/descent angle is air mass related and flight-path angle is air mass related.

questions.questionText: To achieve the maximum range over ground with headwind the airspeed should be:
options: higher compared to the speed for maximum range cruise with no wind.
reduced to the gust penetration speed.
lower compared to the speed for maximum range cruise with no wind.
equal to the speed for maximum range cruise with no wind.

questions.questionText: During a climb with all piston engines operating, the altitude where the rate of climb reduces to 100 ft/min is called:
On a dry runway the accelerate stop distance is increased:
options: by a lower Take-off Mass because the aeroplane accelerates faster to V1.

questions.questionText: Which of the following combinations adversely affects take-off and initial climb performance?
options: High temperature and low relative humidity.
Low temperature and low relative humidity.
Low temperature and high relative humidity.
High temperature and high relative humidity.

questions.questionText: An aeroplane is in a power off glide at best gliding speed. If the pilot decreases the pitch attitude the glide distance:
options: may increase or decrease depending on the aeroplane.

questions.questionText: Besides lift, the forces that determine the gradient of climb of an aeroplane are:
With all engines out, a pilot wants to fly for maximum time. Therefore he has to fly the speed corresponding to:
If the thrust available exceeds the thrust required for level flight:
options: the aeroplane decelerates if the altitude is maintained.
the aeroplane descends if the airspeed is maintained.
the aeroplane accelerates if the altitude is maintained.
the aeroplane decelerates if it is in the region of reversed command.

questions.questionText: Which of the following three speeds of a jet aeroplane are basically identical? The speeds for:
options: maximum climb angle, minimum glide angle and maximum range.
holding, maximum climb angle and minimum glide angle.
maximum range, minimum drag and minimum glide angle.
maximum drag, maximum endurance and maximum climb angle.

questions.questionText: Two identical aeroplanes at different masses are descending at zero wind and zero thrust. Which of the following statements correctly describes their descent characteristics?
options: At a given angle of attack the lighter aeroplane will always glide further than the heavier aeroplane.
There is no difference between the descent characteristics of the two aeroplanes.
At a given angle of attack the heavier aeroplane will always glide further than the lighter aeroplane.
At a given angle of attack, both the vertical and the forward speed are greater for the heavier aeroplane.

questions.questionText: The maximum rate of climb that can be maintained at the absolute ceiling is:
What is the meaning of specific fuel consumption?
options: Fuel flow per unit of thrust.

questions.questionText: The take-off distance available is:
options: the runway length plus half of the clearway.
the total runway length, without clearway even if this one exists.
the length of the take-off run available plus the length of the clearway available.

questions.questionText: In unaccelerated climb:
options: lift is greater than the gross weight.
thrust equals drag plus the downhill component of the gross weight in the flight path direction.
lift equals weight plus the vertical component of the drag.
thrust equals drag plus the uphill component of the gross weight in the flight path direction.

questions.questionText: Which of the following diagrams correctly shows the movement of the power required curve with increasing altitude (H1 < H2)?

How do the best angle of climb and best rate of climb vary with increasing altitude for an aeroplane with a normal aspirated piston engine?
options: Best angle of climb increases while best rate of climb decreases.
Best angle of climb decreases while best rate of climb increases.

questions.questionText: Density altitude is the:
options: pressure altitude corrected for 'non standard temperature.
altitude read directly from the altimeter.
altitude reference to the standard datum plane.

questions.questionText: Uphill slope:
options: increases the take-off distance more than the accelerate stop distance.
decreases the accelerate stop distance only.

questions.questionText: Assuming other factors remain constant and not limiting, increasing the aerodrome pressure altitude:

will cause the maximum permitted take-off mass to decrease.
will cause the maximum permitted take-off mass to increase.

questions.questionText: The specific range (jet airplane) is
options: the fuel flow divided by TAS.
the fuel flow per unit of thrust.
the number of miles per unit mass of fuel.
nautical air miles flown divided by the specific fuel consumption.

On a reciprocating engined aeroplane, to maintain a given angle of attack, configuration and altitude at higher gross mass
options: the airspeed and the drag will be increased.
the lift/drag ratio must be increased.
the airspeed will be increased but the drag does not change.
the airspeed will be decreased and the drag increased.

questions.questionText: What is going to happen with the take-off distance required and the accelerate stop distance required if you compare a runway with an upslope to a runway with no slope? For a runway with an upslope:
options: the take-off distance required increases and the accelerate stop distance required increases.
the take-off distance required increases and the accelerate stop distance required decreases.
the take-off distance required decreases and the accelerate stop distance required decreases.
the take-off distance required decreases and the accelerate stop distance required increases.

options: same as that for clean configuration.
higher than that for clean configuration.
changed so that Vx increases and Vy decreases compared to clean configuration.
lower than that for clean configuration.

options: In a climb with constant IAS.
In level flight with constant IAS.
In a descent with constant TAS.

questions.questionText: The speed for maximum lift/drag ratio will result in:
options: the maximum range for a jet aeroplane
the maximum angle of climb for a propeller driven aeroplane.
the maximum endurance for a propeller driven aeroplane
the maximum range for a propeller driven aeroplane.

An uphill slope:

options: requires an increase in power and decrease in the airspeed.
an increase in airspeed is required but power setting does not change.
a higher coefficient of drag is required.
an increase in airspeed and power is required.

questions.questionText: What is the effect of increased mass on the performance of a gliding aeroplane?
options: The speed for best angle of descent increases.

questions.questionText: Any acceleration in climb, with a constant power setting:
options: decreases rate of climb and increases angle of climb.
improves the rate of climb if the airspeed is below Vy.
decreases the rate of climb and the angle of climb.
improves the climb gradient if the airspeed is below Vx.

options: the minimum drag decreases and the IAS for minimum drag increases.
the minimum drag increases and the IAS for minimum drag increases.
the minimum drag decreases and the IAS for minimum drag decreases.
the minimum drag increases and the IAS for minimum drag decreases.

options: 1.4 times the stall speed in clean configuration.
that which gives the minimum value of drag.
that which gives the minimum lift to drag ratio.
that which gives the maximum value of lift.

options: Yes, but the stopway must be able to carry the weight of the aeroplane.
Yes, but the stopway must have the same width as the runway.
No, unless its centreline is on the extended centreline of the runway.

options: With decreasing density altitude, take-off distance will decrease and landing distance will decrease.
With increasing density, take-off distance will decrease and landing distance will increase.
With increasing density, take-off distance will increase and landing distance will increase.
With increasing density altitude, take-off distance will decrease and landing distance will decrease.

The higher the gross mass the greater is the speed for descent.

is the point where the Lift to Drag ratio is a maximum.

options: decreases with increasing altitude, but only at low speeds.

questions.questionText: What is the effect of tailwind on the time to climb to a given altitude?
options: The time to climb does not change.
The effect on time to climb will depend on the aeroplane type.

questions.questionText: An increase in atmospheric pressure has, among other things, the following consequences on landing performance
options: a reduced landing distance and degraded go-around performance.
an increased landing distance and degraded go-around performance.
an increased landing distance and improved go-around performance.
a reduced landing distance and improved go-around performance.

questions.questionText: The stopway is an area which allows an increase only in the
Maximum endurance for a piston engined aeroplane is achieved at:
options: the speed that approximately corresponds to the maximum rate of climb speed.
the speed that approximately corresponds to the maximum climb angle speed.
the speed for maximum lift coefficient.

questions.questionText: The rate of climb is approximately equal to:
options: the still-air gradient multiplied by the TAS.
the angle of climb divided by the TAS.
the angle of climb multiplied by the TAS.
the still-air gradient divided by the TAS.

questions.questionText: Considering TAS for maximum range and maximum endurance, other factors remaining constant:
options: TAS for maximum range will increase with increased altitude while TAS for maximum endurance will decrease with increased altitude
both will decrease with increasing altitude.
both will increase with increasing altitude.
both will stay constant regardless of altitude.

questions.questionText: The angle of climb with flaps extended, compared to that with flaps retracted, will normally be:
options: increase at moderate flap setting, decrease at large flap setting.

questions.questionText: The maximum horizontal speed occurs when:
options: the maximum thrust is equal to the total drag.
the thrust is equal to minimum drag.
the thrust is equal to the maximum drag.
the thrust does not increase further with increasing speed.

questions.questionText: The best rate of climb at a constant gross mass:
options: increases with increasing altitude due to the higher true airspeed.
decreases with increasing altitude since the thrust available decreases due to the lower air density.
increases with increasing altitude since the drag decreases due to the lower air density.

questions.questionText: How does the lift coefficient for maximum range vary with altitude? (No compressibility effects.)
options: The lift coefficient is independent of altitude
Only at low speeds the lift coefficient decreases with increasing altitude.
The lift coefficient decreases with increasing altitude.
The lift coefficient increases with increasing altitude.

options: a lower airspeed requires more thrust.
a thrust reduction results in an acceleration of the aeroplane.
a lower airspeed requires less thrust because drag is decreased.
the thrust required is independent of the airspeed.

questions.questionText: Which of the following factors leads to the maximum flight time of a glide?

options: the increase of altitude to horizontal air distance expressed as a percentage.
rate of climb to true airspeed.
the increase of altitude to distance over ground expressed as a percentage.

options: The speed for minimum thrust required is used to fly the long range cruise regime.
The speed for minimum thrust required is used to climb with the best angle of climb.
The speed for maximum thrust required is used to obtain a maximum range in a descent.
The speed for maximum thrust required is used to climb with the best rate of climb.

questions.questionText: What is the flight path angle?
options: The angle of the flight path of an aircraft and true north.
The angle of the flight path of an aircraft and local vertical.
The angle of the flight path of an aircraft and the angle of attack.
The angle of the flight path of an aircraft and the ground.

questions.questionText: In which of the following distances can the length of a stopway be included?
options: In the accelerate stop distance available.

options: remains unchanged but the TAS increases.
increases and the TAS increases by the same percentage.
decreases slightly because of the lower air density.

questions.questionText: Which of the equations below defines specific range (SR)?
options: SR = Indicated Airspeed / Total Fuel Flow.
SR = Mach Number / Total Fuel Flow
SR = True Airspeed / Total Fuel Flow.
SR = Ground speed / Total Fuel Flow.

is used to establish minimum clearance of 2000 feet over mountains.
is used to calculate the FL above the Transition Altitude.

questions.questionText: A lower airspeed at constant mass and altitude requires
options: more thrust and a lower coefficient of drag
more thrust and a lower coefficient of lift.
less thrust and a lower coefficient of lift.

If there is a stopway or a clearway and the runway is wet, the TODx1.15 shall not exceed the TORA
If there is a stopway or a clearway, the ASDA shall not exceed the TORA.
If there is no stopway or clearway, the TODx1.25 shall not exceed the TORA.

decreases angle and rate of climb.
has no effect on rate of climb

options: The value of the gradient is always higher than the value of the angle
The variation of the angle is represented by the gradient.
The gradient considers wind, the angle does not.
The gradient is based on mass, the angle is not.

decreases the ground distance flown during that climb

is always lower than the minimum drag speed.

questions.questionText: The induced drag of an aeroplane at constant mass in un-accelerated level flight is greatest at

It will increase the take-off ground run.
It will increase the accelerate stop distance.

options: An increase will cause a decrease in take-off distance required.
A decrease will cause an increase of the climb gradient
An increase will cause a decrease in the landing distance required.
A decrease will cause an increase in the take-off ground run.

Take-off ground roll distance is reduced and climb gradient is higher.

Aeroplane Mass: 3400 lbs
Tailwind component: 10 kts
Flaps: Approach
Runway: Tarred and dry
VLO is defined as:
options: minimum possible speed that the aircraft could lift off the ground.
the maximum speed for landing gear operation.
actual speed that the aircraft lifts off the ground.

questions.questionText: Consider the power required of a performance class B aeroplane with two piston engines during an engine failure to the power required with both engines operative. If the critical engine fails and the pilot wants to maintain level flight:
options: the power required is higher because the total drag has increased.
the power required has doubled because the aircraft lost 50% of its performance.
the power required remains the same if the pilot is able to counteract the adverse yaw.
the power required is higher because the total drag has decreased.

questions.questionText: The critical engine inoperative:
options: decreases the power required and the total drag due to the reduced drag of the windmilling engine and the compensation of the yaw moment.
increases the power required and the total drag due to the additional drag of the windmilling engine and the compensation of the yaw moment.
increases the power required and decreases the total drag due to the windmilling engine.
does not affect the aeroplane performance since it is independent of the power plant.

questions.questionText: Vx is defined as:
options: the speed for best specific range.
the speed for best rate of climb.
the speed for best angle of flight path.
the speed for best angle of climb.

questions.questionText: As long as an aeroplane is in a steady climb:
options: Vx may be greater or less than Vy depending on altitude.
Vx is always less than Vy.
Vy is always greater than VMO
Vx is always greater than Vy.

questions.questionText: What happens to Vx and Vy on a multi-engine airplane after engine failure?
The speed for best rate of climb is called:
Refer to the critical engine of a propeller-driven airplane with two engines. If this engine fails, the most unfavourable effect is related to the:
The speed for maximum rate of climb:
options: may be higher or lower than the speed for best angle of climb depending on aircraft type.
will be the same as the speed for best angle of climb under any circumstances.
lower than the speed for the best angle of climb.
will be higher than the speed for best angle of climb.

questions.questionText: Typically the CL/CD ratio is:
options: maximum at an angle of attack of 4°.
maximum at an angle of attack of 16°.
minimum at an angle of attack of 4°.
minimum at an angle of attack of 16°.

questions.questionText: Consider a graph showing the rate of climb on the vertical axis and the speed on the horizontal axis.
options: A tangent which is drawn from the origin will intercept the graph on a specific location which shows Vy and the peak of the graph shows Vx.
A tangent which is drawn from the origin will intercept the graph on a specific location which shows Vy
The peak of the graph shows Vx.
A tangent which is drawn from the origin will intercept the graph on a specific location which shows Vx and the peak of the graph shows Vy

questions.questionText: Which is the critical engine in case of an aeroplane with two piston engines (clockwise rotating propellers) and an engine failure during take-off?
options: The left engine during ground roll, the right engine when airborne.

questions.questionText: Which of the following provides maximum obstacle clearance during climb?
options: The speed for maximum climb angle Vx.
The speed, at which the flaps may be selected one position further UP.
The speed for maximum rate of climb.

questions.questionText: Consider the drag of a performance class B aeroplane with two piston engines during an engine failure to the drag with both engines operative. If the critical engine fails:
options: drag decreases because induced drag decreases since the airplane has to fly at a higher speed to maintain controllability.
drag remains constant as long as the propeller of the critical engine is in feathered position and does not rotate.
drag increases because the pilot has to increase rudder input.
drag increases because induced drag decreases with a factor of 1 to 5 because slipstream is lost.

questions.questionText: How does the thrust of a fixed-pitch propeller vary during take-off run, assuming unstalled flow conditions at the propeller blades? The thrust:
options: increases while the aeroplane speed builds up.
has no change during take-off and climb.
decreases while the aeroplane speed builds up.

questions.questionText: Compare an approach and landing of an airplane with full flaps setting to zero flaps setting. An approach and landing with full flaps setting will result in:
options: a slower speed during the approach and a shorter ground-roll distance.
a higher speed during the approach and a shorter ground-roll distance.
a slower speed during the approach and a longer ground-roll distance.
a higher speed during the approach and a longer ground-roll distance.

questions.questionText: What percentages of the headwind and tailwind components are taken into account when calculating the take-off field length required?
Assume a normal approach and landing with a class B airplane on a wet runway. Which of the following values shows the corresponding screen height?
The flight manual of a light twin engine recommends two cruise power settings: 65% and 75%. The 75% power setting in relation to the 65% results in:
options: same speed and an increase of the fuel-burn per hour and fuel-burn/distance.
same speed and fuel-burn/distance, but an increase in the fuel-burn per hour.
an increase in speed, fuel consumption and fuel-burn/distance.
an increase in speed and fuel-burn/distance, but an unchanged fuel-burn per hour.

questions.questionText: Consider a climb with a piston engine airplane. What will happen to the fuel flow if the pilot maintains the same power setting?
options: The temperature decreases and thereby over-compensates for the decreasing air density which is caused by the decreasing pressure. This results in a decreasing fuel flow.
With increasing altitude air density decreases and the power output of the engine decreases. This has to be compensated for by an increased fuel flow.
With increasing altitude air density decreases, therefore less fuel is required for combustion, so the fuel flow decreases.
The temperature decreases and thereby compensates for the decreasing air density which is caused by the decreasing pressure. Therefore the fuel flow remains the same.

questions.questionText: Moving the centre of gravity from the forward to the aft limit (gross mass, altitude and airspeed remain unchanged):
options: affects neither drag nor power required.
decreases the induced drag and reduces the power required.

questions.questionText: The combination of factors that most requires a low-angled flap setting for take-off is:
options: Low field elevation, close-in obstacles in the climb-out path, long runway and a high ambient temperature.
High field elevation, no obstacles in the climb-out path, low ambient temperature and short runway.
High field elevation, distant obstacles in the climb-out path, long runway and a high ambient temperature.
Low field elevation, no obstacles in the climb-out path, short runway and a low ambient temperature.

questions.questionText: The following parameters positively affect the take-off ground run: 1) decreasing take-off mass. 2) increasing take-off mass. 3) increasing density. 4) decreasing density. 5) increasing flap setting. 6) decreasing flap setting. 7) increasing pressure altitude. 8) decreasing pressure altitude.

Consider a performance class B aeroplane which is used for commercial transports and a runway where a clearway or a stopway is available. The take-off mass shall not exceed the maximum take-off mass specified in the AFM for the pressure altitude and the ambient temperature at the aerodrome of departure. The unfactored take-off distance, specified in the AFM, shall not exceed the take-off distance available (TODA), when multiplied by a factor of:

Consider a multi-engine piston airplane performance class B. Both, METAR and TAF indicate that you can expect a wet runway at your destination airport, therefore the landing distance should be multiplied by the following factor.
A runway is contaminated by a 0.5 cm layer of wet snow. The take-off is nevertheless authorized by a light-twin's flight manual. The take-off distance in relation to a dry runway will be:
The take-off distance required increases:
options: due to slush on the runway.
due to downhill slope because of the smaller angle of attack.
due to lower gross mass at take-off.
due to headwind because of the drag augmentation.

questions.questionText: Approaching in turbulent wind conditions during manual flight requires:
What is the influence of the mass on maximum rate of climb (ROC) speed if all other parameters remain constant?
options: The ROC and the ROC speed are independant of the mass.
The ROC is affected by the mass, but not the ROC speed.
The ROC speed decreases with increasing mass.
The ROC speed increases with increasing mass.

questions.questionText: Changing the take-off flap setting from flap 15° to flap 5° will normally result in:
options: a better climb and an equal take-off distance.
a shorter take-off distance and an equal climb.
a shorter take-off distance and a better climb.
a longer take-off distance and a better climb.

questions.questionText: On a twin engined piston aircraft with variable pitch propellers, for a given mass and altitude, the minimum drag speed is 125 kts and the holding speed (minimum fuel burn per hour) is 95 kts. The best rate of climb speed will be obtained for a speed:

Consider a performance class B aeroplane which is used for commercial transports and a runway where no clearway and no stopway is available. The take-off mass shall not exceed the maximum take-off mass specified in the AFM for the pressure altitude and the ambient temperature at the aerodrome of departure. The unfactored take-off distance, specified in the AFM, shall not exceed the take-off run available (TORA), when multiplied by a factor of:
If the actual Landing Mass is higher than planned
options: the landing distance will be unaffected.
the landing distance will be longer.
the approach path will be steeper.
the approach path will be steeper and threshold speed higher.

questions.questionText: In case of an engine failure with a multi-engine aeroplane, how are the changes from Vx to Vxse and from Vy to Vyse?
options: Vxse is lower than Vx and Vyse is lower than Vy
Vxse is higher than Vx and Vyse is lower than Vy
Vxse is lower than Vx and Vyse is higher than Vy
Vxse is higher than Vx and Vyse is higher than Vy

questions.questionText: Consider a glide with all engines off. Initial conditions are zero wind and attitude of the airplane gives maximum range. A headwind arises and initially the aircraft attitude is not changed. After some time has passed:
options: the glide range remains the same when CL is reduced.
the glide range remains maximum when CL remains the same.
the glide range increases when the CL is reduced.
the glide range decreases when the CL is reduced.

questions.questionText: Other factors remaining constant, how does increasing altitude affect Vx and Vy in terms of TAS?
options: Vy remains constant and Vx increases.
Vy remains constant and Vx decreases.

questions.questionText: The pilot of a light twin engine aircraft has calculated a 4000 m service ceiling, based on the forecast general conditions for the flight and a Take-off Mass of 3250 kg. If the Take-off Mass is 3000 kg, the service ceiling will be:
options: only a new performance analysis will determine if the service ceiling is higher or lower than 4000 m.

questions.questionText: Consider maximum range speed and speed for maximum angle of climb. How will a headwind affect those speeds? (Assume all factors remain the same)
options: Maximum range speed remains the same and speed for maximum angle of climb remains the same.
Maximum range speed remains the same and speed for maximum angle of climb increases.
Maximum range speed increases and speed for maximum angle of climb remains the same.
Maximum range speed decreases and speed for maximum angle of climb increases.

questions.questionText: Consider a performance class B aeroplane which is used for commercial transports and a runway where a clearway or a stopway is available. The take-off mass shall not exceed the maximum take-off mass specified in the AFM for the pressure altitude and the ambient temperature at the aerodrome of departure. The unfactored take-off distance, specified in the AFM, shall not exceed the accelerate stop distance available (ASDA), when multiplied by a factor of:

An aircraft has two certified landing flaps positions, 25° and 35°. If a pilot chooses 25° instead of 35°, the aircraft will have:
options: a reduced landing distance and degraded go-around performance.
an increased landing distance and better go-around performance.
an increased landing distance and degraded go-around performance.
a reduced landing distance and better go-around performance.

options: an increased landing distance and degraded go-around performance.
a reduced landing distance and better go-around performance.

unaffected because all take-off techniques are accounted for.

During certification flight testing of a transport aeroplane, the distances measured from brake release to the 35 feet point are equal to:
1 547 m with all engines running
1720 m with failure of critical engine at V1 with all other things remaining unchanged
The take-off distance adopted for the certification file is:
The use of reduced take-off thrust is permitted, only if:
options: the actual Take-off Mass (TOM) is lower than the field length limited TOM.
the actual Take-off Mass (TOM) including a margin is greater than the performance limited TOM.
the actual Take-off Mass (TOM) is greater than the climb limited TOM.
the take-off distance available is lower than the take-off distance required one engine out at V1.

questions.questionText:
On a segment of the take-off flight path an obstacle requires a minimum gradient of climb of 2.6% in order to provide an adequate margin of safe clearance. At a mass of 110000 kg the gradient of climb is 2.8%
For the same power and assuming that the sine of the angle of climb varies inversely with mass, at what maximum mass will the aeroplane be able to achieve the minimum gradient?
If the Take-off Mass of an aeroplane is brake energy limited a higher uphill slope would
options: decrease the maximum mass for take-off.
increase the maximum mass for take-off.
have no effect on the maximum mass for take-off

questions.questionText: An airplane takes off from a runway which is contaminated with compressed snow. What happens to the TODA and ASD?
Which of the following is true with regard to VMCA (air minimum control speed)?
options: The aeroplane is uncontrollable below VMCA
The aeroplane will not gather the minimum required climb gradient
Straight flight can not be maintained below VMCA. when the critical engine has failed.
VMCA only applies to four-engine aeroplanes.

questions.questionText: The net flight path climb gradient after take-off compared to the gross climb gradient is:
V1 has to be
options: equal to or higher than VMCA
equal to or higher than VMCG
equal to or higher than V2.

questions.questionText: The lowest take-off safety speed (V2 MIN) IS.
options: 1.15 VSR for all turbojet and turho-propeller aeroplanes.
1.13 VSR for two-and three-engine turbo-propeller and turbojet aeroplanes.

questions.questionText: V2 has to be equal to or higher than:

What is the maximum brake release mass when the following data is given:
Pressure altitude: 6000 ft
Field length available: 7500 ft
Upslope: 2%
Tailwind: 5 kts
Flaps: 15
OAT: -20 °C
A/C: AUTO
Anti-ice: OFF
PMC: OFF
Which one of the following is not affected by a tailwind?
The value of V1 has to be equal to or higher than:
What effect has a downhill slope on the take-off speeds? The slope:
options: has no effect on the take-off speed V1

questions.questionText: During take-off the third segment begins:
options: when acceleration to flap retraction speed (final take-off speed - VFTO) is started.
when landing gear is fully retracted.
when acceleration starts from VLOF to V2.

questions.questionText: Which of the following statements is applicable to the acceleration height at the beginning of the 3rd climb segment?
options: There is no requirement for minimum climb performance when flying at the acceleration height.
The maximum acceleration height depends on the maximum time take-off thrust may be applied.
The minimum one engine out acceleration height must be maintained in case of all engines operating
The minimum legally allowed acceleration height is at 1 500 ft.

questions.questionText:
The determination of the maximum mass on brake release, of an aeroplane (certified under CS 25) with 5°, 15° and 25° flaps angles on take-off, leads to the following values with zero wind:
Flap angle 25°: Runway 66 000 kg, Climb 72 200 kg
Flap angle 15°: Runway 69 500 kg, Climb 69 000 kg
Flap angle 5°: Runway 71 500 kg, Climb 61 800 kg
Wind corrections:
Headwind: +120 kg/1 kt
Tailwind: -360 kg/1 kt
Given that the tailwind component is equal to 5 kts, the maximum mass on brake release and corresponding flap angle will be:

Given:
TORA: 3380 m
Clearway: 780 m
Stopway: 1250 m
What is the TODA if the pilot wants to calculate the RLLTOM (Runway Length Limited Take-Off Mass) for an unbalanced field?
The decision speed at take-off (V1) is the calibrated airspeed:
options: below which the take-off must be continued.
below which take-off must be rejected if an engine failure is recognized, above which take-off must be continued.
at which the take-off must be rejected.
at which the failure of the critical engine is expected to occur.

questions.questionText: An operator shall ensure that the net take-off flight path clears all obstacles. The half-width of the obstacle-corridor at the distance D from the end of the TODA is at least:
In accordance with EASA CS 25 the take-off safety speed V2 MIN for turbo-propeller powered aeroplanes with more than three engines may not be less than
Regarding the obstacle limited Take-off Mass, which of the following statements is correct?
options: A take off in the direction of an obstacle is also permitted in tailwind condition
Wind speed plays no role when calculating this particular mass.
The maximum bank angle which can be used is 10'.
The obstacle limited mass can never be lower than the climb limited Take-off Mass.

questions.questionText: When V1 has to be reduced because of a wet runway the one engine out obstacle clearance/climb performance
What is likely to happen during take-off if a pilot uses a "dry V1" in wet runway conditions?
options: In case of an aborted take-off procedure the airplane would exceed the ASDA because braking action would be lower
In case of an aborted take-off procedure the airplane would exceed the ASDA because the anti-skid system would be adjusted to dry conditions.
In case of an aborted take-off procedure the airplane would not exceed the ASDA because V1 wet and V1 dry are always the same.
In case of an aborted take-off procedure the airplane would not exceed the ASDA because V1 dry considers a safety margin for wet conditions.

questions.questionText: The one engine out take-off run is the distance between the brake release point and:
options: the point where V2 is reached.
the middle of the segment between VLOF point and 35 ft point
the point half way between V1 and V2.

questions.questionText: Which of the following set of factors could lead to a V2 value which is limited by VMCA?
options: High Take-off Mass, low flap setting and high field elevation.
Low Take-off Mass, high flap setting and low field elevation.
High Take-off Mass, high flap setting and low field elevation.
Low Take-off Mass, low flap setting and low field elevation.

questions.questionText: Regarding take-off, the take-off decision speed V1
options: is always equal to VEF (Engine Failure speed).
is the airspeed on the ground at which the pilot is assumed to have made a decision to continue or discontinue the take-off.
is the airspeed of the aeroplane upon reaching 35 feet above the take-off surface.
is an airspeed at which the aeroplane is airborne but below 35 ft and the pilot is assumed to have made a decision to continue or discontinue the take-off.

questions.questionText: Which of the following will decrease V1?
Reduced take-off thrust:
options: can be used if the actual Take-off Mass is higher than the performance limited Take-off Mass.
can be used if the headwind component during take-off is at least 10 kts.
has the benefit of improving engine life.
is not recommended at very low temperatures (OAT).

questions.questionText: Which statement, in relation to the climb limited Take-off Mass of a jet aeroplane, is correct?
options: The climb limited Take-off Mass decreases with increasing OAT.
On high elevation airports equipped with long runways the aeroplane will always be climb limited.
The climb limited Take-off Mass is determined at the speed for best rate of climb.
50% of a headwind is taken into account when determining the climb limited Take-off Mass.

questions.questionText: The speed VR:
options: is the speed at which rotation to the lift-off angle of attack is initiated.
must be equal to or lower than V1.

questions.questionText: In case of an engine failure which is recognized at or above V1:
options: the take-off must be rejected if the speed is still below VLOF
the take-off should be rejected if the speed is still below VR
a height of 50 ft must be reached within the take-off distance.

questions.questionText: Does using engine bleed air affect the take-off performance?
options: No, it does not have an affect on the take-off performance.
Yes, it decreases the take-off performance.
Yes, it increases the take-off performance.
No, it only affects the landing performance.

questions.questionText: May anti-skid be considered to determine the take-off and landing data?

During the flight preparation the climb limited Take-off Mass (TOM) is found to be much greater than the field length limited TOM using 5 flap. In what way can the performance limited TOM be increased? There are no limiting obstacles.
options: By selecting a lower flap setting.
By selecting a higher flap setting.

questions.questionText:
