Considering a take-off with flaps at:
options: 5°, the obstacle limit is increased but the runway limit decreases.
20°, the obstacle limit is increased but the runway limit decreases.

questions.questionText: For a turbojet aeroplane the 2nd segment of the take-off climb begins when:
options: accelerating from V2 to flap retraction speed begins.

questions.questionText: A higher outside air temperature (OAT):
options: increases the climb limited Take-off Mass.
decreases the brake energy limited Take-off Mass.
increases the field length limited Take-off Mass.

questions.questionText: In relation to the net take-off flight path, the required 35 ft vertical distance to clear all obstacles is
options: the height at which power is reduced to maximum climb thrust.
the height by which acceleration and flap retraction should be completed.
the minimum vertical distance between the lowest part of the aeroplane and all obstacles within the obstacle domain.

questions.questionText: The speed used to determine the "maximum tyre-speed limit" is the
In the event that the Take-off Mass is obstacle limited and the take-off flight path includes a turn, the bank angle should not exceed:
options: 10° up to a height of 400 ft.
25° up to a height of 400 ft.
15° up to a height of 400 ft.
20° up to a height of 400 ft.

questions.questionText: Take-off run is defined as the:
options: distance to V1 and stop, assuming an engine failure at V1.

horizontal distance along the take-off path from the start of the take-off to a point equidistant between the point at which VLOF is reached and the point at which the aeroplane is 35 ft above the take-off surface.
distance to 35 feet with an engine failure at v1 or 115% all engine distance to 35 feet.
distance from brake release to V2.

questions.questionText: The speed v1 is defined as:
options: speed for best angle of climb.

questions.questionText: Reduced take-off thrust should normally not be used when:
options: obstacles are present close to the end of the runway.

questions.questionText: What is the advantage of a balanced field length condition?
options: A balanced take-off provides the lowest elevator input force requirement for rotation.
A balanced field length provides the greatest margin between "net" and "gross" take-off flight paths.
A balanced field length gives the minimum required field length in the event of an engine failure.
For a balanced field length the required take-off runway length always equals the available runway length.

questions.questionText: On a segment of the take-off flight path an obstacle requires a minimum gradient of climb of 2.6% in order to provide an adequate margin of safe clearance. At a mass of 110000 kg the gradient of climb is 2.95%. For the same power and assuming that the sine of the angle of climb varies inversely with mass, at what maximum mass will the aeroplane be able to achieve the minimum gradient?
The climb limited Take-off Mass can be increased by.
options: a lower flap setting for take-off and selecting a higher V2.

questions.questionText: Consider the take-off performance for the twin jet aeroplane climb limit chart. Why has the wind been omitted from the chart?
options: The climb limit performances are taken relative to the air.
There is no effect of the wind on the climb angle relative to the ground.
The effect of the wind must be taken from another chart.
There is a built-in safety measure.

questions.questionText: Assume a take-off with a class A airplane on a wet runway. Which of the following values shows the corresponding screen height of the take-off distance?
The effect of increasing the flap setting, from zero to the recommended take-off setting, on the length of the Take-off Distance Required (TODR) and the Field-Length-Limited Take-off mass (TOM) is:
options: Decreased TOD required and increased field length limited TOM.
Decreased TOD required and decreased field length limited TOM.
Increased TOD required and decreased field length limited TOM.
Increased TOD required and increased field length limited TOM.

questions.questionText: Which of the following are to be taken into account for the runway in use for take-off?
options: Airport elevation, runway slope, standard temperature, standard pressure and wind components.
Airport elevation, runway slope, standard temperature, pressure altitude and wind components.
Airport elevation, runway slope, outside air temperature, pressure altitude and wind components.
Airport elevation, runway slope, outside air temperature, standard pressure and wind components.

questions.questionText: What is a balanced field length?
options: The all engine take-off distance and the rejected take-off distance to full stop are equal.
The all engine acceleration distance to V1 and the rejected take-off distance to full stop are equal
The one engine inoperative take-off distance and the rejected take-off distance to full stop are equal.
The one engine inoperative acceleration distance to V1 and the rejected take-off distance to full stop are equal.

questions.questionText: A higher pressure altitude at ISA temperature:
options: has no influence on the allowed Take-off Mass.
increases the climb limited Take-off Mass.
decreases the field length limited Take-off Mass.

questions.questionText: When an aircraft takes off with the mass limited by the TODA:
options: the distance from brake release to V1 will be equal to the distance from V1 to the 35 feet point.
the "balanced take-off distance" equals 115% of the "all engine take-off distance"
the end of the runway will be cleared by 35 feet following an engine failure at V1.
the actual Take-off Mass equals the field length limited Take-off Mass.

questions.questionText: An airport has a 3000 metres long runway, and a 2000 metres clearway at each end of that runway. For the calculation of the maximum allowed Take-off Mass, the take-off distance available cannot be greater than:
Which statement about the balanced/unbalanced field concept is correct?
options: A balanced field cannot contain a stopway
If TOD<ASD then V₁ < Vibalanced unbalanced field.
If TOD>ASD then V₁ > Vibalanced = unbalanced field.

questions.questionText: Occasional movements by aircraft with an ACN of 50 should not adversely affect the pavement if the reported PCN is at least:
The effect of a higher take-off flap setting up to the maximum certified take-off flap setting is:
options: a decrease of the field length limited Take-off Mass but an increase of the climb limited Take-off Mass.
a decrease of both the field length limited Take-off Mass and the climb limited Take-off Mass.
an increase of the field length limited Take-off Mass but a decrease of the climb limited Take-off Mass.
an increase of both the field length limited Take-off Mass and the climb limited Take-off Mass.

questions.questionText: Which combination of circumstances or conditions would most likely lead to a tire speed limited take-off?
options: A low runway elevation and a headwind.
A high runway elevation and a headwind.
A low runway elevation and a cross wind.
A high runway elevation and tailwind.

questions.questionText: VR cannot be lower than:
options: 1.2 Vs for twin and three engine jet aeroplane.
1.15 Vs for turbo-prop with three or more engines.

questions.questionText: The engine failure during take-off run results in:
The 1st segment of the take-off flight path ends:
Which statement concerning the balanced/unbalanced field concept is correct?
options: The balanced field concept provides only one single v_{1} value.
If there is a clearway and a stopway, the field length can never be balanced.

questions.questionText: Which statement about the balanced/unbalanced field concept is correct?
options: A balanced field cannot contain a clearway.
If TOD>ASD then V₁ > Vibalanced = unbalanced field.

questions.questionText: Under what circumstances can the improved climb performance procedure be used?
options: If a stopway is available, the climb limited take-off mass and the climb gradient can be increased by decreasing V1.
If a clearway is available, the climb limited take-off mass and the climb gradient can be increased by increasing V1.
If sufficient excess runway is available, the climb limited take-off mass and the climb gradient can be increased by increasing V2.
If a sufficient excess runway is available, the climb limited take-off mass and the climb gradient can be increased by decreasing V2.

questions.questionText: Which is the correct sequence of speeds during take-off?
Which of the following answers is correct if you consider the take-off distances and the corresponding speeds for take-off?
options: There is a single speed for V_{1} at the runway limited take-off mass for a balanced condition.
In a balanced V_{1} condition ASD = engine failure TOD = engine failure TOR.
V_{1} in a balanced condition is the maximum speed where the TOR and engine failure TOD will not be exceeded.
In a balanced V_{1} condition ASD = engine failure TOD = all engine TOR.

questions.questionText: Considering the take-off decision speed V_{1} which of the following is correct?
options: If an engine failure is recognized before reaching v_{1} the take-off must be aborted.
V_{1} may not be less than v_{2min} the minimum take-off safety speed.
If an engine failure is recognized after reaching V_{1}, the take-off must be aborted.
V_{j} is sometimes greater than the rotation speed VR.

questions.questionText: Given: VS= Stalling speed, VMCA= Air minimum control speed, VMU= Minimum unstick speed, V1= Take-off decision speed, VR= Rotation speed, V2MIN= Minimum take-off safety speed, VLOF= Lift-off speed. The correct formula is:
Due to standing water on the runway the field length limited Take-off Mass will be:
options: only higher for three and four engine aeroplanes.

questions.questionText: In the diagram you can see a take-off distance versus V_{1} speed graph (AE=All Engines, EF=Engine Failure). Which speed corresponds to the balanced V₁?
An airplane takes off from a runway which is contaminated with compressed snow. What happens to the take-off and stop distances?
options: TODR remains the same and ASDR increases.

questions.questionText: Which of the following statements is correct?
options: V_{R} is the speed at which the pilot should start to rotate the aeroplane.
V_{R} should not be higher than 1.05 VMCG
V_{R} should not be higher than v_{1}.
V_{R} is the speed at which, during rotation, the nose wheel comes off the runway.

questions.questionText: A twin engine jet aeroplane is flying at the minimum control speed with take-off thrust on both engines. The critical engine suddenly fails. After stabilising the engine failure transient which parameter(s) must be maintainable?
options: Heading, altitude and a positive rate of climb of 100 ft/min.

questions.questionText: Maximum Tire Speed can limit the Lift-off Speed. Which kind of speed can be directly used to determine this limitation?
If you compare the following speeds with V_{1}, which of them can be lower than V_{1}?
Reduced take-off thrust should normally not be used when:
Which statement is correct?
options: The climb limited Take-off Mass increases when a larger take-off flap setting is used.
The performance limited Take-off Mass is the highest of: i) field length limited Take-off Mass; ii) climb limited Take-off Mass; iii) obstacle limited Take-off Mass.
The climb limited Take-off Mass will increase if the headwind component increases.
The climb limited Take-off Mass depends on pressure altitude and outside air temperature.

questions.questionText: If the value of the balanced v_{1} is found to be lower than VMCG, which of the following is correct?
options: V_{1} must be increased to at least the value of VMCG
The ASDR will become greater than the one engine out take-off distance.
The one engine out take-off distance will become greater than the ASDR.
The V_{MCG} will be lowered to v_{1}.

questions.questionText: Given that the characteristics of a three engine turbojet aeroplane are as follows: Thrust: 50 000 Newton / Engine, g: 10 m/s^{2}, Drag: 72 569 N, Minimum gross gradient (2nd segment): 2.7%. The maximum Take-off Mass under 2nd segment conditions is:
The speed V_{SR} is defined as
options: as reference stall speed and may not be less than 1-g stall speed.

questions.questionText: Which statement is correct?
options: V_{R} is the lowest climb speed after engine failure.
In case of engine failure below V_{R} the take-off should be aborted.
V_{R} is the speed at which rotation should be initiated.
V_{R} is the lowest speed for directional control in case of engine failure.

questions.questionText: Reduced take-off thrust should normally not be used when:
options: windshear is reported on the take-off path.

questions.questionText: How is wind considered in the take-off performance data of the aeroplane Operations Manuals?
options: Since take-offs with tailwind are not permitted, only headwinds are considered
Not more than 80% headwind and not less than 125% tailwind.
Unfactored headwind and tailwind components are used.
Not more than 50% of a headwind and not less than 150% of the tailwind.

questions.questionText: Which statement regarding v_{1} is correct?
options: V_{1} may not be higher than VMCG
When determining V_{11} reverse thrust may only be used on the remaining symmetric engines.
v_{R} may not be lower than v_{1}.
The correction for up-slope on the balanced v_{1} is negative.

questions.questionText: If the field-length-limited take-off mass and V_{1} were calculated using balanced field-lengths, the use of any additional clearway in the calculation would cause:
options: an increased obstacle-limited take-off mass with the same v_{1}.
an increased obstale-limited take-off mass and an increased v_{1}
an increased field-length-limited take-off mass and an increased v_{1}.
an increased field-length-limited take-off mass and a reduced v_{r}

questions.questionText: Which statement regarding the influence of a runway down-slope is correct for a balanced take-off?
options: increases V_{1} and reduces the accelerate stop distance required (ASDR).
reduces V_{1} and reduces take-off distance required (TODR).
reduces V_{1} and increases the accelerate stop distance required (ASDR).
increases V_{1} and increases the take-off distance required (TODR)

questions.questionText: Consider an airplane performance category A. Which of the following speeds below must not be exceeded by V₁?
If the Take-off Mass of an aeroplane is tire speed limited, downhill slope would:
options: increase the maximum mass for take-off.
decrease the maximum mass for take-off.
have no effect on the maximum mass for take-off.

questions.questionText: Which statement is correct?
options: VR must not be less than 1.1 V_{MCA} and not less than V₁.
V_{R} must not be less than 1.05 VMCA and not less than 1.1 V1.
V_{R} must not be less than 1.05 VMCA and not less than V₁.
V_{R} must not be less than VMCA and not less than 1.05 V1.

questions.questionText: In the event of engine failure below v_{1}, the first action to be taken by the pilot in order to decelerate the aeroplane is to:
How is V_{2} affected if T/O flaps 20° is chosen instead of T/O flaps 10°?
options: v_{2} decreases if not restricted by VMCA.
V_{2} increases in proportion to the angle at which the flaps are set.
V_{2} has the same value in both cases.
V_{2} has no connection with T/O flap setting, as it is a function of runway length only.

questions.questionText: The "maximum tire speed" limits:
options: V_{R}, or V_{MU} if this is lower than V_{R}.
VLOF in terms of ground speed.

questions.questionText: Which statement about reduced thrust is correct?
options: In case of reduced thrust v_{1} should be decreased.
Reduced thrust is primarily a noise abatement procedure.
Reduced thrust can be used when the actual Take-off Mass is less than the field length limited Take-off Mass.
Reduced thrust is used in order to save fuel.

questions.questionText: The pilot decides to increase V_{2}. Does this have any effect on the 35 ft screen height?
options: The screen height and the location of the screen height along the runway will not change.
The screen height will be increased to 50 ft.
The distance from the beginning of the take-off roll to the location of the screen height will be shorter.
The 35 ft screen height is located further down along the runway.

questions.questionText: Which of the following statements is correct?
options: The climb limited Take-off Mass is independent of the wind component.
The accelerate stop distance required is independent of the runway condition.
The take-off distance with one engine out is independent of the wind component.
The performance limited Take-off Mass is independent of the wind component.

questions.questionText: Complete the following statement regarding the take-off performance of an aeroplane in performance class A. Following an engine failure before ________ correctly and allowing for a pilot reaction time of ________ loaded aircraft must be capable of decelerating to a stop within the ________

Given that: VEF= Critical engine failure speed, VMCG= Ground minimum control speed, VMCA= Air minimum control speed, VMU= Minimum unstick speed, V1= Take-off decision speed, VR= Rotation speed, V2 MIN= Minimum take-off safety speed. The correct sequence of speeds is:
What is the advantage of balancing V_{1} even in the event of a climb limited take-off?
options: The safety margin with respect to the runway length is greatest.
The take-off distance required with one engine out at V_{1} is the shortest.
The climb limited Take-off Mass is the highest.
The accelerate stop distance required is the shortest.

questions.questionText: Which of the following answers is true?

Due to a pilot mistake, if on a particular flight the value of v_{1} used on take-off exceeds the correct value of v_{1} and an engine fails at a speed immediately above the correct value of v_{1} then:
options: the one-engine-inoperative take-off distance may exceed the take-off distance available.
the accelerate-stop distance will exceed the accelerate-stop distance available.
V_{2} may be too high so that climb performance decreases.

questions.questionText: What is true if the take-off mass of an aeroplane is increased?
Minimum control speed on ground, VMCG, is based on directional control being maintained by:
options: primary aerodynamic control, nosewheel steering and differential braking.

questions.questionText: v_{1} for a balanced-field is calculated when:
options: the take-off mass is field-length limited by the stopway to produce the maximum take-off mass.
the take-off mass is field-length limited by the clearway to produce the maximum take-off mass.
the acceleration/stop distance available is equal to the take-off distance available.
the speed is equal to V_{2}.

questions.questionText: What is the factor for class a aeroplanes, when determining the take-off distance and when considering a take-off from a dry runway with all engines operating?
The requirements with regard to take-off flight path and the climb segments are only specified for.
options: the failure of the critical engine on an multi-engine aeroplane.
the failure of any engine on a multi-engined aeroplane.
the failure of two engines on a multi-engined aeroplane.

questions.questionText: The minimum value of V₂ must exceed VMC by.
The requirements of the take-off net flight path for a Class A aeroplane assume:
options: the failure of any engine of a multi-engined aeroplane at VR.
the failure of the critical engine of a multi-engined aeroplane at VEF
the failure of two engines of a three-engined or four-engined aeroplane at VEF
the failure of any engine of a multi-engined aeroplane at VEF

questions.questionText: How does runway slope affect the field length limited Allowable Take-off Mass, assuming other factors remain constant and not limiting?
options: Allowable Take-off Mass is not affected by runway slope.
A downhill slope increases Allowable Take-off Mass.
An uphill slope increases Take-off Mass.
A downhill slope decreases Allowable Take-off Mass.

questions.questionText: Which of the following statements with regard to the actual acceleration height at the beginning of the 3rd climb segment is correct?
options: There is no legal minimum value, because this will be determined from case to case during the calculation of the net flight path
The minimum value according to regulations is 400 ft
The minimum value according to regulations is 1000 ft.
A lower height than 400 ft is allowed in special circumstances e.g. noise abatement.

questions.questionText: For take-off obstacle clearance calculations, obstacles in the first segment may be avoided:
options: by banking as much as needed if aeroplane is more than 50 ft above runway elevation.
by banking not more than 15° between 50 ft and 400 ft above the runway elevation.
by standard turns - but only after passing 1 500 ft.

questions.questionText: The minimum climb gradient required on the 2nd flight path segment after the take-off of a jet aeroplane is defined by the following parameters: 1) Gear up. 2) Gear down. 3) Wing flaps retracted. 4) Wing flaps in take-off position. 5) N engines at the take-off thrust. 6) (N-1) engines at the take-off thrust. 7) Speed over the path equal to V2 + 10 kts. 8) Speed over the path equal to 1.3 Vs. 9) Speed over the path equal to V2. 10) At a height of 35 ft above the runway. The correct statements are:
If there is a tailwind, the climb limited TOM will:
options: increase in the flaps extended case.

questions.questionText: Explain the effect of a stopway/clearway on the allowed take-off mass and appropriate V1 when using an unbalanced field.
options: If there is a stopway and no clearway the take-off mass must be lower than the balanced field take-off mass, and the appropriate V1 needs to be lower than V1balanced
If there is a stopway and no clearway the take-off mass can be higher than the balanced field take-off mass, and the appropriate V1 needs to be higher than V1balanced
If there is a clearway and no stopway the take-off mass must be lower than the balanced field take-off mass, and the appropriate V1 needs to be higher than V1balanced
If there is a clearway and no stopway the take-off mass can be higher than the balanced field take-off mass, and the appropriate V1 needs to be higher than V1balanced

questions.questionText: Explain the effects of aeroplane and meteorological variables on the obstacle-limited take-off mass. The obstacle-limited take-off mass:
options: increases with a high atmospheric pressure because a higher thrust is available.
increases because of upwards currents if the outside air temperature is high.
decreases in headwind conditions due because of a higher air resistance.
decreases when the runway is contaminated because of increased wheel friction.

questions.questionText: Which of the following distances will increase if you increase V1 but VR remains unchanged?
Which of the following speeds may vary if a stopway or clearway is available?
The 2 seconds time interval during take-off between VEF and V1 is used for.

During certification test flight for a turbojet aeroplane, the actual distance is measured of 1747 m from brake release to a point equidistant between the point at which VLOF is reached and the point at which the aeroplane is 35 ft above the take-off surface. A further distance is measured of 1 950 m from brake release point to a point equidistant between VLOF and 35 ft, having simulated an engine failure at V1. The distance specified as being the aircraft's certificated value of the Take-off Run (TOR) is:
Field length is balanced when:
options: take-off distance available equals accelerate stop distance available.
all engine acceleration to V1 and braking distance for rejected take-off are equal.
one engine acceleration from V1 to VLOF plus flare distance between VLOF and 35 feet are equal.
calculated V2 is less than 110% VMCA and V1

questions.questionText: Which of the following statements, concerning the obstacle limited Take-off Mass for performance class A aeroplane, is correct?
options: It should be calculated in such a way that there is a margin of 50 ft with respect to the "net take-off flight path".
It cannot be lower than the corresponding climb limited Take-off Mass.
It should not be corrected for 30° bank turns in the take-off path.
It should be determined on the basis of a 35 ft obstacle clearance with respect to the "net take-off flight path".

questions.questionText: If the anti-skid system is inoperative, which of the following statements is true?
options: Take-off with anti-skid inoperative is not permitted.
It has no effect on the accelerate-stop distance.

questions.questionText: The result of a higher flap setting (up to the optimum) for take-off is:
Which of the alternatives represents the correct relationship?
options: VMCA and VR should not exceed V1
V2 and V1 should not exceed VMCG
VMCL and V1 should not exceed VR.
VMCG and V1 should not exceed VR.

questions.questionText: During certification flight testing on a four engine turbojet aeroplane the actual take-off distances measured are: 3050 m with failure of the critical engine recognised at V1, 2555 m with all engines operating and all other things being equal. The take-off distance adopted for the certification file is:
Which statement concerning the inclusion of a clearway in take-off calculation is correct?
options: The usable length of the clearway is not limited.
The field length limited Take-off Mass will increase.

questions.questionText: At which minimum height will the second climb segment end?
The engine failure take-off run is:
options: the distance of the point of brake release to a point equidistant between the point at which VLOF is reached and the point at which the aeroplane attains a height of 50 ft above the runway assuming a failure of the critical engine at V1

1.5 times the distance from the point of brake release to a point equidistant between the point at which VLOF is reached and the point at which the aeroplane attains a height of 35 ft above the runway with all engines operative.

the horizontal distance along the take-off path from the start of the take-off to a point equidistant between the point at which VLOF is reached and the point at which the aeroplane is 35 ft above the take-off surface.
1.15 times the distance from the point of brake release to the point at which VLOF is reached assuming a failure of the critical engine at V1.

questions.questionText: Before take-off the temperature of the wheel brakes should be checked. For what reason?
options: To ensure that the wheels have warmed up evenly.
To ensure that the thermal blow-out plugs are not melted.
To ensure that the brake wear is not excessive.
Because overheated brakes will not perform adequately in the event of a rejected take-off.

questions.questionText: Which of the following sets of factors will increase the climb limited TOM (every factor considered independently)?
options: High flap setting, low PA, low OAT.
Low flap setting, high PA, high OAT.
Low flap setting, high PA, low OAT
Low flap setting, low PA, low OAT.

questions.questionText: Which statement is true when calculating take-off data for a class a aeroplane and if the reverse thrust is inoperative?
options: The ASD will increase if the runway is wet.
The ASDA will decrease in case of wet and dry runways.

questions.questionText: In accordance to EASA CS-25 which of the following listed speeds are used for determination of V2 MIN?
The take-off safety speed V2 for two-engine or three-engine turbo propeller powered aeroplanes may not be less than:
The Take-off Mass of an aeroplane is restricted by the climb limit. The effect an increased headwind component on the value of this limit is:
options: The effect would vary depending upon the height of any obstacle within the net take-off flight path.
The climb limited Take-off Mass would decrease.
The climb limited Take-off Mass would increase.

questions.questionText: Which statement related to a take-off from a wet runway is correct?
options: A reduction of screen height is allowed in order to reduce weight penalties.
The use of a reduced VR is sufficient to maintain the same safety margins as for a dry runway.
In case of a reverser inoperative the wet runway performance information can still be used.
Screenheight reduction can not be applied because of reduction in obstacle clearance.

questions.questionText: For a take-off from a contaminated runway, which of the following statements is correct?
options: The take-off performance data is generally determined by the manufacturers by calculation, only a few values are verified by flight tests.
The greater the depth of contamination at constant Take-off Mass, the more V1 has to be decreased to compensate for decreasing friction.
Dry snow is not considered to affect the take-off performance.
A slush covered runway must be cleared before take-off, even if the performance data for contaminated runway is available.
In case of an engine failure recognized below the take-off must be rejected.
the take-off is to be continued unless v1 is less than the balanced V1.
the take-off may be continued if a clearway is available.
the take-off should only be rejected if a stopway is available.

questions.questionText: Refer to figure 032-11. For a twin engine turbojet aeroplane two take-off flap settings (5° and 15°) are certified. Using the attached chart, what is the maximum allowed Take-off Mass, given the following conditions: Airport PA: 7000 ft, OAT: -10°C, Field length available: 2400 m, Slope: Level, Wind: Calm, PMC: ON, A/C: AUTO
The take-off runway performance requirements for transport category aeroplanes are based upon
options: failure of critical engine or all engines operating which ever gives the greater take-off distance.

questions.questionText: Refer to figure 032-12. With regard to the take-off performance of a twin jet aeroplane, why does the take-off performance climb limit graph show a kink at 30 °C and pressure altitude 0 ft?
options: At lower temperatures one has to take the danger of icing into account.
At higher temperatures the VMBE determines the climb limit mass.
The engines are pressure limited at lower temperature, at higher temperatures they are temperature limited.
At temperatures higher than ISA the flat rated engines determine the climb limit mass.

questions.questionText: Which of the following represents the maximum value for V1 assuming max tire speed and max brake energy speed are not limiting?
Which of the following represents the minimum for V1?
Which statement regarding v1 is correct?
options: v_1 is not allowed to be greater than V_R
When determining the v1, reverse thrust is only allowed to be taken into account on the remaining symmetric engines.
v1 is not allowed to be greater than VMCG
The v1 correction for up-slope is negative.

questions.questionText: The rate of climb of a class a aeroplane is 740 ft/min, TAS=200 kts, headwind = 15 kts. What is the minimum gross gradient during the take-off climb?
If the climb speed schedule is changed from 290/0.74 to 280/0.74 the new crossover altitude will be
options: influenced only by the aeroplane gross mass.

questions.questionText: If the climb speed schedule is changed from 280/0.74 to 290/0.74 the new crossover altitude will be
options: higher or lower, depending on the aircraft.

questions.questionText: A four jet-engined aeroplane (mass 150000 kg) is established on climb with all engines operating. The lift to-drag ratio is 14. Each engine has a thrust of 75000 Newtons. Given g=10 m/s, the gradient of climb is:
You climb with a climb speed schedule 300/0.78. What do you expect in the crossover altitude 29 200 ft (OAT=ISA)?
options: During the acceleration to the Mach number 0.78 the rate of climb is approximately zero.
No noticeable effect since the true airspeed at 300 kts IAS and 0.78 Mach are the same (at ISA temperature TAS=460 kts)
The rate of climb increases as the constant IAS-climb is replaced by the constant Mach-climb.
The rate of climb decreases since climb performance at a constant Mach number is grossly reduced as compared to constant IAS.

questions.questionText: For an aircraft climbing at a constant IAS and a constant mass the drag will:
Explain the term cross over altitude which occurs during the climb speed schedule:
options: At the cross over altitude during a climb with a constant speed, a constant Mach number is changed to a constant IAS/CAS.
The cross over altitude is the only pressure altitude where a certain IAS/CAS matches the same Mach number in any atmospheric condition.
At the cross over altitude during a climb with a constant speed, a constant IAS/CAS is changed to a constant Mach number.
At the cross over altitude during a climb with a constant IAS/CAS, the airplane has to be levelled off to achieve a desired Mach number.

questions.questionText: A jet aeroplane is climbing with constant IAS. Which operational speed limit is most likely to be reached?
options: The Mach limit for the Mach trim system.

questions.questionText: Given a jet aircraft. Which order of increasing speeds in the performance diagram is correct?
options: Maximum endurance speed, Maximum range speed, $v_{x}.$
Maximum endurance speed, Long range speed, Maximum range speed.

questions.questionText: What is the maximum vertical speed of a three engine turbojet aeroplane with one engine inoperative (N-1) and a mass of 75000 kg? Using the following: $g=10~m/s^{2}$, $1~knot=100~ft/min$, $sin(Angle~of~climb)=(Thrust-Drag)/Weight$
What happens to the drag of a jet aeroplane if, during the initial climb after take-off, constant IAS is maintained? (Assume a constant mass)
options: The drag increases initially and decreases thereafter.

questions.questionText: A jet aeroplane is climbing at constant Mach number below the tropopause. Which of the following statements is correct?
Assume standard atmospheric conditions. An aeroplane is climbing and has passed its cross over altitude. During further climb the aeroplanes TAS will decrease while flying through the troposphere and
options: thereafter decreases at a higher rate while climbing through the tropopause.
thereafter increases while climbing through the tropopause.
thereafter remain constant while climbing through the tropopause.
thereafter decreases at the same rate while climbing through the tropopause.

questions.questionText: The approach climb requirement has been established so that the aeroplane will achieve
options: manoeuvrability in the event of landing with one engine inoperative.
manoeuvrability during approach with full flaps and gear down, all engines operating.
obstacle clearance in the approach area.
minimum climb gradient in the event of a go-around with one engine inoperative.

questions.questionText: How does TAS vary in a constant Mach climb in the troposphere (assume ISA conditions)?
options: TAS is not related to Mach Number.

questions.questionText: Which speed provides maximum obstacle clearance during climb?
options: The speed at which the excess of thrust available over thrust required is greatest.
The speed for maximum rate of climb.

questions.questionText: Which of the following sequences of speed for a jet aeroplane is correct? (from low to high speeds)
options: $V_{S}.$ maximum range speed, maximum angle climb speed.
$V_{S}$ maximum angle climb speed, maximum range speed.
Maximum endurance speed, long range speed, maximum range speed.
Maximum endurance speed, maximum range speed, maximum angle of climb speed.

questions.questionText: For a jet aeroplane, the maximum climb angle is achieved at a speed corresponding to:
A jet aeroplane is climbing at a constant IAS and maximum climb thrust, how will the climb angle/the pitch angle change?
What happens to the climb performance if the temperature is getting higher?
options: The maximum climb angle will increase and the engine will not be flat-rated anymore.
TAS increases therefore the maximum rate of climb also increases.
Since density is decreasing the speed for the best angle of climb will decrease.
The maximum climb angle decreases because less thrust is available.

questions.questionText: Which speed provides maximum obstacle clearance during climb?
options: The speed for maximum rate of climb.
The speed for which the ratio between rate of climb and forward speed is maximum.

questions.questionText: Which of the following factors determines the maximum flight altitude in the "Buffet Onset Boundary" graph?
The long-range cruise speed is selected because:
options: it is the cruise-climb speed for one or two engines inoperative.
the higher speed to achieve 99% of maximum specific range in zero wind.
it is the best speed for economy.
it achieves the best specific range with a headwind.

questions.questionText: Refer to an aeroplane with jet engines and its thrust required versus speed graph. Which speed is given at the minimum of this curve?
options: The maximum range speed since the fuel flow is approximately proportional to the lift-to-drag ratio and the lift-to-drag ratio is maximum at this point.
The maximum range speed since this speed gives the maximum lift-to-drag ratio.
The maximum endurance speed since the fuel flow is approximately proportional to the thrust setting.
The maximum endurance speed since the fuel flow is approximately proportional to the power required.

questions.questionText: With respect to the optimum altitude, which of the following statements is correct?
options: An aeroplane always flies below the optimum altitude, because Mach buffet might occur.
An aeroplane always flies at the optimum altitude because this is economically seen as the most attractive altitude.
An aeroplane sometimes flies above or below the optimum altitude because optimum altitude increases continuously during flight.
An aeroplane flies most of the time above the optimum altitude because this yields the most economic result.

questions.questionText: Which statement with respect to the step climb is correct?
options: A step climb may not be performed unless it is indicated in the filed flight plan.
Performing a step climb based on economy can be limited by the 1.3 g buffet onset requirements.
In principle a step climb is performed immediately after the aircraft has exceeded the optimum altitude.
A step climb provides better economy than a cruise climb.

questions.questionText: Which statement with respect to the step climb is correct?
options: A step climb is executed because ATC desires a higher altitude.
A step climb is executed in principle when, just after leveling off, the 1.3 g altitude is reached.
A step climb must be executed immediately after the aeroplane has exceeded the optimum altitude.
Executing a desired step climb at high altitude can be limited by buffet onset at g-loads larger than 1.

questions.questionText: The danger associated with low speed and/or high speed buffet:
options: has to be considered at take-off and landing.
limits the maneuvering load factor at high altitudes.
can be reduced by increasing the load factor.

questions.questionText: For a jet transport aeroplane, which of the following is the reason for the use of "maximum range speed"?
The optimum cruise altitude increases:
options: if the tailwind component is decreased.
if the aeroplane mass is decreased.
if the aeroplane mass is increased.

questions.questionText: At a constant Mach number the thrust and the fuel flow of a jet engine:
options: are independent of outside air temperature (OAT).
decrease in proportion to the ambient pressure at constant temperature.
increase in inverse proportion to the ambient pressure at constant temperature.

questions.questionText: If a flight is performed with a higher "Cost Index" at a given mass which of the following will occur?
A jet aeroplane is flying below its optimum altitude. How does the specific range change when the altitude increases while flying with the speed for maximum range?
options: Increases only if there is no wind.

questions.questionText: Which cruise system gives minimum fuel consumption during cruise between top of climb and top of descent? (still air, no turbulence)
Maximum endurance:
options: is achieved in unaccelerated level flight with minimum fuel flow.
is the same as maximum specific range with wind correction.
can be reached with the 'best rate of climb' speed in level flight.
can be flown in a steady climb only.

questions.questionText: Under which condition should you fly considerably lower (4000 ft or more) than the optimum altitude?
options: If the maximum altitude is below the optimum altitude.
If the temperature is lower at the low altitude (high altitude inversion).
If at the lower altitude either more headwind or less tailwind can be expected.
If at the lower altitude either considerably less headwind or considerably more tailwind can be expected.

questions.questionText: The optimum altitude of an aeroplane substantially increases during cruise because of:
options: the moving centre of gravity of the aircraft.
the decreasing temperature of the atmosphere with decreasing altitude.
the fuel consumption of the aircraft.
the decreasing density of the atmosphere with increasing altitude.

questions.questionText: Two identical turbojet aeroplanes (whose specific fuel consumption is assumed to be constant) are in a holding pattern at the same altitude. The mass of the first one is 95 000 kg and its hourly fuel consumption is equal to $3~100~kg/hr.$ Since the mass of the second one is 105000 kg, its hourly fuel consumption is:
At a given altitude, when a turbojet aeroplane mass is increased by 5%, assuming the engines specific consumption remains unchanged, its hourly consumption is approximately increased by:
During a cruise flight of a jet aeroplane at constant flight level and at the maximum range speed, the IAS / the drag will:
The thrust of a jet engine at constant RPM:
options: increases in proportion to the airspeed.
does not change with changing altitude.
is inversely proportional to the airspeed.

questions.questionText: Which statement regarding the relationship between Traffic Load and range is correct?
options: The maximum Traffic Load is not limited by the reserve fuel quantity.
The Maximum Zero Fuel Mass limits the maximum quantity of fuel.
The Maximum Landing Mass is basically equal to the Maximum Zero Fuel Mass.
The Traffic Load can be limited by the desired range.

questions.questionText: On a long distance flight the gross mass decreases continuously as a consequence of the fuel consumption. The result is:
options: the specific range decreases and the optimum altitude increases.
the specific range increases and the optimum altitude decreases.
the speed must be increased to compensate the lower mass.
the specific range and the optimum altitude increases.

questions.questionText: Long range cruise is a flight procedure which gives:
options: a 1% higher TAS for maximum specific range.
a specific range which is 99% of maximum specific range and a lower cruise speed.
an IAS which is 1% higher than the IAS for maximum specific range.
a specific range which is about 99% of maximum specific range and higher cruise speed.

questions.questionText: Below the optimum cruise altitude:
options: the Mach number for long range cruise decreases continuously with decreasing altitude.
the IAS for long range cruise increases continuously with decreasing altitude.
the Mach number for long range cruise increases continuously with decreasing altitude.
the TAS for long range cruise increases continuously with decreasing altitude.

questions.questionText: Which data can be extracted from the Buffet Onset Boundary Chart?
options: The value of the critical Mach number at various masses and altitudes.
The value of the Mach number at which low speed and shock stall occur at various weights and altitudes.
The values of the Mach number at which low speed and Mach buffet occur at various masses and altitudes.
The value of maximum operating Mach number (MMO) at various masses and power settings.

questions.questionText: The centre of gravity near, but still within, the aft limit:
With zero wind, the angle of attack for maximum range for an aeroplane with turbojet engines is:
options: equal to the angle of attack corresponding to maximum lift to drag ratio.
lower than the angle of attack corresponding to maximum endurance.
equal to the angle of attack corresponding to maximum endurance.
equal to the angle of attack corresponding to zero induced drag.

questions.questionText: The optimum altitude:
options: increases as mass decreases and is the altitude at which the specific range reaches its maximum.
is the altitude at which the specific range reaches its minimum.
is the altitude up to which cabin pressure of 8000 ft can be maintained.

questions.questionText: The optimum long-range cruise altitude for a turbojet aeroplane:
options: is only dependent on the outside air temperature.
is always equal to the power plant ceiling.
is independent of the aeroplane mass.
increases when the aeroplane mass decreases.

questions.questionText: The aerodynamic ceiling:
options: is the altitude at which the aeroplane reaches 50 ft/min.
is the altitude at which the best rate of climb theoretically is zero.
is the altitude at which the speeds for low speed buffet and for high speed buffet are the same.
depends upon thrust setting and increase with increasing thrust.

questions.questionText: The optimum cruise altitude is:
options: the pressure altitude at which the fuel flow is a maximum.
the pressure altitude at which the best specific range can be achieved.
the pressure altitude up to which a cabin altitude of 8 000 ft can be maintained.
the pressure altitude at which the speed for high speed buffet as TAS is a maximum.

questions.questionText: An airplane is normally flying with long-range speed because:
options: it results in the longest flight distance (range).
It results in the longest flight time (endurance).

questions.questionText: Assume an airplane is flying at maximum endurance speed. If thrust is permanently increased, how will the values for endurance and range change?
options: Endurance will increase and range will decrease.
Endurance will increase, range will first decrease then increase.
Endurance will decrease, range will first increase then decrease.
Endurance will decrease and range will increase.

questions.questionText: Compared to the maximum range cruise speed, the long range cruise speed is:
options: depends on the outside air temperature and gross mass.
depends on the pressure altitude and gross mass.

questions.questionText: Determine % N1 with the help of the attached table: Pressure Altitude (PA): 6000 ft, PMC: ON, Temperature (OAT): 30°C, A/C packs: AUTO
The speed range between low speed buffet and high speed buffet
options: is only limiting at low altitudes.
narrows with increasing mass and increasing altitude.
decreases with increasing mass and is independent of altitude.

questions.questionText: A jet aeroplane equipped with old engines has a specific fuel consumption of 0.06 kg per Newton of thrust and per hour and, in a given flying condition, a fuel mileage of 14 kg per Nautical Mile. In the same flying conditions, the same aeroplane equipped with modern engines with a specific fuel consumption of 0.035 kg per Newton of thrust and per hour, has a fuel mileage of:
Why are step climbs used on long distance flights?
options: Step climbs do not have any special purpose for jet aeroplanes; they are used for piston engine aeroplanes only.
Step climbs are only justified if at the higher altitude less headwind or more tailwind can be expected.
To respect ATC flight level constraints.
To fly as close as possible to the optimum altitude as aeroplane mass reduces.

questions.questionText: At constant thrust and constant altitude the fuel flow of a jet engine:
What is a cost index?
options: A number denoting the ratio of the costs of crew and maintenance to the cost of fuel.
A number denoting the cost per nautical mile.
A number denoting the ratio of the cost of fuel to speed.
A number denoting the ratio of direct operating costs to speed.

questions.questionText: A higher altitude at constant mass and speed requires:
The maximum endurance speed for jet engined aircraft is corresponds to the:
options: speed where the ratio between TAS and Drag (TAS / Drag) is at maximum.
speed for minimum drag (lowest point of the drag curve).

questions.questionText: Which of the following statements with regard to the optimum cruise altitude (best, fuel mileage) is correct?
options: An aeroplane always flies below the optimum cruise altitude, as otherwise Mach buffet can occur.
An aeroplane usually flies above the optimum cruise altitude, as this provides the largest specific range.
An aeroplane sometimes flies above the optimum cruise altitude, because ATC normally does not allow to fly continuously at the optinum cruise altitude.
An aeroplane always flies on the optimum cruise altitude, because this is most attractive from an economy point of view.

questions.questionText: A stepped climb procedure is used on long-range flights:
options: for airplanes with piston engines only, not for jet airplanes.
to fly as close to the tropopause as possible.
to remain as close to the optimum altitude as possible or to avoid severe turbulence.
only if the headwind is less or the tailwind is greater at higher altitudes.

questions.questionText: A jet aeroplane is flying long range cruise. How does the specific range / fuel flow change?
The speed for maximum endurance:
options: is always lower than the speed for maximum specific range.
is the lower speed to achieve 99% of maximum specific range.
is always higher than the speed for maximum specific range.
can either be lower or higher than the speed for maximum specific range.

questions.questionText: Which of the following is a reason to operate an aeroplane at "long range speed"?
options: The aircraft can be operated close to the buffet onset speed
In order to achieve speed stability.
It is efficient to fly slightly faster than with maximum range speed.
In order to prevent loss of speed stability and tuck-under.

questions.questionText: Have a look at the attached Long Range Cruise Control document and find %N1 and KIAS for a weight of 250 000 kg during a cruise flight in a pressure altitude of 28 000 ft.
For a jet aeroplane, the speed for maximum range is:
options: that corresponding to the point of contact of the tangent from the origin to the Power required versus TAS curve.
that corresponding to the point of the minimum drag at the Drag versus TAS curve.
that corresponding to the point of contact of the tangent from the origin to the Drag versus TAS curve.
that corresponding to the point of the minimum power required the Drag versus TAS curve.

questions.questionText: Which one of the following statements concerning drift-down is correct?
options: The drift-down procedure requires a minimum descent angle after an engine failure at cruising altitude.
The drift-down procedure requires a minimum obstacle clearance of 35 ft.
An engine failure at high cruising altitude will always result in a drift-down, because it is not permitted to fly the same altitude with one engine inoperative as with all engines operating.
When determining the obstacle clearance during drift-down, fuel dumping may be taken into account.

questions.questionText: With one or two engines inoperative the best specific range at high altitudes, compared to all engines operative cruise and assuming altitude remains constant, is:

options: Usually there is an inversion at 17000 ft.
The air condition is turned off below 17000 ft.
The air condition it turned off above 17000 ft.
Fuel dumping can be started at 17000 ft and above.

questions.questionText: A twin engine airplane encounters an engine failure above the OEl ceiling. What is the best speed to descent?
options: The best angle of climb speed.
The best rate of climb speed.

questions.questionText: With regard to the drift down performance of the twin jet aeroplane, what is meant by "equivalent gross weight at engine failure"?
options: This gross weight accounts for the lower Mach number at higher temperatures.
The equivalent gross weight at engine failure is the actual gross weight corrected for OAT higher than ISA + 10°C.
The increment represents fuel used before engine failure.
The increment accounts for the higher fuel flow at higher temperatures.

questions.questionText: A twin engined aeroplane in cruise flight with one engine inoperative has to fly over high ground. In order to maintain the highest possible altitude the pilot should choose:
options: the speed corresponding to the maximum value of the lift / drag ratio.
the speed at the maximum lift.
the speed corresponding to the minimum value of lift / drag ratio.

questions.questionText: How is VMCA influenced by increasing pressure altitude?
options: VMCA decreases with increasing pressure altitude.
VMCA is not affected by pressure altitude.
VMCA increases with increasing pressure altitude.
VMCA increases with pressure altitude higher than 4000 ft.

questions.questionText: If the level-off altitude is below the obstacle clearance altitude during a drift down procedure:
options: fuel jettisoning should be started when the obstacle clearance altitude is reached.
fuel jettisoning should be started at the beginning of drift down.
the recommended drift down speed should be disregarded and it should be flown at the stall speed plus 10 kts.

questions.questionText: "Drift down" is the procedure to be applied:
options: to conduct an instrument approach at the alternate.
after engine failure if the aeroplane is above the one engine out maximum altitude.
to conduct a visual approach if VASI is available.

questions.questionText: With regard to the drift down performance of the twin jet aeroplane, why does the curve representing 35 000 kg gross mass in the chart for drift down net profiles start at approximately 4 minutes at FL370?
options: Because at this mass the engines slow down at a slower rate after failure, there is still some thrust left during three minutes.
Because at this mass it takes about 4 minutes to decelerate to the optimum speed for drift down at the original cruising level.
All the curves start at the same point, which is situated outside the chart.
Due to higher TAS at this mass it takes more time to develop the optimal rate of descent, because of the inertia involved.

questions.questionText: The drift down procedure specifies requirements concerning the:
options: engine power at the altitude at which engine failure occurs.
climb gradient during the descent to the net level-off altitude.
weight during landing at the alternate.

questions.questionText: The drift down requirements are based on:
options: the maximum flight path gradient during the descent.
the Landing Mass limit at the alternate.
the obstacle clearance during a descent to the new cruising altitude if an engine has failed.
the actual engine thrust output at the altitude of engine failure.

questions.questionText: Which of the following will affect the level-off altitude during a drift-down? 1) outside air temperature 2) wind 3) distance to alternate airport 4) engine thrust
An engine failure of a twin engined airplane occured above the OEI ceiling. The pilot wants to fly the least rate of sink thereby the aircraft should be flown at the
options: best OEl rate of climb speed.
best OEI angle of climb speed.

questions.questionText: With reference to the above given values and by using the attached graph, which maximum mass can be used to clear the obstacle with one engine inoperative (OEI)?
You are flying over a mountain range in a jet at FL370 and your aeroplane has an engine failure. Which speed should you fly at to ensure the least rate of descent?

options: increase, because the lift coefficient increases.
decrease, because the lift coefficient decreases.
remain constant, because the Mach number remains constant.
increase, because the lift coefficient decreases.

questions.questionText: An aircraft is descending at 193 kts TAS in still air with a vertical speed of 1000 ft/min. The descent gradient is:

An aircraft is descending in a standard atmosphere above the tropopause at a constant Mach number:
options: TAS is not related to Mach number.

questions.questionText: The lift coefficient decreases during a glide with constant Mach number, mainly because the:

A jet aeroplane descends with constant Mach number. Which of the following speed limits is most likely to be exceeded first?
During a glide at constant Mach number, the pitch angle of the aeroplane will:
options: increase at first and decrease later on.

questions.questionText: At the destination aerodrome the landing distance available is 3000 m. The appropriate weather forecast indicates that the runway at the estimated time of arrival will be wet. For a commercial flight the mass of a turbojet aeroplane at landing must be such that the aeroplane can be landed within:
If the airworthiness documents do not specify a correction for landing on a wet runway; the landing distance must be increased by.

options: Maximum Take-off Run is 0.5 x runway.
Maximum Landing Distance at destination is 0.95 x LDA (Landing Distance Available).
Maximum Landing Distance at the destination aerodrome and at any alternate aerodrome is 0.7 x LDA (Landing Distance Available).
Maximum use of clearway is 1.5 x runway

options: the climb requirements with all engines in the approach configuration
the climb requirements with one engine inoperative in the landing configuration.
the climb requirements with all engines in the landing configuration but with gear up.
the climb requirements with one engine inoperative in the approach configuration.

questions.questionText: What is the landing distance available for runway 09R in London (EGLL)?

It is the maximum mass for quick taxiing, especially for turns during taxiing.
If you cannot vacate the runway via the quick exit lane.
It is the maximum mass corresponding to the minimum time you need to wait before commencing another take-off.

questions.questionText: What should be avoided if quick turnarounds with short sectors have been planned?
To minimize the risk of hydroplaning during landing the pilot should:
options: make a "positive" landing and apply maximum reverse thrust and brakes as quickly as possible.
use normal landing, braking and reverse technique.
use maximum reverse thrust, and should start braking below the hydroplaning speed.
postpone the landing until, the risk of hydroplaning no longer exists.

questions.questionText: What is the minimum field length required for the worst wind situation, landing a twin jet aeroplane with the anti-skid inoperative?

options: He/She must not use the thrust reverser.
He/she has to wait for a certain time until he/she can commence the next take-off.

He/she must not use the quick exit lane.

questions.questionText: The relationship of the reference landing speed (VREF) to the reference stalling speed in the landing configuration (VSRO) is that VREF may not be below:

options: 1.23 VSRO for turbojet powered and 1.30 for turboprop powered aeroplanes.
1.23 VSRO and must be maintained down to 50 ft height.

questions.questionText: At a given mass, the reference stall speed of a twin engine turboprop aircraft is 100 kts in the landing configuration. The minimum speed a pilot must maintain in short final is:

options: The required landing field length is the distance from 35 ft to the full stop point.
In any case runway slope is one of the factors taken into account when determining the required landing field length.
An anti-skid system malfunction has no effect on the required landing field length.
When determining the maximum allowable Landing Mass at destination, 60% of the available distance taken into account, if the runway is expected to be dry.

questions.questionText: According to EASA AIR OPS, for turbo-prop aeroplanes, the required runway length at a destination airport is:
options: 60% greater than that required at an alternate airport.
more than that required at an alternate airport.
the same as that required at an alternate airport.
less then that required at an alternate airport.

questions.questionText: A commercial flight is planned with a turbojet aeroplane to an aerodrome with a landing distance available of 2400 m. The aeroplane mass must be such that on arrival the aeroplane can be landed within:

options: If auto-brake and thrust-reversers are used, the performance will not change.
The braking capability is reduced, therefore the landing distance required will be longer.
The regulated take-off mass will only change if the runway is wet.
The regulated take-off mass decreases but V1 does not change.

questions.questionText: What is the approximate speed of a radio wave?

the angular difference between the corresponding points of two cycles of equal wavelength, which is measurable in degrees.
the sequence of events in which the RF energy goes from zero to maximum positive, to zero, to maximum negative and returns to zero forming a single wave.
the fraction of one wavelength expressed in degrees from 000° to 360°

questions.questionText: Single Side Band (SSB) is used:
options: with transmissions of the Meteorological Aerodrome Report (METAR).
with NDB transmissions to reduce bandwidth.
in VHF transmissions to reduce transmission power.

Continuous Wave transmission can be either frequency or amplitude modulated.

interrupting the modulating signal to break it into dots and dashes.

changing the amplitude of the carrier wave to add information.

questions.questionText: The frequency which corresponds to a wavelength of 12 cm is:

is a modulation form used in ILS where the phase of the navigation signal is changed with 90° or 150°.
results in a change of polarisation of the modulated signal.

questions.questionText: Which of the following is true with reference to frequency of a radio wave?
options: Frequency is the minimum number of cycles occurring in one minute in a radio wave expressed in Hertz (Hz).
Frequency is the number of cycles in one second in a radio wave expressed in Hertz (Hz).
Frequency is the maximum deflection in a radio wave expressed in Hertz (Hz).

options: The information is added onto the carrier wave by altering the amplitude of the carrier wave.
The information is added onto the carrier wave by altering the frequency of the carrier wave.
The information is added onto the carrier wave by reversing the phases of the carrier wave.
The information is added onto the carrier wave by interrupting the carrier wave.

questions.questionText: In accordance with the ITU (International Telecommunication Union) a radio signal may be classified by three symbols. Third symbol indicates (e.g. A1A):
options: Type of modulation of the main carrier.
Type of modulation of the secondary carrier.
Nature of signal(s) modulating the main carrier.
Nature of the information to be transmitted.

questions.questionText: The definition of unit "Hertz" (Hz) is:
options: the number of oscillations per minute of an electromagetic wave.
the distance covered by a radio wave in one second.
the number of oscillations per second of an electromagnetic wave.

questions.questionText: The wavelength of a radio signal with a frequency of 121.95 MHz is:

options: HF Volmet and HF two-way communication.

questions.questionText: The term "Modulation" means:
options: Addition of a low frequency signal (tone, voice) onto high frequency carier wave
Tuning receiver into the frequency of the transmitter
Elimination of disturbances for improving reception
Addition of a high frequency signal (tone, voice) onto low frequency carrier wave

questions.questionText: With regard to radio waves propagation, a "cycle" is defined as:
options: a complete series of values of a periodical process.
the distance covered by a radio wave in one second.
a number of oscillations per second.

questions.questionText: The term "phase" can be defined as:
options: the sequence of events in which the RF energy goes from zero to ma mum positive, to zero, to maximum negative and returns to zero forming a single wave.
the number of complete cycles formed per second.
the angular difference between the corresponding points of two cycles of equal wavelength, which is measurable in degrees.
the fraction of one wavelength expressed in degrees from 000° to 360°.

questions.questionText: The wavelength of a non-directional beacon (NDB) at a frequency of 300 kHz is:

questions.questionText: UHF (Ultra High Frequency) waves appear in the frequency spectrum:
In its basic type, a dipole antenna adapted for a frequency of 110 MHz will have a wire length of:

options: the parabolic reflector generates less side lobes than the flat plate antenna.
the flat plate antenna has a considerably smaller beam width.
the flat plate antenna generates less side lobes than the parabolic reflector.
the parabolic reflector has a considerably smaller beam width.

questions.questionText: Given a wire fed with alternating current and a second wire parallel to, but remote from it. The results will be:
options: the first wire will radiate electromagnetic waves into space and the second wire will double the transmission power of the first one.
the first wire will radiate electromagnetic waves into space and alternating current will be induced in the second wire.
the first wire will radiate electromagnetic waves into space but the transmitted energy will be completely absorbed by the second wire.
the first wire will radiate electromagnetic waves into space and direct current will be induced in the second wire.

questions.questionText: An electromagnetic wave consists of an oscillating electric field (E) and an oscillating magnetic field (H). Which statement is correct?
options: when signal passes through the antenna, the "H" field is parallel to the wire and the "E" field is perpendicular to the wire.
a dipole antenna can only transmit the "H" field.
when signal passes through the antenna, the "E" and "H" fields are perpendicular to to the wire.
when signal passes through the antenna, the "E" field is parallel to the wire and the "H" field is perpendicular to the wire.

questions.questionText: The advantage of the use of slotted antennas in modern radar technology is to:
options: virtually eliminate lateral lobes and as a consequence concentrate re energy in the main beam.
eliminate the need for azimuth slaving.
simultaneously transmit weather and mapping beams.
have a wide beam and as a consequence better target detection.

questions.questionText: The simplest type of antenna construction is a:
options: parabolic antenna used in weather radars.
dipole antenna which is a wire of length equal to one half of the wavelength.
loop antenna used in ADF receivers.
slotted antenna used in modern weather radars.

questions.questionText: An AC will be induced:
options: in a wire, connected in series to a wire fed with a DC.
in a wire, parallel to the wire fed with a AC, but remote from it.
in a wire, perpendicular to the wire fed with an AC, but remote from it.
in a wire, parallel to the wire fed with a DC, but remote from it.

questions.questionText: What is necessary to enable an antenna to transmit an electromagnetic wave?
options: The length of the antenna has to be equal to the wavelength.
The antenna has to be fed with alternating current of high frequency.
The antenna has to be fed with direct current.
The mounting of the antenna has to be perpendicular to the ground.

questions.questionText: What describes polarization?
options: Orientation of the plane of oscillation of the magnetic component (H) of the electromagnetic wave.
Rotation of the antenna around a fixed axis.
Orientation of the plane of oscillation of the electrical component (E) of the electromagnetic wave.
Orientation of the antenna to the North Pole.

questions.questionText: Considering a primary radar system, what kind of aerials are used?
options: One directional antenna for transmitting and one for receiving.
An omni-directional antenna for transmitting, and a directional antenna for receiving.
One directional antenna both for transmitting and for receiving.
A directional antenna for transmitting, and an omni-directional antenna for receiving.

both transmitter and receiver move away from each other.

At a certain moment of time, the phase of the reference signals is unequal and the phase of variable signals is equal for both aircraft.

questions.questionText: An aircraft, on a heading of 180° (M) is on a radial of 270° (M) from a VOR. The bearing you should select on the OMNI bearing selector to centralize the VOR/ILS left/right deviation needle and to proceed to the VOR is:

questions.questionText: Given: Aircraft position 34°15'N 098°E, magnetic variation 28°W, FL 280. PTC VOR/DME position 36°12'N 098°E, magnetic variation 13°E. In order to read the most accurate ground speed given by the DME receiver from his present position, the pilot must fly on which PTC radial?
options: Aircraft will not receive DME information from PTC due to the line of sight rule.

questions.questionText: With respect to the principle of distance measurement using DME can be stated that: 1) the interrogation signal is transmitted on the same frequency as the reply signal. 2) the DME station always has a transponder delay of 50 milliseconds. 3) the time between the pulse pairs of the interrogation signal is at random. 4) in the search mode more pulse pairs per second are transmitted than in the tracking mode. Which of the above given statements are correct?
Which aircraft will be denied first, if the number of pulse pairs received by a DME transponder exceeds the maximum number possible?
options: The aircraft from which the weakest pulse pares are received.
The smallest aircraft using the DME station for distance measurement.
The aircraft which is flying at the lowest altitude.
The aircraft which is flying at the greatest distance from the DME station.

questions.questionText: The accuracy of a DME:

options: range of a DME system is always less than 200 NM.
aeroplane is circling around the station.
aeroplane is below the line of sight altitude.

questions.questionText: Regarding the DME system, which one of the following statements is true?
options: DME operates in the VHF frequency band.
When passing overhead the DME station the DME will indicate 0.
The DME measures the phase difference between the reference and variable phase signals to calculate the distance.
The transponder reply carrier frequency differs by 63 MHz from that of the interrogation signal.

questions.questionText: An aircraft DME receiver does not lock on to its own transmissions reflected from the ground because:
options: the pulse recurrence rates are varied.
they are not on the receiver frequency.

questions.questionText: What is true about the interrogation pulse transmission sequence of a DME system installed on board an aircraft?
options: It changes from interrogation to interrogation.
It is assigned to an aircraft for registration purposes.
It changes, corresponding to the slant range.
It is the same from interrogation to interrogation.

questions.questionText: The reason for using different frequencies for the airborne and ground equipment of a DME is:
options: to prevent that DME interrogation pulse pairs being received by the aircraft after reflection on the earth surface.
that more DME frequencies are available for different beacons.
that side lobes can be suppressed by the Side Lobe Suppressor (SLS).
to avoid second trace returns when a DME is more than 200 NM away.

questions.questionText: Distance Measuring Equipment (DME) operates in the:

questions.questionText: Which one of the statements below is correct regarding the DME?
options: The DME operating frequencies are in the UHF frequency band.
The DME ground station is always co-located with a VOR station.
The indicated distance is the ground distance measured from the aircraft's projected position on the ground to the DME ground installation.

In 30 seconds the DME ident comes up 3 times at an audio frequency of 1 350 Hz.

questions.questionText: A VOR and DME are co-located. You want to identify the DME by listening to the Morse code identifier (call sign). Having heard the same Morse identifier 4 times in 40 seconds the:
options: DME Morse ident was not transmitted, the distance information is sufficient proof of correct operation.
DME Morse ident is the one with the lower pitch that was broadcast several times.
VOR and DME Morse idents were the same and broadcast with the same pitch.
DME Morse ident is the one with the higher pitch that was broadcast only once.

questions.questionText: The DME (Distance Measuring Equipment) operates in the following frequency range:

options: The Airborne installation switching to the memory mode for about 10 to 15 seconds.
The ground installation not sending any pulse pairs..

The airborne equipment switching directly to the search mode.

questions.questionText: A typical DME frequency is:

options: When an ILS/DME is flown then selection of the DME has always to be done manually.
The DME frequency is paired with the localizer frequency so only the localiser frequency is set.
The DME frequency has to be selected by the pilot.

options: A VOR station sited on the flight route.
A VDF station sited across the flight route.
A DME station sited on the flight route.
An ADF sited on the flight route.

questions.questionText: Why can DME stations only send out distance information to approximately 100 aircraft at a time?
options: A DME ground station can only monitor 100 aircraft at a time.

A DME ground station is only able to process 2700 ppps.
A DME ground station is only able to process 100 ppps.

questions.questionText: Of what use, if any, is a military TACAN station to civil aviation?
options: It can provide a magnetic bearing.

options: Every 30 seconds the DME ident will be repeated.

The DME ident will repeat three times every period of 30 seconds.
The VOR ident and DME ident are the same and no difference can be heard.

questions.questionText: ICAO specifications are that range errors indicated by Distance Measuring Equipment (DME) should not exceed:
options: ±1.25 NM plus 0.25% of the distance measured.
±0.25 NM plus 1.25% of the distance measured.

questions.questionText: The reason for using different frequencies for transmitting and receiving in the DME system:
options: is to prevent self-triggering of the receiving equipment by the transmitter.
is to prevent overload of the system.

questions.questionText: The aircraft DME receiver cannot lock on to interrogation signal reflected from the ground because:

options: The EPC has to detect if pulse-pairs reflected by the ionosphere are interfering with directly received pulse-pairs.

The EPC has to detect if the interrogator-receiver has been locked on, by reflected pulse-pairs.

questions.questionText: How can a DME interrogator distinguish between its own reply pulse-pairs and the reply pulse-pairs of other aircraft in the area, using the same DME station?

options: When tracking directly towards the station at a range of 100 NM or more.

Localizer transmitter. 100 meter behind end of runway; Glide path transmitter. 100 meter behind threshold; Middle Marker. 1000 meter from threshold; Outer Marker. 4 NM from threshold

questions.questionText: The ILS marker with the lowest aural frequency is the:

To make both the ILS-localizer- and glide path receiver less susceptible to interference from earth-reflected localizer-signals.

options: Measurement of the frequency shift between the MLS azimuth and elevation transmissions.
Timing the interval between the transmission and reception of primary radar pulses from the aircraft to MLS station.
Timing the interval between the reception of sequential secondary radar pulses from the MLS station to the aircraft.

provides the capability for curved approaches but not of segmented approaches.
provides the capability for CAT III approaches.
provides the capability for segmented approaches but of not curved approaches.

the transmission can be interrupted to avoid reflection by stationary objects.

±20° about the nominal courseline out to a range of 10 NM.
±40° about the nominal courseline out to a range of 20 NM.

PSRs determine and show target range, bearing

when two or more aircraft are in approximately the same direction from the interrogator with a difference in slant range of less than 1.7 NM.
with two or more aircraft in different directions from the interrogator, at the same altitude and a slant range of more than 1.7 NM.

options: unlawful interference with the planned operation of the flight.

questions.questionText: Why is a secondary radar display screen free of storm clutter?
options: The frequencies employed are too high to give returns from moisture sources.
The principle of echo return is not used in secondary radar.
A moving target indicator facility suppresses the display of static or near static returns.
The frequencies employed are too low to give returns from moisture sources.

questions.questionText: Which statement is correct with respect to selections on the transponder control panel?
options: With ON selected a reply in the modes A, C and S can be transmitted.
With the selector knobs the four digits of the squawk can be selected using the decimal symbols 0 through 9.

questions.questionText: The selection of code 7500 on an aircraft SSR transponder indicates:
options: unlawful interference with the planned operation of the flight.

options: Mode S Selective will trigger replies of all mode S transponders. Mode A/C transponders are suppressed.
Mode S Only all-call will trigger transponder replies of all mode transponders. $A/C/S$
Mode S Broadcast will transmit information to all mode S transponders.
Intermode A/C/S all call and Intermode A/C only all call, differ in the pulse spacing used between the P1 and P3 pulse.

questions.questionText: What does it mean if a transponder receives a P2 pulse which is stronger than P1 (and P3) pulses?
options: The transponder responses will be reinforced.

questions.questionText: What is true about code garbling?

It happens at the transponder when interrogation pulses of two different ground stations are received.

To avoid code garbling if two aircraft are located in the same direction (as seen from the interrogator) and at a distance of less than approximately 1.7 NM apart.

questions.questionText: Which one of the following is an advantage of a secondary radar system when compared to a primary radar system?

The relatively small ground antenna transmits no side lobes, thus eliminating the danger of false replies from the airborne transponder.

continuous automatic position reporting using Global Positioning System (GPS) satellites and collision avoidance using TCAS II.

questions.questionText: When and from which station is an SPI (Special Position Identification) pulse sent?

within the coverage of station-referenced navigation aids provided that it is equipped with a minimum of one serviceable self-contained navigation aid.

+5 NM or better throughout the flight.

questions.questionText: Which of the following nav aids can provide an RNAV system with position?

questions.questionText: You are preparing for a descent and ATC asks you to arrive at a specific point and at specific time. What RNAV capability do you need to comply with such instruction?
