must update any altitude change in RNAV system to have correct cross track error.
reads VOR/DME bearing and distance on CDI or HSI to compute himself the cross track error.
reads cross track error and the distance to go on CDI or HSI.

questions.questionText: Which of the following lists information required to input a waypoint or Phantom Station into a basic VOR/DME-based Area Navigation System?
options: Magnetic track and distance from the aircraft to the waypoint or Phantom Station.
Radial and distance from a VOR/DME to the waypoint or Phantom Station.
Radials from a minimum of two VORs to the waypoint or Phantom Station.
Magnetic track and distance to a VOR/DME from the waypoint or Phantom Station.

questions.questionText: Erratic indications may be experienced when flying towards a basic VOR/DME-based Area Navigation System Phantom Station:
options: because, under adverse conditions (relative bearing to the Phantom Station other than 180°/360°) it takes the computer more time to calculate the necessary information.
when in the cone of silence overhead the Phantom Station.
when operating at low altitudes close to the limit of reception range from the reference station.
when the Phantom Station is out of range.

questions.questionText: Which of the following is one of the functions of the Course-Line-Computer in a basic Area Navigation (RNAV) system?
options: It automatically selects the two strongest transmitters for the Area-Nav-Mode and continues working by memory in case one of the two necessary station goes off the air.
It calculates cross track information for NDB approaches.
It checks the ground station accuracy using a built-in test programme.
It transforms the information given by a VOR/DME station into tracking and distance indications to any chosen Phantom Station/waypoint.

questions.questionText: A phantom station (as used in a 2D RNAV-system) is:
options: An existing VOR/DME-station created in the memory of the Navigation Computer Unit of the RNAV-system.
A waypoint defined by two DME-distances from two different VOR/DME-stations.
A waypoint defined by a radial and a DME-distance from a VOR/DME-station.
A non-existing VOR/DME-station defined by two DME.

questions.questionText: Which of the following input data is used by a simple VOR/DME-RNAV systems?
options: Valid VOR/DME signal and desired heading.
Waypoint (phantom station), valid VOR/DME signal and desired course.
Waypoint (phantom station), valid VOR/DME signal and desired heading.

questions.questionText: The navigation computer of the simple 2D-RNAV system computes the navigational problems by:
options: simple sine and cosine mathematics, solving the triangular problems.

questions.questionText: In order to enter a phantom waypoint that is designated by a VOR/DME simple RNAV system, the VOR/DME:
options: has to be positively identified by one of the pilots.
does not have to be in range when entered but must be when used.
does not have to be in range when entered or used.

questions.questionText: Which one of the following lists information given by a basic VOR/DME-based Area Navigation System when tracking inbound to a phantom waypoint?
options: Aircraft position in latitude and longitude.

questions.questionText: What is the deviation per dot on the HSI when using a 2-dot HSI RNAV system in the EN-ROUTE (ENR) mode?
Output from which of the following combination of navigational sources provide enough information to the RNAV-equipment to calculate the wind vector?
options: Inertial Reference System and Air Data Computer.
Global Positioning System and Compass System.
Compass system and Inertial Reference System.
Inertial Reference System and Global Positioning System.

questions.questionText: Under which of the following circumstances does a VOR/DME Area Navigation system switch to Dead Reckoning mode?
options: The system is receiving information from only one VOR.
The system is receiving information from one VOR and one DME.
The system is receiving information from the two DMES.
The system is receiving information from one VOR and two DMEs.

questions.questionText: How does a VOR/DME Area Navigation system select the DME stations to be used for position determination?
options: The VOR/DME Area Navigation System has its own NAV tuner and it always tunes the DME stations closest to the aircraft position.
The VOR/DME Area Navigation system uses whatever stations are tuned on the aircraft's normal NAV selector.
the pilot tunes the closest VOR/DME stations within range on the VOR/DME Area navigation control panel.
The VOR/DME Area Navigation system has its own NAV tuner and the system itself tunes the DME stations providing the most accurate position.

questions.questionText: On what data is a VOR/DME Area Navigation system operating in the dead reckoning mode?
options: Radial from one VOR; distances from two DMEs.
Radial from one VOR; distances from two DMES; TAS from the Air Data computer; heading from the aircraft compass.
TAS from the Air Data Computer; heading from the aircraft compass; the last computed W/V
TAS from the Air Data Computer heading from the aircraft compass.

questions.questionText: Which one of the following inputs to an Area Navigation System (RNAV) comes from an external, not on-board, system?
Which component of an Area Navigation System displays the Cross Track Distance?
In relation to Area Navigation Systems (RNAV), which of the following is an air data input?

In order to give the flight crew control of the required lateral guidance functions, the RNAV equipment should be able to perform several functions. Which of the following functions is not such a function?
options: Provide guidance to another selected flight level.
Make available to the flight crew estimates of positional uncertainty.
Execute a direct clearance to any waypoint.

questions.questionText: Under which of the following circumstances does a VOR/DME Area Navigation system switch to Dead Reckoning mode?
options: When "DR" is selected by the pilot.
VOR/DME Area Navigation computer is receiving neither radial nor distance data information from VOR/DME stations.
VOR/DME Area Navigation computer is not receiving information from the aircraft compass system.
VOR/DME Area Navigation computer is not receiving information from the Air Data Computer.

questions.questionText: The navigational function of the horizontal situation indicator (HSI) in relation to area navigation systems is
options: the indication of the track angle error (TKE).
the indication of the wind component.
the indication of the cross track distance (XTK).

questions.questionText: The reason for an RNAV computer of an aircraft to receive TAS and heading input is:
options: to be able to calculate the actual wind velocity.
to be able to calculate time to go (TTG) to the next TO-waypoint.
to be able to calculate ETA at destination airport.
to be able to calculate cross track errors.

questions.questionText: Which of the following gives the best information about the progress of a flight between 2 enroute waypoints from a RNAV equipment?
In order to give the flight crew control of the required lateral guidance functions, the RNAV equipment should be able to perform several functions. Which of the following are these functions?
options: review, assemble, modify or verify a flight plan in flight, with affecting the guidance output
execute a modified flight plan automatically (no action by the crew required)
where provided, assemble and verify an alternative flight plan with affecting the active flight plan
select or enter the required flight plan through the Control and Display Unit (CDU)

questions.questionText:
Which of the following are output data from 4D-RNAV systems?
1) Distances to any waypoints
2) Wind vectors
3) Radials to VOR stations
4) Altitudes and TASS
Which answer combines the correct output data?
Where is information about the wind direction and velocity derived from?
options: The pilots enter wind data manually, based on current available information.
Wind information is derived by the flight management computer (FMC) by heading, drift, true air speed and ground speed inputs.
Wind information is uploaded via data link from the nearest met office.
Wind information is directly entered into the flight management computer (FMC) by pilots or dispatchers.

questions.questionText: In order to give the flight crew control of the required lateral guidance functions, the RNAV equipment should be able to perform several functions. Which of the following functions is not such a function?
options: Control automatically the speed of the aircraft in order reach a waypoint at a specified time (timing function).
Execute a modified flight plan after positive action by the flight crew.
Fly parallel tracks at the selected offset distance.
Conform to WGS-84 geodetic reference system.

questions.questionText: Which of the following combinations is likely to result in the most accurate Area Navigation (RNAV) position fixes?
Apart from radials and distances from VOR/DME stations, what information is required by the VOR/DME Area Navigation computer in order to calculate the wind?
options: Vertical speed from the air data computer.
True airspeed from the air data computer.
Heading from the aircraft compass system.
Heading from the aircraft compass system and true airspeed from the air data computer.

questions.questionText: A fix obtained by "rho-rho" navigation is based on information from two:
What is cross track distance (XTK-distance) in an Area Navigation System?
options: Distance between actual position and next waypoint.
Distance between air position and great circle track between active waypoints.
Distance between air position and planned track.
Distance between actual position and great circle track between active waypoints.

questions.questionText:
The FMS (Flight Management System) navigation database typically includes the following data:
1) airport reference data.
2) ATC frequencies.
3) company routes.
4) nav aid frequencies.
The combination regrouping all the correct statements is:
The FMS overfly function consists in:
options: selecting the secondary flight plan making it active to fly over the legs.
manually selecting a flight plan to fly over each of the waypoints at the transitions along the route.
manually selecting a flight plan waypoint to fly over when sequencing it instead of flying by at the transition.
manually selecting a flight plan waypoint to hold over for a selected time.

questions.questionText: The navigational database of Flight Management Systems (FMS):
options: contain additional space so that crew members can add their own waypoints.
need to be updated by the cockpit crew as soon as the performance database is updated.
can be modified by the crew so that navigation aids, airways and waypoints may be deleted.
is updated via datalink on a permanent basis to contain actual NOTAMS.

questions.questionText: Which one of the following is an advantages of a multi-sensor system using inputs from a global navigation satellite system (GNSS) and an inertial reference system (IRS)?
options: The activation of 'Selective Availability' can be recognised by the INS.
The GNSS can be used to update the FMS position from a drifting IRS.
The GNSS is never used as an input in a multi-sensor system.
The FMS always uses average position calculated from the GNSS and the IRS.

questions.questionText: What is the period of validity of the navigational database for FMC units?

Components of the FMS (Flight Management System) are:
1) CDU (Control and Display Unit).
2) FCC (Flight Control Computer).
3) FMC (Flight Management Computer).
4) electronic check-lists.
5) GPWS (Ground Proximity Warning System) mode controller.
The combination regrouping all the correct statements is:
In the Flight Management Computer (FMC) of the Flight Management System (FMS), data relating to cruising speeds is stored in the:

The most common sensors interfacing a FMS to compute the aircraft position along the flight plan are:
1) IRS
2) DME
3) NDB
4) GPS
The combination which regroups all of the correct statements is:
Some of the FMS have a navigation mode called Dead Reckoning mode (DR), computing airspeed, heading, wind data ground speed and time. This mode is:
options: the normal navigation mode for FMS which do not use Inertial navigation Systems INS to compute the aircraft position.
a navigation mode used to monitor the FMS position.
a back up navigation mode to compute the FMS position when the other navigation sensors are no longer operating.
an operating mode used to intercept radials To or From a flight plan waypoint.

questions.questionText: On modern passenger aircraft, the navigation database usually contains:

The FMS (Flight Management System) navigation database typically includes the following data:
1) obstacles.
2) waypoints.
3) SID, STAR.
4) terrain cells.
5) magnetic variation.
The combination regrouping all the correct statements is:
The Flight Management System (FMS) is organised in such a way that:
options: the main navigation database of the FMC is created by the pilot.
the pilot is able to modify the main navigation database in the FMC between two updates.
the main navigation database is read only to the pilot.
the main navigation database of the FMC is valid for one year.

questions.questionText: Kalman filtering is used within:

The FMS (Flight Management System) navigation database typically includes the following data:
1) obstacles.
2) navaids.
3) SID, STAR and approaches procedures.
4) waypoints.
5) airways.
The combination regrouping all the correct statements is:

The FMS (Flight Management System) navigation database typically includes the following data:
1) SIDs and STARS.
2) radio aids.
3) optimum flight level.
4) company routes.
5) cruise speeds.
The combination regrouping all the correct statements is:
The Flight Management Computer (FMC) position is:
options: the same as that given on the No. 1 IRS.
the computed position based on a number of sources (IRS, Radio, ILS, GPS etc.).
the actual position of the aircraft at any point in time.
another source of aircraft position; it is independent of other position sources (IRS, Radio, ILS etc.).

questions.questionText: In the Flight Management Computer (FMC) of the Flight Management System (FMS), data relating to flight plans is stored in the:
The IRS is a self-contained system because:
options: it operates independently of navigational aids outside the aircraft.
the system generates a warning in case of a failure.
it operates off its own power supply.
the calculation of the position does not require any software.

questions.questionText: In the Flight Management Computer (FMC) of the Flight Management System (FMS), data relating to aircraft flight envelope computations is stored in the:
In an Electronic Flight Instrument System (EFIS) data relating primarily to navigation in the FMC is provided by:
options: GPS, Aircraft Weather Radar, Navigation radios.
Navigation radios, GPS, Inertial Reference Systems.
Inertial Reference Systems, Navigation radios, Terrain Collision Alerting System.
Inertial Reference Systems, Aircraft Weather Radar, Navigation radios.

questions.questionText:
The FMS (Flight Management System) navigation database typically includes the following data:
1) airports.
2) take-off speeds.
3) navaids.
4) terrain cells.
5) runways.
The combination regrouping all the correct statements is:
Concerning FMC databases:
options: company database and FMC databases can not be used at the same time.
the navigation database may be customized for the specific airline operations.
only the navigation database may be customized by the company's cost index strategy.
only the performance database may be customized by the company.

questions.questionText: On a modern commercial aircraft, the FMS provides:
options: lateral and vertical navigation and guidance and performance management.
a 3D area navigation and an air/ground datalink.

questions.questionText: The FMS cross track (XTK) is:
options: the distance error between the FMS computed position and the IRS computed position.
the abeam distance error, to the left or right from the desired flight plan leg to the aircraft position.
the distance error between the FMS computed position and the GPS computed position.
the angular distance error, to the left or right from the desired track (DTK) to the aircraft track (TK).

questions.questionText: In the Flight Management Computer (FMC) of the Flight Management System (FMS), data relating to waypoints is stored in the:
When two waypoints are entered on the FMS flight plan page, a track between the two fixes is computed and can be displayed on the Navigation map Display (ND). This leg created is:
options: two rhumb-lines joined by a straight segment.
two great circle arcs joined by a straight segment.

questions.questionText: Which component of typical EFIS generates the visual displays on the EADI and EHSI?
Which of the following lists all the stages of flight when it is possible to change the route in the active flight plan on an FMS equipped aircraft?
options: At any time before take-off and throughout the flight.
Only before the flight plan is activated.
Only once the aircraft is airborne.

questions.questionText: The FMC database is divided into two broad sections, namely:
options: the performance database and the ATC information database.
the navigation database and the FMS database.
the navigation database and the performance database.
the NAV database and the flight engineer's database.

questions.questionText:
The most common sensors interfacing a FMS to compute the aircraft position along the flight plan are:
1) DME
2) GPS
3) LOCALIZER
4) NDB
The combination which regroups all of the correct statements is:
What is true about the FMC databases:
options: the data can not be customized for the specific airline operations.
the navigation database contains the company's cost index strategy.
the data includes SIDs, STARS and runway approaches.
the performance database contains aeronautical information for the planned area of operations of the aircraft.

questions.questionText: In the Flight Management Computer (FMC) of the Flight Management System (FMS), data relating to aircraft fuel flow parameters is stored in the:
In the Flight Management Computer (FMC) of the Flight Management System (FMS), data relating to V1, VR and V2 speeds is stored in the:
In the Flight Management Computer (FMC) of the Flight Management System (FMS), data relating to STARs and SIDs is stored in the:

The FMS (Flight Management System) navigation database typically includes the following data:
1) airports.
2) obstacles.
3) navaids.
4) airways.
5) terrain cells.
The combination regrouping all the correct statements is:
IRS is a self-contained system because:
options: the system has the ability to calculate the aircraft's radio position without any reference to either man-made or natural information.
the system has the ability to calculate the aircraft's position with an accuracy comparable to the GPS-position.
the system has a battery back-up which guarantees the well-functioning of the system in case of power-failure.
the system calculates the position of the aircraft without reference to externally generated (man-made) signals.

questions.questionText: In the Boeing 737-400 FMS, the CDU is used to:
options: manually initialize the Flight Director System and FMC with dispatch information.
automatically initialize the IRS and FMC with dispatch information.
manually initialize the Flight Director System, FMC and Autothrottle with dispatch information.
manually initialize the IRS and FMC with dispatch information.

questions.questionText: In which modes of an Electronic Horizontal Situation Indicator (EHSI) on a B737-400 will radar returns not be shown?
options: FULL VOR/ILS, EXP VOR/ILS and PLAN.
FULL NAV, FULL VOR/ILS and PLAN.

questions.questionText: The Control and Display Unit (CDU) on an FMS is:
options: the system used to update the navigation database.
used by the crew to input data into FMC.
used on ground only to monitor the maintenance procedure.

questions.questionText: Under EASA CS-25 regulations colour code rules for EFIS displays, features displayed in amber / yellow indicate:
Which FMC/CDU page normally appears on initial power application to the B737-400 Electronic Flight Instrument System?
Which of the following lists the first three pages of the FMC/CDU normally used to enter data on initial start-up of the B737-400 Electronic Flight Instrument System?
According to EASA regulations, for what type of EFIS message is the colour RED used?
options: Flight envelope, system limits, engaged mode.

questions.questionText: Under EASA CS-25 regulations colour code rules for EFIS displays, increasing intensity of precipitation are coloured in the order.
Under EASA CS color code rules features displayed in cyan/blue, on an Electronic Flight Instrument Systems (EFIS), indicate:
How does a typical EFIS system respond to the failure of a VHF navigation (VOR) receiver?
options: The pointer flashes and a VOR 1 or 2 failure warning bar appears.
It removes the associated magenta deviation bar and/or pointer from the display.
The deviation bar and/or pointer change colour to red and flash intermittently.
The pointer rotates around the display and a VOR 1 or 2 failure warning bar appears.

questions.questionText: Under the EASA regulations color code rules for EFIS displays, the features displayed in GREEN indicate:
How can a pilot enter a waypoint into his FMS RNAV system?
In what piece of FMS equipment will the pilot enter the waypoint information for the route?
The main task of the user segment (receiver) of the satellite navigation system NAVSTAR/GPS is to calculate receiver position by.
options: transmitting signals which, from the time taken, are used to determine the distance to the satellite.
selecting appropriate satellites automatically, to track the signals and to measure the time taken by signals from the satellites to reach the receiver.
monitoring the status of the satellites, determine their positions and to measure the time.
monitoring the orbital planes of the satellites.

questions.questionText: Which of the following is the datum for altitude information when conducting flights under IFR conditions on airways using the NAVSTAR GPS satellite navigation system?
options: GPS altitude if 4 or more satellites are received otherwise barometric altitude.
The average of GPS altitude and barometric altitude.

questions.questionText: What is correct regarding Galileo satellites? 1) In each satellite there are two identical atomic clocks. 2) In each satellite there are two non-identical atomic clocks. 3) In each satellite there is one master atomic clock and one reference clock. 4) The clocks of the satellites are synchronized with each other. 5) The clocks of the satellites are synchronized with a network of clocks which located on the ground.
The GPS control segment consists of
options: a Master Control Station, Monitor Stations and Ground Antennas.
Master Control Stations and Monitor Stations.
a Master Control Station, Monitor Stations and Geostationary Satellites.
a Master Control Station, Monitor Stations, Ground Antennas and Geostationary Satellites.

questions.questionText: Refer to the navigation message of GPS. What is contained in the almanac data?
options: Precise ephemeris data of the concerning GPS satellite.
Orbital data about all satellites in the GPS constellation.
Precise ephemeris data of all satellites in the GPS constellation.
Broadcast ephemeris data of the navigation message of a GPS satellite.

questions.questionText: The distance between a NAVSTAR GPS satellite and receiver is:
options: calculated from the Doppler shift of the known frequencies.
determined by the phase shift of the Pseudo Random Noise code multiplied by the speed of light.
calculated, using the WGS-84 reference system, from the known positions of the satellite and the receiver.
determined by the time taken for the signal to arrive from the satellite multiplied by the speed of light.

questions.questionText: What is the time taken to receive the complete Navigation Message (complete set of data) from all satellites?
options: 12 hours (= period of the satellites orbit).

questions.questionText: How many operational satellites are required for Full Operational Capability (FOC) of the satellite navigation system NAVSTAR GPS?
In a satellite assisted navigation system (GNSS/GPS) a position line is obtained by.
options: timing the period that is taken for a satellites transmission to reach the aircrafts receiver.
timing the period that is taken for a transmission from the aircrafts transmitter/receiver to reach and return from a satellite in a known position.
the aircrafts receiver measuring the phase angle of the signal received from a satellite in a known position.
the aircrafts receiver measuring the time difference between signals received from a minimum number of satellites.

questions.questionText: Which of the following satellite navigation systems has Full Operational Capability (FOC) and is approved for specified flights under IFR conditions in Europe?

In order to carry out an independent three-dimensional fix, Receiver Autonomous Integrity Monitoring (RAIM) and failure detection and exclusion of any faulty satellite, signal reception is required from a minimum number of how many satellites?
In the NAVSTAR/GPS satellite navigation system, re-use of Selective Availability (SA) would give the option to artificially degrade the accuracy by.
options: using a less accurate atomic clock in a satellite for signal processing.
offsetting satellite atomic clocks by a predetermined constant amount.

questions.questionText: What type of satellite navigation system NAVSTAR/GPS receiver is most suitable for use on board an aircraft?
GPS satellite system. The navigation message contains several data, e.g. parameters of the model of the ionosphere. These parameters are used by.
options: receivers to use satellites with the least tropospheric delay.
receivers to choose satellites with the best GPS signal strength.
authorized users to work on the L1 and L2 frequency.
receivers to calculate the delay of GPS signals.

questions.questionText: The navigation message contains clock correction parameters which are used for.
options: correction of both, satellite time and receiver time.

questions.questionText: How does a receiver of the NAVSTAR GPS satellite navigation system determine the elevation and azimuth data of a satellite relative to the location of the antenna?
options: It calculates it by using almanac data transmitted by the satellites.
The data is based on the direction to the satellite determined at the location of the antenna.
The data is determined by the satellite and transmitted together with the navigation message.
The data is stored in the receiver together with the Pseudo Random Noise (PRN) code.

questions.questionText: GPS satellites transmit on two L-band frequencies with different types of signals. Which of these are generally available for use by civil aviation?
The required 30 GALILEO operational satellites are located on:
options: 9 orbital planes with 3 satellites in each plane plus 3 reserve satellites positioned in a geostationary orbital plane.
5 orbital planes with 6 satellites in each plane.
6 orbital planes with 5 satellites in each plane.
3 orbital planes with 9 satellites + 1 spare replacement in each plane.

questions.questionText: What datum is used for the Minimum Descent Altitude (MDA) on a non-precision approach when using the NAVSTAR GPS satellite navigation system?
options: If using Differential-GPS (D-GPS) the altitude obtained from the D-GPS, otherwise barometric altitude.

questions.questionText: One of the tasks of the GPS control segment is:
options: to calculate and transmit differential corrections to users.
to control continuously the motion of GPS-satellites.
to detect the malfunctioning of satellites.
to calculate the accuracy of GPS.

questions.questionText: Which of the following coordinate systems is used by the NAVSTAR GPS receiver to determine position (latitude, longitude and altitude)?
In relation to the satellite navigation system NAVSTAR GPS, which of the following statements correctly describes the term Pseudo Random Noise (PRN) signal?
options: PRN is the atmospheric jamming that affects the signals transmitted by the satellites.
PRN is a code used for the identification of the satellites and the measurement of the time taken by the signal to reach the receiver.
PRN occurs in the receiver. It is caused by the signal from one satellite being received from different directions (multipath effect).
PRN describes the continuous electro-magnetical background noise that exists in space.

questions.questionText: Errors occur in GPS satellite orbits, these errors are because of:
options: solar winds and gravitation of the sun, moon and planets.
lunar winds and gravitation of the earth, moon and stars.

questions.questionText: What is the function of the control segment in GPS NAVSTAR?
options: To ensure the transmitted data of the satellites is controlled and updated from time to time by ground stations.
To transmit a signal used by a suitable receiver to calculate position.
To monitor and ensure that the transmitted signals are saved and processed to utilize WAAS.

questions.questionText: Which of the following lists are all errors that affect the accuracy and reliability of the satellite assisted navigation system (GNSS/GPS)?
options: Satellite to ground time lag; atmospheric propagation; satellite clock.
Satellite mutual interference; frequency drift; satellite to ground time lag.
Satellite clock; satellite ephemeris; atmospheric propagation.
Satellite mutual interference satellite ephemeris; atmospheric propagation.

questions.questionText: What is the procedure to be followed if, on a flight under IFR conditions using the NAVSTAR GPS satellite navigation system, the number of satellites required to maintain the RAIM (Receiver Autonomous Integrity Monitoring) function are not available?
options: The flight may be continued using other certificated navigation systems.
The flight has to be continued under VFR conditions.
The flight may be continued as planned if at least 4 satellites are available and the pilot monitors the GPS manually.
A constant heading and speed must be flown until the required number of satellites are again available.

questions.questionText: Which information about the GALILEO system is correct? (Note: orbital heights = approximate values)
options: Satellites: 24, orbital planes: 6, orbital height: 20 200 km
Satellites: 24, orbital planes: 3, orbital height: 19100 km
Satellites: 30, orbital planes: 3, orbital height: 23 200 km
Satellites: 10, orbital planes: 3, orbital height: 18400 km

questions.questionText: Almanac data stored in the receiver of the satellite navigation system NAVSTAR GPS is used for the:
options: fast identification of received signals coming from visible satellites.
assignment of received PRN-codes (Pseudo Random Noise) to the appropriate satellite.

questions.questionText: Which of the following lists all the parameters that can be determined by a GPS receiver tracking signals from 4 different satellites?
How does a NAVSTAR GPS satellite navigation system receiver recognise which of the received signals belongs to which satellite?
options: Each satellite transmits its signal, on common frequencies, with an individual Pseudo Random Noise code.
The Doppler shift is unique to each satellite.
The receiver detects the direction from which the signals are received and compares this information with the calculated positions of the satellites.
Each satellite transmits its signal on a separate frequency.

questions.questionText: At what approximate height above the WGS 84 ellipsoid are NAVSTAR GPS satellites circling the Earth?
How long does it take a NAVSTAR GPS satellite to orbit the earth?
options: Approximately 12 hours (1/2 of a sidereal day).
365 days because the satellites are located in a geostationary orbit.

questions.questionText: Which of the following combinations of satellite navigation systems provide the most accurate position fixes in air navigation?
How many satellites form the nominal NAVSTAR GPS constellation?
In relation to the satellite navigation system NAVSTAR GPS, "all in view" is a term used when a receiver.
options: requires the signals of all visible satellites for navigation purposes.
is tracking all currently visible satellites above the receiver's mask angle and uses them to compute the position.
is receiving the signals of all visible satellites but tracking only those of the 4 with the best geometric coverage.
is receiving and tracking the signals of all 24 operational satellites simultaneously.

questions.questionText: One of the main tasks of the control segment of the satellite navigation system NAVSTAR GPS is to:
options: manipulate the signals of selected satellites to reduce the precision of the position fix (Selective Availability SA).
monitor the status of the satellites.

questions.questionText: What is true concerning UTC parameters within the navigation message of a NAVSTAR GPS satellite?
options: They provide the difference in seconds - between UTC time and GPS time.
They are used for the correction of the receiver clock which is used to measure the travel time between satellite and receiver.
UTC parameters are time parameters given in UTC, if the moment of transmission of a GPS signal cannot be determined.
They are used for the satellite's clock correction to convert from satellite-time to GPS time.

questions.questionText: Refer to the Galileo satellite system. The inclination of the satellite orbits to the plane of the equator is:
Which of the following statements about the visibility of NAVSTAR GPS satellites is correct?
options: It varies, depending on the time and observers location.
It is greatest at the equator.
It is the same throughout the globe.
It is greatest at the poles.

questions.questionText: The influence of the ionosphere on the accuracy of the satellite navigation system NAVSTAR GPS is:
options: minimised by computing the average of all signals.
only significant if the satellites are located at a small elevation angle above the horizon.
reduced by using a model of the ionosphere by the satellites in Standard Positioning Service (SPS) receivers.

questions.questionText: Signal reception is required from a minimum number of satellites that have adequate elevation and suitable geometry in order for a satellite assisted navigation system (GPS) to carry out independent three dimensional operation without the Receiver Autonomous Integrity Monitoring (RAIM) function. The number of satellites is:

When talking about the GALILEO GNSS we can say that every satellite posses two atomic clocks: 1) which are identical. 2) which are not identical. 3) whereof one is the master and one is the slave. 4) which are not synchronized with each other. Which of the above is/are correct?
In accordance with ICAO Annex 10 the NAVSTAR/GPS global average 95% position accuracy in SPS should be:
When considering a GPS navigation system, the following is true:
options: There would be no advantage if a GPS and INS system would be combined.
The tropospheric delay can be neglected in P-RNAV conditions.
For a position fixing during cruise flight, the dilution of precision (DOP) has to be more than six.
For precise position fixing, information about the satellite clock error is very important.

questions.questionText: Which one of the following errors can be compensated for by a NAVSTAR GPS receiver comparing L1 and L2 frequencies?
How long does it take a GALILEO satellite to orbit the earth?
The basic elements of the satellite navigation system NAVSTAR GPS are the:
options: main control station, the monitoring station and the ground antennas.
antenna, the receiver and the central control unit (CDU).
atomic clock, power supply and transponder.

questions.questionText: The receiver aerial for a NAVSTAR GPS system should be mounted:
options: on the upper side of the fuselage in the vicinity of the centre of gravity.
under the fuselage in order to receive correction data transmitted by D-GPS stations.
inside the tail fin to minimise the influence of reflections from the wing and fuselage.
in the vicinity of the receiver to avoid long transmission lines.

questions.questionText: What type of clock is used in NAVSTAR GPS satellites?
In the event of the re-use of Selective Availability, how does this affect, if at all, the navigation accuracy of the NAVSTAR GPS satellite navigation system?
options: It has no influence because, by selecting of the most suitable signals, the computing process in the receiver is quicker.
It degrades position accuracy by manipulating satellite signals.
It degrades accuracy by reducing the number of available satellites.
It increases because only signals from satellites in the roost suitable geometric constellation are selected by the receiver.

questions.questionText: What is the difference between NAVSTAR GPS and GLONASS, if any?
options: GPS satellites orbit faster around the Earth than GLONASS satellites.
GPS and GLONASS satellites need the same time to orbit around the Earth.

The speed of the satellites is changing permanently. Sometimes GPS satellites orbit faster around the Earth than GLONASS satellites and sometimes GLONASS satellites orbit faster around the Earth than GPS satellites.
GLONASS satellites orbit faster around the Earth than GPS satellites

questions.questionText: How many clocks are installed in each NAVSTAR GPS satellite?
One of the tasks of the space segment of the satellite navigation system NAVSTAR GPS is to:
options: transmit signals which can be used, by suitable receivers, to determine time, position and velocity.
compute the user position from the received user messages and to transmit the computed position back to the user segment.
monitor the satellites orbits and status.
transmit signals to suitable receivers and to monitor the orbital planes autonomously.

questions.questionText: Which of the following statements concerning the L1 and L2 NAVSTAR GPS transmission frequencies and codes is correct?
options: The higher frequency is only used to transmit the P code.
C/A and P codes are transmitted at different times on both frequencies.
The higher frequency is used to transmit both the C/A and P codes.
The lower frequency is used to transmit both the C/A and P codes.

questions.questionText: In which frequency bands are the L1 and L2 frequencies used by the satellite navigation system NAVSTAR GPS for transmission of the navigation message?
The orbital planes of the satellite navigation system NAVSTAR GPS are:
Which of the following geometric satellite constellations provides the most accurate NAVSTAR GPS position fix?
options: 4 satellites with an azimuth of 90° from each other and a low elevation above the horizon.
3 satellites with an azimuth of 120° from each other and an elevation of 45° above the horizon.
4 satellites with an azimuth of 90° from each other and an elevation of 45° above the horizon.
3 satellites with a low elevation above the horizon and an azimuth of 120° from each other together with a fourth directly overhead.

questions.questionText: What is the maximum latitude of a GPS-satellite ground track?
The navigation message contains:
Which statement is true regarding GLONASS?
options: GLONASS satellites circle at 3 polar orbits.
Orbiting time of the GLONASS satellites is the same as NAVSTAR satellites.
Satellite system consists of 6 orbital planes.
Satellites orbit at lower height than GPS/NAVSTAR satellites.

questions.questionText: GPS system satellites transmit their signals on two carrier waves 1 575 MHz and 1 227 MHz and supply two possible codes accessible according to user (civil or military). Commercial aviation uses:
options: only the 1 575 MHz carrier wave and one code.
only the 1 575 MHz carrier wave and two codes.
the two carrier waves and one public code.
only the 1 227 MHz carrier wave and one code.

questions.questionText: Which of the following NAVSTAR GPS satellite navigation system codes can be processed by unauthorised civil aviation receivers?
The geometric shape of the reference system for the satellite navigation system NAVSTAR GPS, defined as WGS 84, is:
options: a mathematical model that describes the exact shape of the Earth.

questions.questionText: One of the tasks of the GPS control segment is:
options: to regulate the transmitted power by the satellites.
to calculate and transmit differential corrections to users which are able to receive Wide Area Differential GPS systems.
to control continuously the position and the motion of GPS-satellites.
to determine and send new ephemeris and new satellite clock error data to the GPS-satellites.

questions.questionText: In which frequency band do satellite assisted navigation systems (GNSS/GPS) provide position information that is available to civil aircraft?
In relation to the NAVSTAR GPS satellite navigation system, "search the sky" is a
options: procedure performed by the receiver to recognise new satellites becoming operational.
continuous procedure performed by the receiver that searches the sky for satellites rising above the horizon.
procedure that starts after switching on a receiver if there is no stored satellite data available.
continuous process by the ground segment to monitor the GPS satellites.

questions.questionText: In relation to the satellite navigation system NAVSTAR GPS, the term inclination denotes the angle between the:
options: orbital plane and the earths axis.
horizontal plane at the location of the receiver and the direct line to a satellite.
orbital plane and the equatorial plane.
horizontal plane at the location of the receiver and the orbital plane of a satellite.

questions.questionText: Of how many satellites does the space segment of a NAVSTAR/GPS system consist in the notional constellation?
For the 'timing section' of the Galileo satellite system, how many clocks have been developed?
options: 2 clocks have been developed, a Rubidium Frequency Standard clock and a Passive Hydrogen Maser clock
1 clock has been developed, a very precise Passive Hydrogen Maser clock
2 clocks have been developed, a Caesium Frequency Standard clock and a Passive Hydrogen Maser clock
3 clocks have been developed, an optical clock (Strontium) and a Caesium Frequency Standard clock and a Passive Hydrogen Maser clock

questions.questionText: Concerning the NAVSTAR GPS satellite navigation system, what is the meaning of the term Receiver Autonomous Integrity Monitoring (RAIM)?
options: It is a method whereby a receiver ensures the integrity of the Pseudo Random Noise (PRN) code transmitted by the satellites.
It is the ability of the GPS satellites to check the integrity of the data transmitted by the monitoring stations of the ground segment.
It is a technique by which a receiver checks the reliability of the signals it is receiving and can detect if one of the signals is incorrect.
It is a technique whereby the receivers of the world-wide distributed monitor stations (ground segment) automatically determines the integrity of the navigation message.

questions.questionText: In what type of nominal orbit are NAVSTAR GPS satellites placed?
The required 24 NAVSTAR GPS operational satellites are located on:
options: 6 orbital planes with 4 satellites in each plane.
3 orbital planes with 8 satellites in each plane.
4 orbital planes with 6 satellites in each plane.
6 orbital planes with 3 satellites in each plane plus 6 reserve satellites positioned in a geostationary orbital plane.

questions.questionText: For what reason is a mask angle set-up in a GPS-receiver?
options: To make the receiver select GPS-satellites with a certain optimum elevation which is about equal to the mask angle.
To deny the receiver the use of GPS-satellites with an elevation less than the mask angle.
To eliminate the reception of the signal from GPS-satellites with an elevation of about 90°.
To select from all visible GPS-satellites the satellite-configuration which results in the smallest GDOP

questions.questionText: What is the minimum number of satellites required for the NAVSTAR/GPS to carry out two dimensional operation?
What is the approximate orbital height of the GALILEO satellites circling the Earth?
In the NAVSTAR GPS satellite navigation system, receiver clock error:
options: is negligible small because of the great accuracy the atomic clocks installed in the satellites.
is corrected by using signals from four satellites.
is the biggest part of the total error; it cannot be corrected.
can be minimised by synchronisation of the receiver clock with the satellite clocks.

questions.questionText: The reason why the measured distance between a NAVSTAR GPS satellite navigation system satellite and a receiver is called a pseudo range is because the:
options: calculated range includes receiver clock error.
calculated range is based on an idealised Keplerian orbit.
measured distance is based on the pseudo random noise code.
movement of satellite and receiver during the distance calculation is not taken into account.

questions.questionText: Which of the following, if any, is a prerequisite if a receiver of a NAVSTAR GPS satellite navigation system is to be used in combination with a multi sensor system?
options: The RAIM function of the GPS receiver must be able to monitor all prescribed navigation systems.
The prescribed IFR equipment must be in working correctly and the navigation information continuously displayed.
The prescribed IFR equipment must be installed and operational.
Multisensor systems are not certificated for flights under IFR conditions.

questions.questionText: What is the inclination to the equatorial plane of the satellites orbit in the NAVSTAR GPS constellation?
In civil aviation, the height value computed by the receiver of the satellite navigation system NAVSTAR GPS is the:
What is the minimum number of NAVSTAR GPS satellites required to obtain an accurate 3D position fix?
What are the effects, if any, of shadowing by parts of the aircraft (e.g. wing) on the reception of signals from NAVSTAR GPS satellites?
options: It has no influence because high frequency signals are unaffected.
The signals will be distorted, however the error can be corrected for using an algorithm and information from unaffected signals.
It may prevent the reception of signals.

questions.questionText: In a Satellite-Assisted Navigation System (GNSS/GPS), a fix is obtained by:
options: the aircraft's receiver measuring the phase angle of signals received from a number of satellites in known positions.
measuring the time taken for a minimum number of satellites' transmissions, in known positions, to reach the aircraft's receiver.
measuring the time taken for an aircraft's transmissions to travel to a number of satellites, in known positions, and return to the aircraft's receiver.
measuring the pulse lengths of signals received from a minimum number of satellites received in a specific sequential order.

questions.questionText: Explain the principle of SBAS:
options: Measure in additional satellites the signal errors transmitted by GNSS satellites and transmit differential corrections and integrity messages for navigation satellites.
Measure on the ground the signal errors transmitted by GNSS satellites and transmit differential corrections and integrity messages for navigation satellites.
Measure in aircraft and additional satellites the signal errors transmitted by GNSS satellites and transmit differential corrections and integrity messages for navigation satellites.
Measure in aircraft the signal errors transmitted by GNSS satellites and transmit differential corrections and integrity messages for navigation satellites.

questions.questionText: Frequency band of the GBAS data link is:
options: VHF band of ILS and VOR systems.
a signal with the same frequency as GPS.

questions.questionText: An SBAS (Satellite Based Augmentation System) consists of:
Refer to GNSS. The ground based augmentation system (GBAS) sends corrections for:
options: multipath errors and satellite clock errors via VHF.
multipath errors and receiver clock errors.
errors due to ionospheric and tropospheric disturbances.
receiver clock errors and errors due to ionospheric disturbances.

questions.questionText: Which of the following statements about SBAS/GBAS is true?
options: SBAS can cover more area than GBAS.
GBAS does not need satellite signals, SBAS does.
SBAS does not need ground stations, GBAS does.
GBAS can cover more area than SBAS.

questions.questionText: EGNOS (European Geostationary Navigation Overlay System) is a form of:
In relation to the NAVSTAR GPS satellite navigation system, what is involved in the differential technique (D-GPS)?
options: The difference between signals transmitted on the L1 and L2 frequencies are processed by the receiver to determine an error correction.
Fixed ground stations compute position errors and transmit correction data to a suitable receiver on the aircraft.
Signals from satellites are received by 2 different antennas which are located a fixed distance apart. This enables a suitable receiver on the aircraft to recognise and correct for multipath errors.
Receivers from various manufacturers are operated in parallel to reduce the characteristical receiver noise error.

questions.questionText: Which statement is correct about the SBAS (Satellite-Based Augmentation Systems)?
options: In an SBAS the pseudo range corrections are sent to the users by geostationary satellites.
In an SBAS the pseudo range correction are sent to the users by the satellite of the satellite systems (e.g. by GPS satellites for GPS).
In an SBAS the pseudo range correction are determined by geostationary satellites and sent to the users by a network of ground stations.
In an SBAS the pseudo range corrections are determined by geostationary satellites and sent to the users by the satellite of the satellite systems (e.g. by GPS satellites for GPS).

questions.questionText: A GBAS signal is sent to:
options: a space vehicle (satellite) for clock synchronisation.
a ground station which is checking horizontal and vertical information.
a receiver which is located in the aircraft and modifies horizontal and vertical guidance corrections.
a space vehicle (satellite) for position corrections.

questions.questionText: An ABAS system is installed:
options: in the aircraft and on the ground.

questions.questionText: The "GBAS" positioning service provides:
options: horizontal position information for Initial segment.
horizontal position information to support RNAV operations in terminal areas.
deviation guidance for Final Approach segment.

questions.questionText: Which statement about a Satellite Based Augmentation System (SBAS) is correct?
options: SBAS is able to provide guidance for precision approaches.
SBAS is an augmentation device for Ground Based Augmentation Systems.
SBAS is able to provide approach and landing operations with horizontal guidance only.
SBAS is able to provide approach and landing operations with vertical guidance only.

questions.questionText: The Satellite-Based Augmentation Systems include:
options: GPS in the USA, EGNOS in Europe, JSTV in Japan, GAGAN in India
WAAS in the USA, EGNOS in Europe, MSAS in Japan, INDRA in India
WAAS in the USA, GALILEO in Europe, MSAS in Japan, GAGAN in India
WAAS in the USA, EGNOS in Europe, MSAS in Japan, GAGAN in India

questions.questionText: What is the advantage, if any, by using a combination of EGNOS and GPS?
options: Elimination of multi-path and noise errors.
Redundancy is increased but there is no improvement of the system itself.
Improving of the integrity by alerting users within 6 seconds instead of 3 hours.
Integrity is not improved but position accuracy is highly increased.

questions.questionText: If you compare SBAS and GBAS, what is an advantage of SBAS over GBAS?
options: SBAS is more accurate in local areas.
GBAS is less accurate in local areas.

questions.questionText: The meaning of the term "ABAS" is:

error correction data, integrity data and approach data for more than one runway.
error correction data and integrity data.
error correction data, integrity data and approach data for one runway.

questions.questionText: One type of Airborne Based Augmentation Systems (ABAS) is called Aircraft Autonomous Integrity Monitoring (AAIM). This system:
options: depends on information from GNSS only.
depends on a ground based augmentation system (GBAS) and thereby depends on the range of GBAS.
depends on additional onboard system and on information from GNSS.

questions.questionText: Which of the following statements about SBAS is correct?
options: SBAS satellites are navigation satellites which orbit around the Earth above the Greenwich meridian and anti-meridian and above the 90° and 270° meridian.
SBAS satellites are geostationary satellites which can also be used as navigation satellites with their own PNR code.
SBAS satellites are navigation satellites with the same inclination as other navigation satellites.

questions.questionText: The system capable of measuring on the ground, the signal errors transmitted by GNSS satellites and transmitting differential corrections and integrity messages for navigation satellites is:

options: SBAS is able to provide approach and landing operations with vertical guidance.
The SBAS monitor stations send corrected positions to the users within the range of the station.
SBAS is an augmentation device for Ground Based Augmentation Systems.

questions.questionText: Which of the following statements about the accuracy that can be obtained with the LAAS (local area augmentation system) of the satellite navigation system NAVSTAR GPS is correct?

The closer the receiver is to a LAAS ground reference station, the more accurate is the aircraft position fix.

questions.questionText: Frequency band of the SBAS data link is:
options: VHF band of ILS and VOR systems.
a signal with the same frequency as GNSS.

It allows the flight crew to specify a lateral offset from a defined route.

questions.questionText: What is correct about fly-by / fly-over waypoints and turns?
options: A fly-by waypoint is a waypoint which requires turn anticipation to allow tangential interception of the next segment of a route or procedure.
A fly-over waypoint is a waypoint which requires turn anticipation to allow tangential interception of the next segment of a route or procedure.
A turn via a fly-over waypoint is always shorter than a turn via a fly-by waypoint.
A fly-by waypoint is a waypoint at which a turn is initiated in order to join the next segment of a route or procedure.

questions.questionText: Concerning PBN - Performance Based Navigation - which of the following terms refers to fixed radius path for en-route sections?

options: A transmission of messages relating to enroute weather information which may affect the safety of aircraft operations that is not addressed to a specific station or stations.
A transmission where no reply is required from the receiving station.
A transmission of information relating to air navigation that is not addressed to a specific station or stations.
A transmission from one station to another station in circumstances where two-way communication cannot be established but it is believed that the called station is able to receive the transmission.

questions.questionText: Which of the following calls is a "GENERAL CALL"?

options: Communication is possible in both directions (but not simultaneously).
Simplified communications by means of standard words and phrases.
All communication is possible in only one direction.
Communication is possible in both directions (simultaneously).

questions.questionText: What does the term "AERONAUTICAL STATION" mean?
options: A station in the aeronautical telecommunication service located on land or on board of an aircraft to exchange radiotelephony communications.
Any station established to exchange radiotelephony communications.
An airborne station forming part of the aeronautical fixed telecommunication network (AFTN).
A station in the aeronautical mobile service located on land or, in certain instances, on board of a ship or on a platform at sea.

questions.questionText: What does the term "BROADCAST" mean?
options: A transmission containing meteorological and operational information to aircraft engaged in flights over remote and oceanic areas out of range of VHF ground stations.
A radiotelephony transmission from ground station to aircraft in flight.
A transmission of information relating to air navigation that is not addressed to a specific station or stations.
A transmission where no reply is required from the receiving station.

options: True heading to the station (no wind).

questions.questionText: What does QDR mean?
options: Magnetic heading to the station (no wind).

questions.questionText: What is the Q-code for True bearing from the station?

The priority of the pilot's message "REQUEST QDM" is:
options: less than "REQUEST CLIMB TO FLIGHT LEVEL 150".

questions.questionText: The message addressed to an Area Control Centre "REQUEST RADAR VECTORS TO CIRCUMNAVIGATE ADVERSE WEATHER" is:
options: a message relating to direction finding.

questions.questionText: A message concerning an aircraft being threatened by grave and imminent danger, requiring immediate assistance is called:
The clearance "CLEARED FOR IMMEDIATE TAKEOFF RUNWAY 03" is:
The message to an aeronautical ground station "PLEASE CALL A TAXI-CAB FOR US. WE WILL ARRIVE AT 1045 AS SCHEDULED" is:

options: flight regularity message, distress message, meteorological message.
flight safety message, meteorological message, flight regularity message.

questions.questionText: A message concerning aircraft parts and material urgently required is:
The priority of the instruction "TAXI TO RUNWAY 05" is:
options: greater than "CAUTION, CONSTRUCTION WORK LEFT OF TAXIWAY".

questions.questionText: Air Traffic Control messages (clearances, instructions, etc.) belong to the category of:
The order of priority of the following messages in the aeronautical mobile service is:
options: distress message, flight safety message, urgency message.
direction finding message, distress message, urgency message.
distress message, urgency message, direction finding message.
meteorological message, direction finding message, flight regularity message.

questions.questionText: The aeronautical mobile service handles message categories in a specific order. Which of the following descending order of priority is correct?
options: Flight safety, urgency, direction finding, meteorological
Urgency, flight safety, meteorological, direction finding
Urgency, flight safety, direction finding, meteorological
Urgency, direction finding, flight safety, meteorological

questions.questionText: Flight safety messages are:
options: messages concerning the safety of an aircraft, a vessel, any other vehicle or a person.

questions.questionText: When should words be spelled out in radiotelephony?
options: Whenever so requested by the responsible aeronautical reporting and service office.
Whenever the spelling of proper names, service abbreviations and words is doubtful.
If standard phraseology is used the meaning of all words is clear and there is no need to spell them out.
Words, names and abbreviations must be spelled out unless exempted to do so.

questions.questionText: What is the correct way of spelling out HB-JYC in a radio message?
What is the correct way of transmitting 1001 as a QNH?

options: the term DECIMAL can be omitted with friendly ATS units only.
the term DECIMAL must be spoken only if followed by three digits.
the term DECIMAL must always be transmitted.
the term DECIMAL can be omitted if no chance of misunderstanding exists.

questions.questionText: What is the correct way to transmit and read back frequency 120.375 MHz (VHF channel separated by 25 kHz)?
options: One two zero decimal three seven.
One two zero decimal three seven five.

questions.questionText: What would be the controllers answer if you ask for a time check at 23:31 UTC?
options: Time check two three three one.

questions.questionText: The time is 9:20 AM. What is the correct way of transmitting this time if there is no possibility of confusion about the hour?

options: No specific system, as only the minutes are normally required.

questions.questionText: In the event that a pilot is required to make a "BLIND" transmission, this should be made:

questions.questionText: Messages accepted for transmission should be transmitted:
options: without altering the sense of the message in any way.
in plain language or ICAO phraseologies
by using abbreviations with phonetic spelling and thereby expediting communication
by using corresponding subsidiary phraseologies in preference to ICAO phraseology whenever it is possible

questions.questionText: My message will be more effective and understandable if I:

questions.questionText: What are the consequences of an aircraft with a transmit button stuck on?

It improves readability for everyone else.
No one else can use the frequency.
It has no effect on other transmissions.

questions.questionText: What is meant by good microphone technique?
options: Keep the microphone far away since it improves the readability.
Use a normal conversation tone, speak clearly and distinctly.
Speak very loudly into the microphone.
Make large use of hesitation sounds as ER.

questions.questionText: Before transmitting the pilot should:
options: listen out on the frequency to ensure no interference with another station already transmitting will occur.
always write the message and read it during the transmission.
make sure that the aircraft is levelled off.
make sure that the emergency frequency is tuned in at the same time.

questions.questionText: What does the phrase "GO AHEAD" mean?

options: Leave the runway to the right.

questions.questionText: Which of these phrases is used to inform the control tower that a pilot is performing a missed approach?

questions.questionText: An aircraft is departing from Lille, call sign D-ENRW... The pilot receives: "D-RW, behind the landing Airbus, line up and wait runway 26 behind"
options: Behind Airbus, lining up and waiting runway 26 behind, D-RW.
Behind Airbus, lining up and waiting, D-RW.
Behind landing Airbus, lining up runway 26, D-RW.

questions.questionText: Which of these statements best describes the meaning of the phrase "STANDBY"?

Wait and I will call you.
Continue on present heading and listen out.

questions.questionText: Which word or phrase shall be used if you want to say "WAIT AND I WILL CALL YOU"?

I have received all of your last transmission.

questions.questionText: If a pilot receives an instruction from ATC which can not be carried out, the reply should use the phrase:

options: No reply necessary, ATC will call back

questions.questionText: Which phrase shall be used to confirm that a message has been repeated correctly?

options: As communication is difficult, I will call you later.
I have received all of your last transmission.
I understand your message and will comply with it.

questions.questionText: Aircraft X-BC has been instructed to listen on ATIS frequency 123.25... What is the correct response?

options: Hold position on the left side of the runway.

questions.questionText: An aircraft is located west of Friedrichshafen aerodrome. Which position report is correct?
options: Friedrichshafen Tower, Fastair 123, position 10 minutes west of Friedrichshafen, altitude 5 500 ft, next report overhead November.
Fastair 123, Friedrichshafen Tower, position 10 minutes west, 5 500 ft, next report November.
Friedrichshafen Tower, Fastair 123, overhead west, altitude 5 500 ft.

questions.questionText: What does the instruction "GO-AROUND" mean?

options: English only, ICAO phraseology or plain language
English, ICAO phraseology or plain language

questions.questionText: An aircraft is instructed to hold short of the runway-in-use. What is the correct phraseology?

options: Did you correctly receive this message?

Repeat all, or the specified part, of this message back to me exactly as received.

questions.questionText: What standard term are you going to use to indicate that you have understood the message and will carry out the instructions?

In the absence of a pilot action, the aircraft: 1) flies above the glide path. 2) flies below the glide path. 3) has an increasing true airspeed. 4) has a decreasing true airspeed. a vertical or horizontal wind velocity and/or wind direction variation over a large distance.
a horizontal wind velocity variation over a short distance.

Depart runway 27 with maximum throttle during the passage through the inversion.

The combination regrouping all the correct statements is:

from the upper side of the wing towards the underwing.

a quarter of the wingspan of the generating aircraft.
half the wingspan of the generating aircraft.
the wingspan of the generating aircraft.

a different flight path from the preceding aircraft, by remaining behind it and under its path.
a different flight path from the preceding aircraft, by remaining to the left of and under its path.

questions.questionText: How do wing tip vortices look if seen from behind?

These vortices are two counter-rotating cylindrical air masses trailing aft from the aircraft and rotate clockwise on the left side and rotate counter-clockwise on the right side.
These vortices are two co-rotating cylindrical air masses trailing aft from the aircraft.
These vortices are two counter-rotating cylindrical air masses trailing aft from the aircraft and rotate counter-clockwise on the left side and clockwise on the right side.

questions.questionText: When taking-off behind a heavy aircraft, with the wind coming from the left side, you adopt, whenever possible:
options: an identical flight path to the one of the preceding aircraft.
a different flight path from the preceding aircraft, by remaining behind it and under its path.
a different flight path from the preceding aircraft, by remaining to the right of and under its path.
a different flight path from the preceding aircraft, by remaining to the left of and above its path.
